architecture.title: DocketClock: The Reconciled Federal Comment-Deadline Substrate
architecture.one_liner: A federal-only, observation-log-backed reconciliation engine that exposes every public-comment deadline as a stable, provenance-bearing object with an honest confidence/conflict flag — sold as a B2B picks-and-shovels API/webhook to regulatory-affairs and law-firm KM desks, and architected so verticals like Watershed Watch rent it via a tag/enrichment contract without forking the core.
architecture.architecture_thesis: The unit of trust is a public-comment deadline whose net-new value is reconciliation + confidence + provenance, not discovery. The decisive design treats the append-only Observation log as the spine (trust-maximalist) and the ParticipationWindow as a derived, versioned projection — never a silently-mutated truth field — but it ruthlessly defers everything that does not move the first paying B2B contract (pragmatist). The substrate is made rentable by stable OCD-IDs plus a tag/enrichment contract that lets verticals scope without touching core reconciliation (substrate stance). Three critique-killing decisions are non-negotiable from day one: (1) conflict detection compares both source dates normalized to America/New_York before flagging — the known FR-2018-27875 one-calendar-day gap is a timezone artifact, NOT a conflict, so a naive >24h-in-UTC threshold would flood the CONFLICTING bucket and destroy the product's only differentiator; (2) when confidence drops to CONFLICTING/STALE we SUPPRESS the closing-soon alert AND simultaneously FIRE a conflict notification with both source rows attached — silence is a liability for deadline-liable buyers, so suppression is never silence; (3) a Week-1 frDocNum-join-hit-rate measurement is a hard go/no-go gate with a named contingency (pivot to Regs.gov-primary with docket_id/RIN fallback) before any pipeline code is written. The MVP is the reconciliation engine + the public contract; the UI is a thin internal review console, nothing more. Federal-only is a literal 'thou shalt not' until a paying customer funds a specific jurisdiction tranche.

canonical_object.name: ParticipationWindow (derived projection over an append-only Observation log)

key_fields.desc: Stable Open Civic Data ID: ocd-participation-window/federal/{frDocNum} (or regs:{regsObjectId} when FR doc number is absent). Generated once at first observation, never changes across extensions. This is the join key verticals (Watershed Watch) and AI agents use across systems — NOT an internal Postgres UUID.

All identifiers carried explicitly. frDocNum is the primary FR<->Regs.gov reconciliation key; docket_id array overlap and RIN are fallbacks per ACUS finding that agencies omit/misconfigure identifiers. regs_object_id (not documentId) is the comment-association key.
enum: comment | hearing | information_collection | eis_draft | eis_final | other. Typed so EIS-clock verticals can layer on without core changes.
The OPERATIVE deadline after reconciling the extension/correction/reopening chain — what consumers display. NULL is allowed and honest when confidence is CONFLICTING/UNKNOWN; never coerce a guess.

Verbatim legal-language string preserving timezone/channel semantics, e.g. '11:59 p.m. ET / 7:59 p.m. Alaska time' (NPS Alaska case). Separate from resolved_close_utc so collapsing to UTC never silently drops submission-channel meaning.

The unreconciled per-source parsed values, retained for transparency. FR comments_close_on is date-only; Regs.gov commentEndDate is ISO-8601 with offset. Both kept so the API can show the delta the engine reconciled.
enum: high | medium | low | conflicting | stale | unknown. First-class, always present in every response, never suppressed.

typed array: fr_regs_date_mismatch | tz_normalization_only | extension_chain_unresolved | correction_pending | withdrawn_vs_open | null_end_date_open_status | late_comment_ambiguous | multi_target_notice | keyword_false_positive. Array (not boolean) so consumers handle each failure mode distinctly.
enum: open | closed | extended | reopened | withdrawn | finalized | unknown.
submission_url to file; govinfo_url is the legal-reliance backstop link (govinfo.gov/content/pkg/FR-{date}/html/{frDocNum}.htm) embedded in EVERY response + the disclaimer anchor.

array populated by enrichment hooks (agency slug, CFR parts, geo_huc_8/12, keyword_topics), OPAQUE to core reconciliation. This is the extensibility seam: Watershed Watch writes huc_8: tags; DocketClock core never knows what a HUC is.

provenance summarizes which observations agreed/conflicted; current_observation_ids points into the immutable log; change_history is an append-only array of prior resolved_close_utc values with observation refs; version is a monotonic integer.

canonical_object.design_notes: ParticipationWindow is KEPT from Codex but DEMOTED from mutable source-of-truth to a derived view re-computed from the Observation log on every reconcile (trust-maximalist inversion). A single FR notice can extend multiple dockets with different new deadlines (EPA two-actions 2025-02910), so observations relate to windows MANY-TO-MANY via an observation_targets join table — one extension observation writes updates to N windows. resolved_close_utc separated from raw per-source values (substrate) and from resolved_close_display (timezone preservation) is the core data-modeling answer to three documented edge cases at once.

pipeline.what: Measure frDocNum join hit-rate and empirical FR-vs-Regs.gov conflict rate against the live ~1,000 open windows + Mirrulations history BEFORE writing pipeline code.
pipeline.detail: Hard go/no-go: if frDocNum joins <60% of records, the product pivots to Regs.gov-primary with docket_id-array-overlap + RIN fallback as the join, and FR-only records carry confidence=medium with conflict_reason='no_cross_source_join'. The conflict-rate number decides whether the 'conflicts this week' feed is a marquee feature or a quiet field. One DuckDB query over spicy-regs/Mirrulations Parquet produces a defensible estimate in hours.

pipeline.what: Poll FR API (keyless) every 15 min for documents with comments_close_on in the future OR action/title containing extension/correction/reopening/withdrawal keywords; poll Regs.gov v4 filter[withinCommentPeriod]=true with a lastModifiedDate change-cursor.
pipeline.detail: Regs.gov differential polling: fetch the withinCommentPeriod list (cheap), then fetch document detail only where lastModifiedDate advanced. CRITICAL: lastModifiedDate filter is Eastern while response timestamps are UTC — apply a 6-hour cursor-overlap and dedupe by documentId to avoid the documented silent-miss trap. Stay inside 1,000 req/hr on ONE key; apply to GSA for a rate increase in Week 1 (NOT multi-key, which is a ToS-revocation risk).

pipeline.what: SHA-256 hash every raw payload; append one Observation row (DB trigger rejects UPDATE/DELETE); skip if hash matches latest for that (source,document_id). Parse is_extension/is_correction/is_withdrawal flags and verbatim raw_dates_text at insert.
pipeline.detail: Append-only is enforced at the DB level, not by convention — the audit trail IS the trust primitive. Notice-type flags are set with regex BUT routed through a deny-list (RuleBox) to suppress the BLM 'land-withdrawal extension' keyword false-positive (2023-27468); genuinely ambiguous titles escalate to a single Haiku call, not the hot path.

pipeline.what: On new observations, load the full chain, run the deterministic JSON rulebook to produce resolved_close_utc, confidence, conflict_flags, status; write/append a ParticipationWindow version.
pipeline.detail: THE fix: compare FR comments_close_on and Regs.gov commentEndDate by normalizing BOTH to America/New_York calendar date. Same Eastern date => agree (flag tz_normalization_only if UTC strings differ). Differ by >0 Eastern days OR an unresolved extension/correction in the chain => CONFLICTING. commentEndDate null but openForComment=true => LOW. withdrawn vs open => CONFLICTING. Non-monotonic chains (a correction moving a date EARLIER) force re-evaluation, not blind 'latest wins'. LLM adjudicates only chain classification when the rulebook returns AMBIGUOUS. Regression-test every rulebook change against spicy-regs historical chains in CI.

pipeline.what: After reconciliation, pluggable enrichment workers write tags within a tenant's allowed_tag_namespaces WITHOUT touching the reconciled deadline.
pipeline.detail: Built-in: OCD agency-slug normalization, CFR-part extraction. Vertical (separate deployment calling the enrichment API): Watershed Watch resolves USGS WBD HUC-8/12 codes and writes huc_8:/huc_12: tags. This is the layer boundary enforced by data contract — the substrate never imports WBD. Built-in CFR/agency enrichment ships BEFORE design-partner onboarding so tag-based filtering isn't empty on day one.

pipeline.what: Expose windows via REST/RSS/ICS/CSV/webhooks; gate alert eligibility HERE, not in delivery.
pipeline.detail: Only HIGH/MEDIUM windows trigger closing-soon push. For CONFLICTING/STALE: SUPPRESS closing-soon AND simultaneously FIRE a 'conflict'/'needs-verification' notification carrying both source observations + govinfo_url — suppression is never silence. Document-level subscriptions: a docket_id watch fans out to ALL documents in that docket. Every response carries the disclaimer envelope and a request_id.

pipeline.what: After resolved_close_utc passes, re-poll 7 days for late withdrawal/correction/extension; write an AccuracyRecord (published vs actual close); emit window.finalized when a linked final rule appears.
pipeline.detail: AccuracyRecord feeds a track-record metric used in sales ('% of HIGH-confidence deadlines correct, trailing 90d'), but the public-facing accuracy dashboard is design-partner-gated during calibration and only goes fully public above a credibility threshold (e.g. 95% HIGH). Every was_correct=false becomes a labeled regression test.

components.name: Source Adapters
components.responsibility: Thin stateless fetchers, one per source, returning raw payload + metadata only (no parsing/normalization downstream). FR API (keyless), Regs.gov v4 (keyed, differential lastModifiedDate cursor), GovInfo (URL resolver only — no PDF ingestion), Mirrulations/spicy-regs (OFFLINE eval/seed only, never a live freshness source).
components.tech: TypeScript + Zod raw-payload validation at the boundary; node-postgres; cursor state in Postgres.
components.notes: No state/local adapters accepted by the registry until a funded source cluster + named paying customer exists.

components.name: Immutable Observation Log
components.responsibility: Append-only store of every raw observation with payload_hash, fetched_at, parser_version, verbatim raw_dates_text, notice-type flags. The audit spine — full replay can re-derive all windows and conflicts from observations alone.
components.tech: Postgres, table-level BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE trigger raising exception; JSONB raw payload; partition by fetched_at month; index (fr_document_number, source, fetched_at DESC) and (regs_object_id, source, fetched_at DESC).
components.notes: observation_targets join table gives the many-to-many needed for one-notice-extends-many-dockets (EPA 2025-02910).

components.name: Reconciliation / Confidence Engine
components.responsibility: Deterministic versioned JSON rulebook (RuleBox) reading the observation chain to produce resolved_close_utc, confidence, conflict_flags, status. Eastern-date comparison; non-monotonic chain handling; Haiku only for AMBIGUOUS chain classification.
components.tech: TypeScript; JSON rulebook + Zod rule schema; DuckDB-over-spicy-regs regression suite in CI; Anthropic Haiku fallback (<5% of records, ~50 calls/day at launch).
components.notes: Confidence-but-wrong guard: if a newly classified chain member jumps resolved_close_utc by >30 days, force PENDING_REVIEW instead of auto-HIGH.

components.name: Enrichment API + Hook Registry
components.responsibility: Post-reconcile hooks write tags within allowed_tag_namespaces; built-in (agency slug, CFR part) and tenant/vertical (HUC, keyword topics). Core never reads tags for reconciliation. No deadline overrides via this API — corrections route through the review console as a human_review observation to preserve the audit chain.
components.tech: Fastify sub-router; tenant-API-key auth; namespace middleware; Postgres JSONB tags + GIN index.

components.name: Registry + Delivery API
components.responsibility: Public REST (OpenAPI 3.1): GET /windows (filter agency/confidence/status/tags/closes_before-after), GET /windows/{ocd_id} (+observations), GET /conflicts (proof feed), GET /accuracy (gated), watchlist CRUD. Webhook fan-out (HMAC-signed, Postgres outbox, document-level docket fan-out, conflict-dual-fire, retry-then-visible-undelivered). RSS/ICS/CSV. MCP server (search_windows, get_window, list_observations) deferred but contract pre-shaped.
components.tech: Fastify; Zod->OpenAPI 3.1; Postgres outbox polled every 60s (NO Temporal/BullMQ-Redis at MVP — Postgres is the queue); HMAC-SHA256; ical.js; Postgres FTS (tsvector).
components.notes: Permanent webhook delivery failure (3 retries exhausted) -> delivered=false + GET /events visibility, so a missed push never becomes a silent miss.

components.name: Internal Review Console
components.responsibility: Thin internal-operator-only surface (NOT design-partner-facing) showing CONFLICTING/LOW/PENDING_REVIEW windows with side-by-side source values, DATES text, chain members, govinfo link. Reviewer resolves by INSERTING a human_review observation (never mutating the window directly); the reconciler re-derives.
components.tech: Retool-over-Postgres at MVP (30-min setup) OR a minimal Next.js /admin route with Clerk auth. A built console is a paid feature only when a customer asks to self-resolve.
components.notes: Dynamic staleness drives the queue: windows closing within 72h go STALE after 4h of no fresh observation; 30-day-out windows tolerate 48h.

data_sources.role: Discovery spine + legal-publication anchor. Fields: document_number, comments_close_on (date-only), dates (verbatim DATES text — legally authoritative language), comment_url, docket_ids, regulations_dot_gov_info, regulation_id_number_info (RIN), correction_of/corrections, action, title.
data_sources.gotchas: Unofficial XML rendition — NOT legal notice (attach govinfo_url). comments_close_on is date-only with NO timezone; the FR-2018-27875 one-day gap vs Regs.gov is a normalization artifact, not a conflict. correction_of/corrections unreliable (2024-03267 null despite being a correction) — also parse action/title. Extension notices are SEPARATE documents that do NOT update the original's date; the extension doc itself may have commentEndDate=null/openForComment=false (2025-03547). Field semantics under-documented (GitHub issue #9).

data_sources.role: Operational status authority. Fields: commentEndDate (ISO-8601+offset, preferred for resolved_close_utc), openForComment, withinCommentPeriod, allowLateComments, withdrawn, frDocNum (primary join key), objectId (comment-association key), docketId, lastModifiedDate (change cursor).
data_sources.gotchas: 1,000 req/hr GET key; commenting sub-API 50/min & 500/hr (irrelevant — read-only MVP, but don't conflate). Pagination hard cap 20 pages x 250 = 5,000/query; lastModifiedDate paging is GSA-beta and may be removed — file for bulk/limit increase early. lastModifiedDate filter is Eastern, responses UTC (6h cursor-overlap + dedupe). objectId != documentId for comments. commentEndDate can be null while openForComment=true. Agency-configurable submitter fields appear/disappear.

data_sources.role: Legal-reliance backstop link only. Embed govinfo_url in every window/response/alert as the disclaimer anchor. NO PDF/text ingestion in MVP.
data_sources.gotchas: Public URL pattern (content/pkg/FR-{date}/html/{frDocNum}.htm) needs no API call. Flag for review if URL resolution fails (rare).

data_sources.source: Mirrulations S3 + mirrulations-search Postgres / spicy-regs Parquet+DuckDB
data_sources.role: OFFLINE eval + bootstrap ONLY. Seed historical observations, build the labeled conflict/chain regression suite, run rulebook regression tests in CI, mine extension/correction/withdrawal edge cases. Path to a future MCP/DuckDB SQL surface for AI-agent buyers.
data_sources.gotchas: Does NOT reduce ongoing freshness cost — never a live feed. Noisy (ACUS/GAO data-quality findings) — treat as eval set, not clean training set. Static snapshot; refresh periodically.

data_sources.source: USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD) via The National Map ArcGIS REST
data_sources.role: VERTICAL-ONLY (Watershed Watch enrichment), NOT a DocketClock core source. The Watershed Watch enrichment worker resolves HUC-8/12 and writes tags via the enrichment API.
data_sources.gotchas: Substrate stays agnostic — core never imports/queries WBD. Geo recall (whether a docket mentions a basin) is Watershed Watch's problem, not the substrate's; document this boundary explicitly.

states.meaning: FR and Regs.gov agree on the close date when BOTH normalized to America/New_York; openForComment consistent; no unresolved extension/correction/withdrawal in the chain; freshest observation within the dynamic-freshness window for this window's time-to-close.
states.alert_behavior: Eligible for closing-soon push and all delivery.

states.meaning: One source present with no contradiction, OR sources agree but one observation is moderately stale, OR allowLateComments=true creates a formal-close-vs-practical-close ambiguity.
states.alert_behavior: Eligible for push; allowLateComments ambiguity surfaced in payload.

states.meaning: commentEndDate null but openForComment=true; or only date-only FR value with no timezone resolution; or chain plausible but unverified.
states.alert_behavior: Appears in API; not pushed as a confident deadline; surfaced in review console.

states.meaning: FR vs Regs.gov differ by >=1 Eastern calendar day; OR withdrawn=true conflicts with openForComment=true; OR an unresolved extension/correction chain. The marquee differentiator — the one case existing single-source tools give NO signal for. A 1-UTC-day gap that is the SAME Eastern date is flagged tz_normalization_only at MEDIUM, never CONFLICTING.
states.alert_behavior: Closing-soon SUPPRESSED AND a conflict/needs-verification notification SIMULTANEOUSLY FIRED with both source observations + govinfo_url. Published to the GET /conflicts proof feed within 15 min.

states.meaning: No fresh observation within the dynamic threshold: 4h for windows closing within 72h, scaling to 48h for windows 30+ days out.
states.alert_behavior: Push suppressed unless subscriber explicitly opts into low-confidence; flagged in console.

states.meaning: Both structured deadline fields missing; resolved_close_utc = NULL.
states.alert_behavior: Published honestly; never pushed; never coerced to a guessed date.

confidence_model.how_computed: A deterministic, VERSIONED RuleBox rulebook runs in the reconcile stage; every confidence assignment carries a structured explanation (rules_fired, source_agreement, parse_path, staleness, conflict_ids) returned in the API. Confidence is NEVER ML-scored and NEVER LLM-scored — the LLM only classifies AMBIGUOUS extension/correction chains. The cardinal rule: a bad deadline is worse than no alert, so confidence drops suppress confident pushes — but suppression always pairs with an honest conflict/needs-verification signal, never silence. Operational HIGH is defined as measurable parameters (Eastern-date source agreement, freshness SLA by time-to-close, empty extension-chain lookback), not a marketing label, because buyers carry deadline liability.

delivery_surfaces.detail: GET /windows (filter agency/docket/confidence/status/tags/closes_before-after, paginated), GET /windows/{ocd_id} (full provenance + observations), GET /conflicts (proof feed), GET /accuracy (design-partner-gated during calibration). Zod-generated spec published at /openapi.json so buyers inspect before purchase. Every response carries disclaimer + api_version + request_id.

Per-watchlist, document-level fan-out (a docket_id watch hits all its documents). notify_on: open | closing_soon | conflict | changed | closed | needs_verification. Conflict-dual-fire enforced. 3 retries + exponential backoff, then delivered=false surfaced via GET /events so no push silently vanishes.

RSS per agency/watchlist + a first-class /conflicts Atom feed (the public honesty signal). ICS for calendar embed. CSV/JSONL bulk snapshots. RSS/ICS/CSV are v1.1 (after REST + webhooks validated by design partners) to hold MVP scope.

search_windows / get_window / list_observations / subscribe_watchlist, with confidence + conflict_flags mandatory in every structured response and a tool description instructing agents to surface them before acting. Built only when an AI-agent buyer commits — the three named design partners are NOT MCP buyers, so shipping it in the core MVP sprint is rejected.
Operator-only (Retool/Next.js). Design partners get API + webhooks, never console access; their corrections become support tickets -> human_review observations.

tech_stack.why: Zod schema-first gives runtime validation + OpenAPI in one pass; B2B buyers' first ask is the API spec. REST sources + join/rule logic suit TS; type system catches frDocNum-vs-fr_doc_num field-casing bugs across sources.

The whole infra: append-only observation log (DB trigger), canonical store, queue (outbox), FTS, pg_trgm fuzzy fallback. ~1,000 open windows is trivially within Postgres FTS. If on Supabase, lock down roles/Studio to protect the immutable observations table.

tech_stack.choice: Postgres outbox + pg_cron/Node cron at MVP
tech_stack.why: At ~1,000 windows + ~100 subscriptions the outbox never exceeds a few thousand rows. Temporal and BullMQ-on-Redis are both REJECTED for MVP as weeks of ops for zero buyer-visible feature; revisit BullMQ/Temporal only when durable long-running chain workflows or 10k+ subscriptions demand it.

Fastest Zod-schema -> running OpenAPI 3.1 endpoint; schema-based serialization keeps published spec and actual responses from diverging — critical when confidence/provenance are contract, not optional.
Deterministic, versioned, explainable; regression-tested in CI against spicy-regs. LLM reserved for AMBIGUOUS chain classification + deny-list misses only (<$1/day).
No infra; runs in CI; the pre-architecture conflict-rate + join-hit-rate measurement and every rulebook regression test live here.
Internal founder tool, not a built system at MVP; the human-review console in its correct v1 form.

tech_stack.choice: Render or Fly.io (API + workers), managed Postgres
tech_stack.why: Zero-ops containers: web API + background poller + outbox worker from one repo. No Kubernetes/Lambda. ~$25-50/mo covers the design-partner phase.
Chain-classification + extension-keyword disambiguation only, structured JSON, ~50 calls/day at launch. Never in the deadline-resolution or confidence-scoring hot path.

in_scope: Federal-only: all Regs.gov documents with openForComment=true or commentEndDate within the next 90 days, plus FR extension/correction/withdrawal notices
Append-only Observation log (DB-trigger-enforced) + FR and Regs.gov v4 adapters with Eastern-aware differential polling

Reconciliation rulebook covering the documented patterns: Eastern-date mismatch, extension chain, null-end-date-open-status, withdrawn-vs-open, multi-target notice, non-monotonic correction; regression-tested against spicy-regs
ParticipationWindow as a derived versioned projection with stable OCD-IDs, resolved_close_utc + resolved_close_display, typed conflict_flags
REST API + OpenAPI 3.1 spec + API-key auth (the design-partner deliverable)
Webhooks (HMAC, Postgres outbox, document-level fan-out, conflict-dual-fire, undelivered visibility)
GET /conflicts proof feed; AccuracyRecord verification job (dashboard design-partner-gated)
Built-in agency-slug + CFR-part enrichment writing tags (ships before onboarding so tag filtering isn't empty)
Internal-only review console (Retool); govinfo_url + disclaimer on every record
Week-1 frDocNum hit-rate + conflict-rate measurement as a go/no-go gate; Week-1 GSA rate-increase application
deferred: RSS/ICS/CSV bulk delivery (v1.1, after REST+webhooks validated)
MCP server (until a named AI-agent buyer commits)
Enrichment API for THIRD-PARTY/vertical tag namespaces (built-in enrichment ships; the open tenant-writable hook registry follows when Watershed Watch commits)
Watershed Watch geo-enrichment worker, USGS WBD, Open States v3, EIS clock (vertical builds ON TOP)
OpenSearch (hard-deferred — Postgres FTS handles <10k windows indefinitely)
Temporal / BullMQ-Redis (Postgres outbox suffices)
GovInfo full-text PDF ingestion (link-only)
Email digests (webhook -> Zapier handles it)
State/local sources (literal thou-shalt-not until a funded jurisdiction tranche)
Pol.is consensus, comment submission/action layer, final-rule causal impact (vertical action layer, not substrate)
SOC 2 / enterprise procurement (Year-2, after first 3 paying contracts)
mvp_boundary.first_customer: Two boutique/mid law-firm current-awareness (KM) teams + one life-sciences regulatory-affairs team where the buyer is also the technical evaluator (credit-card MSA, no 6-month IT procurement). Design-partner rate $500-$2k/mo WITH an explicit escalation clause to standard pricing ($10k-$50k/yr range the comps justify: RegAlytics $1.8k/mo single seat, AgencyIQ $25k-$75k/yr) after 90 days so the pilot price is not a permanent anchor. Success metric: design partners find >=3 real extension/correction/withdrawal conflicts existing single-source tools missed. Watershed Watch (Chesapeake via a Waterkeeper/CBF chapter) is a FAST-FOLLOW vertical pilot leveraging the user's network access — pursued only when a chapter commits to a pilot contract, never before the substrate has its first B2B customer.

kept.item: ParticipationWindow as the canonical unit of trust (name + concept)
kept.why: The deadline-as-first-class-object-with-provenance/confidence/change-history IS the product. Correct and retained verbatim in name.

kept.item: Immutable event/observation log with payload hash, fetched_at, source URL, parser version, extracted candidates
kept.why: Exactly right — the audit spine that makes deadline changes legally defensible. We strengthen it (DB-trigger-enforced append-only) rather than weaken it.

kept.item: Trust model: unknown/stale/conflicting first-class; suppress/downgrade alerts when confidence drops; a bad deadline is worse than no alert
kept.why: This is the differentiator against every single-source tracker. Non-negotiable.

kept.item: RuleBox pattern: deterministic rules run cheap forever, LLM only adjudicates ambiguous extension chains
kept.why: Correct cost structure; applied to chain classification + the BLM-style keyword deny-list.

kept.item: Confidence engine producing HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/STALE/CONFLICTING from source agreement, parse certainty, freshness, status semantics
kept.why: Right primitive; we add UNKNOWN and a structured explanation object and pin the Eastern-date comparison.

kept.item: Human review console scoped ONLY to uncertain/high-impact records
kept.why: Correct scope; we make it internal-operator-only and resolve via human_review observations to preserve the audit chain.

kept.item: Registry + API emitting webhooks/RSS/ICS/CSV/bulk; OCD Events for downstream joinability
kept.why: Correct delivery surface and the substrate joinability promise (OCD-IDs as the cross-system key for Watershed Watch).

kept.item: Federal-first expansion rule: no state/local until a funded source cluster + paying customer
kept.why: State/local has no normalized cross-state rulemaking API (Legistar has no comment-deadline field). Enforced as a literal thou-shalt-not in the adapter registry.

kept.item: Suggested stack: TypeScript workers/API, Postgres (JSONB raw + versions), Zod/JSON Schema + public OpenAPI, Postgres FTS first
kept.why: All correct; we firm 'FTS first' into a hard deferral of OpenSearch and concretize Fastify.

changed.item: Canonical window's relationship to the event log
changed.from: Mutable canonical window is primary; event log is audit trail
changed.to: Observation log is PRIMARY/append-only; ParticipationWindow is a derived, versioned projection re-computed on every reconcile
changed.why: Mutating the window silently discards prior deadline values — exactly what 'do not publish fake certainty' forbids. Inversion enforces the trust invariant structurally (trust-maximalist).

changed.from: Monolithic entity-resolution graph tying notices/dockets/agencies/CFR-parts/comment-URLs/corrections/reopenings into one canonical graph
changed.to: Three smaller pieces: observation-insert notice-type flags + Reconciliation Engine (deadline resolution) + Enrichment hooks (CFR/agency/HUC tags)
changed.why: The monolith is premature abstraction that blocks vertical extension. Decomposition is what makes the substrate rentable without forking core (substrate).
Both are weeks of ops for zero buyer-visible feature at ~1,000 windows / ~100 subscriptions. Postgres is the queue until durable workflows or 10k+ subscriptions justify BullMQ/Temporal (pragmatist).

changed.to: Postgres FTS, OpenSearch HARD-deferred until a measured bottleneck
changed.why: ~1,000 open windows is trivially within FTS; OpenSearch is real ops burden with no MVP payoff.

changed.to: resolved_close_utc (nullable) + separate verbatim resolved_close_display; conflict comparison normalized to America/New_York
changed.why: The NPS Alaska '11:59 ET / 7:59 AK' case is legally distinct from any single UTC stamp; the FR-2018-27875 1-UTC-day gap is an Eastern-same-date artifact, NOT a conflict. Eastern comparison prevents flooding CONFLICTING with false positives — the single fatal-flaw fix.

changed.from: Internal review trigger / one silent truth field
changed.to: ConflictRecord as a first-class PUBLISHED proof feed (GET /conflicts, Atom) with conflict-dual-fire on alerts
changed.why: Publishing conflicts is the credibility moat no competitor offers; dual-fire ensures suppression is never silence for deadline-liable buyers.

changed.item: Admin + design-partner watchlist UI bundled with the registry
changed.from: One small admin + design-partner UI component
changed.to: Internal-operator review console SEPARATE from the public registry/API; design partners get API+webhooks only
changed.why: Mixing creates a surface where an admin action mutates canonical data and lets partners approve resolutions — a trust liability.

rejected.item: Naming downstream consumer personas (pharmacy desks, childcare associations, journalists, advocacy orgs) inside the substrate architecture
rejected.why: Bleeds vertical concerns into core and pressures pharmacy/childcare-specific fields onto the canonical object. The funded B2B buyers are reg-affairs + law-firm KM (liability-bearing); consumer/civic personas are a later distribution layer handled via the tag/enrichment contract, not core fields.
Product context explicitly flags this as wrong — Mirrulations always lags live Regs.gov; it is an offline eval/seed asset only, never a live pipeline.

rejected.item: Multi-API-key pool to beat the Regs.gov 1,000 req/hr limit
rejected.why: Splitting one use-case across keys is rate-limit circumvention that risks ToS revocation — existential. Correct path is a GSA rate-increase application (granted case-by-case) plus differential polling within budget.

rejected.item: Shipping MCP in the core MVP sprint
rejected.why: The three named design partners are not AI-agent buyers; MCP is operational surface area before its buyer segment is validated. Contract pre-shaped, build deferred to a committed AI-agent buyer.

rejected.item: Email digest delivery as a built v1 feature
rejected.why: Webhook -> Zapier/Make handles email free; building templates/unsubscribe/SPF/DKIM/bounce is a week for nothing a design partner needs.

build_sequence.deliverable: DuckDB measurement over live APIs + Mirrulations/spicy-regs of (a) frDocNum join hit-rate and (b) empirical FR-vs-Regs.gov Eastern-date conflict rate, written up as the first sales-evidence 'Conflict Rate Report'. GO/NO-GO decision on join strategy. GSA rate-increase + bulk-access application filed.

Postgres schema (Observation append-only + trigger, ParticipationWindow, observation_targets M:N, Watchlist, Tenant, outbox). FR + Regs.gov adapters with Eastern-aware differential polling + 6h cursor overlap + dedupe. Payload-hash idempotent observation ingestion. OCD-ID generation validated as stable.

JSON RuleBox covering all documented patterns with Eastern-date comparison, non-monotonic chains, deny-list keyword guard, >30-day-jump PENDING_REVIEW guard. CI regression suite over spicy-regs chains. Confidence + typed conflict_flags + resolved_close_utc/display + explanation object. Haiku chain-adjudication fallback.

Fastify REST API (GET /windows, /windows/{ocd_id}+observations, /conflicts) + Zod-generated OpenAPI 3.1 at /openapi.json + API-key auth + per-tenant rate limiting. HMAC webhook outbox worker with document-level fan-out, conflict-dual-fire, undelivered visibility. govinfo_url + disclaimer on every record. Built-in agency/CFR enrichment writing tags.

Internal Retool review console (CONFLICTING/LOW/PENDING_REVIEW, side-by-side, human_review-observation resolution, dynamic staleness queue). Onboard 2 law-firm KM teams + 1 reg-affairs team on the live API/webhooks with the OpenAPI spec and a demo watchlist; collect conflict-copy and payload-shape feedback.

Post-close verification worker + AccuracyRecord; window.finalized linkage; dynamic STALE detection + low-confidence suppression with dual-fire validated end-to-end. Design-partner-gated accuracy dashboard. Every was_correct=false becomes a regression test.

First paid contract signed with escalation clause. RSS/ICS/CSV delivery (v1.1). Iterate on real workflow needs (Slack-via-Zapier, calendar embed, read-only Postgres). Tenant-writable enrichment hook registry stubbed for the Watershed Watch fast-follow if a Chesapeake chapter commits.

top_risks.risk: FR-vs-Regs.gov reconciliation flags routine timezone-normalization 1-day gaps as CONFLICTING, flooding the bucket and destroying the only differentiator (the fatal flaw across two proposals).
top_risks.mitigation: Compare both dates normalized to America/New_York calendar date, not UTC. Same Eastern date => agree (tz_normalization_only at MEDIUM if UTC strings differ); only a true Eastern-day difference or unresolved chain => CONFLICTING. Validated against FR-2018-27875 in the Week-1 report and the CI regression suite.

top_risks.risk: frDocNum join fails for a large fraction of records (ACUS: agencies omit/misconfigure identifiers), collapsing the reconciliation value prop and leaving a dark zone with no cross-source signal.
top_risks.mitigation: Week-1 hit-rate measurement is a hard go/no-go gate. Fallback join on docket_id array overlap (Postgres &&) + RIN; unjoined records carry confidence=medium, conflict_reason='no_cross_source_join', never dropped. Below 60% => documented pivot to Regs.gov-primary; pg_trgm title fuzzy-match as last resort. Track join-failure as a daily metric.

top_risks.risk: Confidence drop silently suppresses a closing-soon alert for a deadline-liable buyer who then misses the window — silence is worse than a wrong alert.
top_risks.mitigation: Conflict-dual-fire: suppression NEVER means silence. CONFLICTING/STALE suppress closing-soon AND simultaneously fire a needs-verification notification with both source observations + govinfo_url. Permanent webhook failure surfaces via GET /events. Every payload disclaims legal authority and links the authoritative govinfo source.

top_risks.risk: One FR notice extends multiple dockets with different deadlines (EPA 2025-02910) or a chain is non-monotonic (correction moving a date earlier); a 1:1 or 'latest-wins' model silently leaves the second window stale.
top_risks.mitigation: observation_targets M:N join writes N window updates from one notice; the reconciler re-evaluates the whole chain on each new observation rather than blind latest-wins; a >30-day resolved-date jump forces PENDING_REVIEW. All three patterns are CI regression tests mined from spicy-regs.

top_risks.risk: Regs.gov rate limit / lastModifiedDate-beta pagination is hit or removed; or a multi-key workaround triggers ToS revocation — existential loss of the operational status source.
top_risks.mitigation: Differential polling (list cheaply, fetch detail only on lastModifiedDate change) stays well inside 1,000 req/hr at ~1,000 windows. File a GSA rate-increase + bulk-access request in Week 1 (NOT multi-key). Eastern/UTC cursor handled with a 6h overlap + documentId dedupe to prevent silent misses.

top_risks.risk: Watershed Watch is dismissed as 'just DocketClock with a Waterkeeper skin' and gold-plates the MVP, or the substrate leaks vertical (HUC) concerns into core.
top_risks.mitigation: Hard layer boundary: core is HUC-agnostic; verticals scope via stable OCD-IDs + the tag/enrichment contract (separate deployment calling the enrichment API). Watershed Watch is a funded FAST-FOLLOW pursued only on a committed Chesapeake/Waterkeeper pilot, leveraging the user's real design-partner network — never before the first B2B contract, and it earns distribution by adding geo-recall + plain-language briefs the bare registry can't, not by re-skinning it.

open_questions: What is the empirical Eastern-date conflict rate across the live ~1,000 open windows? It decides whether GET /conflicts is a marquee feature or a quiet field, and whether the product story is 'alerts' or 'conflict intelligence'. Must be answered in Week 1 before pipeline code.
Does frDocNum join at >60% hit-rate on the live corpus, or is docket_id/RIN the primary key in practice? Determines the entire reconciliation join strategy (Week-1 go/no-go).

What is the daily volume of genuine extension/correction/reopening notices (vs deny-list false positives)? Determines whether the review console is a 20-min/day task or a part-time staffing line, and the real maintenance burden of the keyword classifier.
Do the first design partners need per-agency notification SLAs (e.g. 'FDA change within X hours') or is the polling cadence acceptable as-is? Determines whether near-close polling must tighten.

Will a Chesapeake-region Waterkeeper/CBF chapter commit to a paid Watershed Watch pilot, given the user's network access — and is that pilot viable before or only after the first B2B substrate contract? Gates whether the enrichment hook registry + WBD geo-enrichment is a fast-follow or stays fully deferred.

Does GSA grant the rate-increase/bulk-access request, and on what timeline? If lastModifiedDate beta pagination is removed before a permanent bulk solution ships, the historical-backfill and chain-evidence paths need a fallback.
divergences_from_prior_dossier: Prior dossier framed DocketClock primarily as a reconciliation/confidence engine; this architecture makes the append-only Observation log the PRIMARY data model and the ParticipationWindow a derived projection — a structural commitment the dossier implied but did not pin.

The dossier left OCD-IDs as a 'normalize downstream' nicety; here they are the PRIMARY public key and the explicit substrate-rental seam (verticals join on OCD-IDs, never internal UUIDs), with a concrete federal namespace scheme.

The dossier did not specify the timezone-comparison rule; this design makes Eastern-date normalization (not UTC) the load-bearing fix that keeps CONFLICTING meaningful, and separates resolved_close_utc from a verbatim resolved_close_display.

The dossier's confidence model listed states but not behavior; here suppression is explicitly paired with conflict-dual-fire so a confidence drop never produces silence — a sharper, liability-aware reading of 'a bad deadline is worse than no alert'.

The dossier suggested Temporal/BullMQ as the queue; this architecture rejects both for MVP in favor of a Postgres outbox, and hard-defers OpenSearch — a more ruthless MVP than the dossier's stack implied.

The dossier treated the design-partner watchlist UI as part of the registry; here the internal review console is strictly separated from the public surface, and corrections flow as human_review observations to preserve the audit chain.

The dossier named broad downstream consumer personas at the substrate layer; this design explicitly removes them from core and routes all vertical/persona scoping through the tag/enrichment contract, keeping the canonical object persona-agnostic.

Added net-new objects the dossier did not enumerate: ConflictRecord (published proof feed), AccuracyRecord (track-record), and the observation_targets M:N join for one-notice-extends-many-dockets — each answering a documented edge case (EPA 2025-02910, BLM 2023-27468, FR 2025-03547).

proposal.stance: SHIP-FAST PRAGMATIST — optimize for a signed design-partner contract within 90 days, not a production-grade platform within 18 months. Every architectural choice is evaluated by a single question: does this help get the first paying B2B customer, or does it serve a hypothetical future scale that does not exist yet?
proposal.thesis: The Codex architecture is directionally correct but over-engineered for v1. The ParticipationWindow object and the core reconciliation insight are worth keeping exactly. Everything else — the immutable event log, OpenSearch, human-review console, Temporal queue, full normalizer graph — should be deferred until a design partner's actual workflow forces them. The fastest credible path to a paying regulatory-affairs team or law-firm KM desk is: (1) a cron job polling FR API + Regulations.gov v4 on a 4-hour cycle, (2) a Postgres table that stores the resolved deadline alongside the raw source rows and a 5-rule heuristic conflict flag, (3) a REST endpoint + webhook that emits the resolved record with provenance JSON, (4) a Retool or basic Next.js admin panel so the founder can manually review flagged records during the design-partner phase. That is it. The unit of trust — a deadline with a confidence flag and source evidence — is deliverable in 3–4 weeks of solo engineering. The immutable event log, full normalizer, OpenSearch, and human-review console can be added incrementally once a customer is paying and telling you which failure modes actually matter.

canonical_model.key_fields: fr_doc_number (PK natural key), docket_id, agency_id, title, resolved_close_date (timestamptz), resolved_close_date_source (enum: regs_gov | fr_api | fr_text | manual), fr_close_date (date), regs_gov_close_datetime (timestamptz), open_for_comment (bool), allow_late_comments (bool), withdrawn (bool), conflict_flag (bool), confidence (enum: high | medium | low | conflicting | stale), conflict_reason (text), submission_url, govinfo_url, fetched_at (timestamptz), last_changed_at (timestamptz), raw_fr_payload (jsonb), raw_regs_gov_payload (jsonb)
canonical_model.why: Codex's ParticipationWindow is kept but renamed and flattened. Flatten first — JSONB columns preserve the full source payload without a separate observation table, so every field is auditable without an immutable event log. No graph, no OCD-ID normalization in v1 — those are correct long-term but add zero value for the first B2B customer who just needs a reliable deadline + conflict flag + source link. 'resolved_close_date_source' is the v1 provenance primitive — it tells the buyer whether the operative date came from Regulations.gov commentEndDate (preferred: it carries timezone and status semantics), FR comments_close_on (secondary: date-only, timezone-ambiguous), or parsed DATES text (fallback). 'conflict_flag' fires on the 5 heuristics described in the pipeline. 'raw_fr_payload' and 'raw_regs_gov_payload' (jsonb) serve as the v1 immutable log — they preserve what was fetched without a separate append-only table.

canonical_model.key_fields: id, modifier_fr_doc_number (FK to DeadlineRecord), target_fr_doc_number (FK to DeadlineRecord), link_type (enum: extension | correction | reopening | withdrawal), new_close_date (date), detected_at (timestamptz), detection_method (enum: docket_join | title_keyword | manual)
canonical_model.why: The mismatch-evidence research shows extension/reopening/correction chains are the primary source of stale deadlines (EPA NPDES had original + extension + reopening; PHMSA had 90-day extension; HUD had an outage-forced extension). A separate junction table captures these without mutating the original DeadlineRecord. Codex called this the 'normalizer' — the v1 version is just this table plus the 5-rule conflict-flag logic. The full normalizer graph is deferred.

canonical_model.key_fields: id, customer_id, filter_type (enum: agency | docket_id | keyword | fr_doc_number), filter_value, webhook_url, email, last_notified_at, notify_on (enum: open | closing_soon | conflict | changed | closed)
canonical_model.why: This is the monetization primitive. Without it there is no product — just a database. Design partners will not pay for a queryable DB; they pay for push delivery to their existing workflows. Codex deferred webhooks to the 'Registry + API' component but this is v1 day-one. Keep it minimal: one webhook URL per subscription, HMAC-signed POST, exponential backoff on failure, no queue manager — use pg_cron or a simple Node cron for v1.

pipeline_stages.what: Poll FR API for documents with comments_close_on in next 90 days (filter: type=proposed_rules,notices; sort=comments_close_on; per_page=100). Separately poll Regulations.gov v4 /documents with filter[withinCommentPeriod]=true and sort by lastModifiedDate. Run every 4 hours via pg_cron triggering a Node worker. No queue manager — a simple setInterval in a long-running process or a Render cron job is sufficient for ~1,000 open dockets.
pipeline_stages.tech: Node.js fetch + Postgres upsert; FR API (keyless); Regulations.gov v4 API (1,000 req/hr key). Rate-limit: fetch Regulations.gov in batches of 20 documents per request (max page[size]=250 but 20 is safe for the 500/hr commenting API sublimit).

pipeline_stages.what: For each FR document fetched, attempt a join to a Regulations.gov document via frDocNum = fr_doc_number. Five-rule heuristic (all deterministic, no LLM): (R1) FR comments_close_on date != Regs.gov commentEndDate date — emit conflict_flag=true, confidence=conflicting; (R2) Regs.gov openForComment=false but FR comments_close_on is in the future — confidence=low; (R3) Regs.gov commentEndDate is null but openForComment=true — confidence=medium; (R4) fetched_at > resolved_close_date + 48 hours and status not updated — confidence=stale; (R5) title/action contains 'extension', 'correction', 'reopening', 'withdrawal' (case-insensitive) AND not a keyword false-positive from BLM-style land-withdrawal — confidence=low, create ExtensionLink candidate. R5 uses a 12-word deny-list (land withdrawal, permit term, etc.) to reduce false positives from the BLM Utah example in the mismatch-evidence research. This is the RuleBox pattern: deterministic rules generated once, run cheaply forever.
pipeline_stages.tech: SQL join + Node rules engine; no LLM in v1 hot path. LLM (Claude Haiku via Anthropic API) is called only for R5 ambiguous cases where the deny-list does not resolve — estimated <5% of extension-title matches. Total LLM calls: likely <50/day at launch.

pipeline_stages.what: Write resolved_close_date and resolved_close_date_source per precedence order: (1) Regs.gov commentEndDate if openForComment=true and no conflict — source=regs_gov, confidence=high; (2) FR comments_close_on if Regs.gov has no record — source=fr_api, confidence=medium; (3) If conflict on dates, keep Regs.gov commentEndDate as operative but set conflict_flag=true — source=regs_gov, confidence=conflicting. Timezone: always store timestamptz. Regs.gov commentEndDate is ISO 8601 with offset (typically UTC midnight). FR comments_close_on is date-only — store as date::date with a note that end-of-day Eastern is the implied semantics per the FR-2018-27875 evidence. Never silently coerce FR date-only to UTC midnight.
pipeline_stages.tech: Postgres upsert with RETURNING; trigger last_changed_at update on any field change.

pipeline_stages.what: Three v1 delivery channels in priority order: (a) REST API — GET /deadlines?agency=&status=open&closing_within_days=14&confidence=high,medium — returns paginated DeadlineRecord + conflict metadata + govinfo_url. API key auth (simple UUID in header, stored in Postgres). (b) Webhooks — on INSERT or UPDATE to DeadlineRecord where conflict_flag or resolved_close_date changed, fan out to matching WatchlistSubscriptions via a Node worker polling a Postgres-backed outbox table. HMAC-SHA256 signed. (c) CSV/ICS export endpoint — /deadlines/export.csv and /deadlines/export.ics — for customers who want to pull into Google Calendar or a spreadsheet. No email digests in v1 — let the webhook hit Zapier/Make and the customer handles routing.
pipeline_stages.tech: Express or Fastify API; Postgres outbox table (delivered boolean, retry_count); Node worker polling outbox every 60 seconds. OpenAPI spec generated from Zod schemas — publish on day 1 because B2B buyers need a spec to evaluate the product.

pipeline_stages.what: A Retool dashboard (or a Next.js admin page — 2 days of work) shows all records where conflict_flag=true or confidence=low/conflicting, ordered by resolved_close_date ASC. The founder reviews these daily during the design-partner phase, manually corrects resolved_close_date, sets resolved_close_date_source=manual, and notes conflict_reason. This is the human-review console in its correct v1 form: a spreadsheet with a save button, not a built system. It becomes a built system when the volume of conflicts exceeds what the founder can review in 30 minutes/day — that threshold is probably >500 open dockets simultaneously, which is months away.
pipeline_stages.tech: Retool connected to Postgres (30-minute setup). Alternatively a Next.js /admin route with Clerk auth and a simple table + edit form.

components.name: Poller / Source Adapters
components.responsibility: Fetch FR API and Regulations.gov v4 on a 4-hour cycle. Upsert raw payloads into raw_fr_payload and raw_regs_gov_payload JSONB columns. Handle rate limits (Regs.gov 1,000 req/hr; commenting sublimit 500/hr) with exponential backoff.
components.tech: Node.js (TypeScript); node-fetch or native fetch; pg (node-postgres); deployed as a Render background worker or Railway cron.

components.name: Conflict Classifier
components.responsibility: Run the 5 deterministic rules on every upsert. Call Claude Haiku only for R5 ambiguous extension-title cases. Write conflict_flag, confidence, conflict_reason back to DeadlineRecord.
components.tech: TypeScript function called synchronously in the upsert transaction. Haiku calls via Anthropic SDK (structured output, 50-token response). Mirrulations S3 / spicy-regs Parquet used as an EVAL set to validate rule accuracy — query via DuckDB locally, not in the hot path.

components.name: Extension Chain Detector
components.responsibility: After classifying a notice as an extension/correction/reopening candidate (R5), attempt to link it to a parent DeadlineRecord via shared docket_id or frDocNum cross-reference in the FR API regulations_dot_gov_info field. Write an ExtensionLink row and update the parent's resolved_close_date if the new date is later.
components.tech: TypeScript; Postgres join on docket_id array overlap (Postgres && operator on text[]). Docket-ID linkage is the primary join key per the ACUS interoperability research.

components.name: REST API + OpenAPI
components.responsibility: Expose DeadlineRecord as a queryable endpoint. API key auth. Pagination. Filter by agency, docket_id, status, confidence, closing_within_days. Return resolved deadline + conflict metadata + raw source provenance fields. Publish OpenAPI spec at /docs.
components.tech: Fastify (TypeScript); Zod schemas generate OpenAPI via fastify-zod or @asteasolutions/zod-to-openapi. Deployed on Render or Railway. Postgres connection via connection pooling (PgBouncer on Supabase or Railway built-in).

components.name: Webhook Outbox Worker
components.responsibility: Poll a Postgres outbox table (deadline_events) every 60 seconds. Fan out HMAC-signed POSTs to matching WatchlistSubscription webhook URLs. Retry up to 3x with exponential backoff. Mark delivered.
components.tech: Node.js; no BullMQ, no Temporal, no Redis in v1. Postgres is the queue. This is sufficient for <1,000 subscriptions and <10,000 events/day — the realistic design-partner load.

components.name: Admin / Founder Review Panel
components.responsibility: Surface conflict_flag=true and confidence=low/conflicting records for daily manual review. Allow editing resolved_close_date and conflict_reason. The only 'human-review console' needed in v1.
components.tech: Retool (2-hour setup) or a minimal Next.js /admin route with Clerk auth. NOT a built system — this is a founder tool.

data_sources.role: Primary discovery source. Fetches proposed rules and notices with comments_close_on, dates text, docket_ids, regulations_dot_gov_info, publication_date, document_number. Keyless — no rate-limit concern for polling at 4-hour intervals.
data_sources.gotchas: FederalRegister.gov is legally unofficial (verify against govinfo PDF for legal reliance). Field semantics undocumented (GitHub issue #9). comments_close_on is date-only — no timezone. corrections/correction_of fields are unreliable (FR-2024-03267 example: null even when a correction exists). Store dates text as raw string; parse only for conflict detection.

data_sources.role: Primary status source. commentEndDate (ISO 8601 with offset), openForComment, withinCommentPeriod, allowLateComments, withdrawn, frDocNum (join key to FR), lastModifiedDate. ~1,000 documents open at any time.
data_sources.gotchas: 1,000 req/hr key limit total; commenting sub-API capped at 500/hr and 50/min — use GET key, not commenting key, for monitoring. filter[withinCommentPeriod]=false not accepted (API only filters true). commentEndDate can be null when openForComment=true (R3 conflict). frDocNum join to FR document_number is the primary reconciliation key but can be missing or wrong per ACUS interoperability finding. Pagination: 20 pages x 250 records max; use lastModifiedDate cursor for full-corpus sweeps.

data_sources.role: Legal backstop reference URL only in v1. Store the govinfo_url on each DeadlineRecord so the buyer can click through to the authoritative source. Do NOT parse govinfo PDFs in v1 — that requires a PDF pipeline that adds weeks of engineering.
data_sources.gotchas: govinfo URL construction from FR document_number is straightforward (https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/FR-{YYYY-MM-DD}/html/{frDocNum}.htm). Include this URL in every API response and in the admin panel — it is the single most important trust signal for law-firm buyers.

data_sources.role: EVAL set only — not in the live pipeline. Use DuckDB locally to query historical comment-window fields and label a ground-truth dataset of extension/reopening/withdrawal notices. Validate the 5-rule conflict classifier against this labeled set before launch. The ACUS and GAO research confirms data quality problems exist in this corpus — treat it as a noisy eval set, not a clean training set.
data_sources.gotchas: Mirrulations does NOT reduce ongoing freshness cost — it is historical. Do not build a live pipeline on Mirrulations; it will always lag the live Regulations.gov API. Use it only for offline testing of the conflict classifier rules.

proposal.confidence_model: Five deterministic rules produce a three-tier confidence signal: HIGH (Regs.gov and FR agree on date within 1 day, openForComment=true, no extension/correction language in title/action, fetched within 4 hours), MEDIUM (FR-only record with no Regs.gov match, or Regs.gov commentEndDate is null but openForComment=true, or fetched 4–48 hours ago), LOW (Regs.gov openForComment=false but future FR date exists, or title/action contains ambiguous extension/correction language that the deny-list did not resolve), CONFLICTING (FR and Regs.gov dates differ by more than 1 day — the FR-2018-27875 real example: FR 2019-03-14, Regs.gov 2019-03-15, plus the HUD outage-extension example), STALE (resolved_close_date is in the past but neither source has closed the record). CONFLICTING is the highest-value signal for buyers — it is the one case where existing tools give no signal at all. Suppress closing-soon alerts when confidence=CONFLICTING or STALE — a bad alert is worse than no alert. Do NOT use an LLM to score confidence — all 5 rules are SQL/TypeScript and run in the upsert transaction with zero latency. LLM is reserved for R5 extension-title disambiguation only, and only when the deny-list fails.

tech_stack.why: Zod schema-first approach generates both runtime validation and OpenAPI spec in one pass. The buyer's first integration question will be 'can I see the API spec' — Zod + openapi-zod-adapter answers that on day 1. No Python vs TypeScript debate needed: the FR and Regulations.gov APIs are REST, the logic is simple joins and rules, and TypeScript's type system catches the inevitable field-name bugs (frDocNum vs fr_doc_num casing differences between sources).

Postgres is the entire infrastructure in v1. It is the queue (outbox table), the event log (JSONB raw payloads), the search index (FTS on title + agency), and the canonical store. Supabase adds a free hosted tier, built-in PgBouncer, and Row Level Security for multi-tenant API keys. No Redis, no Elasticsearch, no Temporal, no separate queue. Postgres FTS handles 'show me all EPA deadlines in the next 14 days' — the actual query a design-partner wants.

Fastest time from 'Zod schema' to 'running OpenAPI endpoint.' fastify-type-provider-zod wires Zod directly into Fastify's serialization pipeline. The alternative (Express + manual swagger-jsdoc) is more setup for the same output.

Zero-ops managed containers. Deploy a background worker (poller), a web service (API), and a cron job (outbox worker) all from the same repo. No Kubernetes, no ECS task definitions, no Lambda cold starts on webhook fan-out. Render's $25/month plan is sufficient for the design-partner phase.

Connects to Postgres in 30 minutes. The founder's daily conflict-review workflow is a table filtered by conflict_flag=true, sorted by resolved_close_date ASC, with an edit modal. Building this as a custom Next.js route is also fine (2 days) but Retool is faster for a non-customer-facing internal tool.

Called only for R5 extension-title disambiguation when the deny-list fails. Structured JSON output (50 tokens: {is_comment_period_extension: bool, confidence: high|low}). Estimated cost: <$1/day at launch volume. Do NOT use an LLM for deadline resolution or confidence scoring — those are deterministic rules. Do NOT use an LLM for the hot path.

SQL-query the Mirrulations historical corpus locally to label extension/correction/reopening ground truth and validate the 5-rule classifier before launch. No cloud cost, no pipeline complexity. The spicy-regs MCP pattern is also the right interface to expose to AI-agent buyers later — but build the MCP adapter only when a customer asks for it.

proposal.mvp_scope: 90-day target: a single design-partner contract (regulatory-affairs team at a life-sciences company, or a law-firm KM desk) paying $500–$2,000/month for API access + webhook alerts. The MVP delivers exactly: (1) all federal comment periods currently open or closing in the next 90 days, refreshed every 4 hours; (2) per-record confidence flag with conflict reason and source provenance (FR raw, Regs.gov raw, govinfo URL); (3) REST API with OpenAPI spec and API key auth; (4) webhooks that fire when a deadline changes or a conflict is detected; (5) CSV/ICS export. Explicitly OUT of v1: email digests (Zapier handles this), state/local sources (funded customer trigger required), OpenSearch (Postgres FTS suffices for <10,000 records), human-review console (Retool for founder), immutable event log (JSONB columns suffice for auditability), OCD-ID normalization (no downstream builder needs this yet), Temporal/BullMQ (Postgres outbox suffices), spicy-regs MCP API (AI-agent buyer is a phase-2 customer), Watershed Watch geo-scoping (vertical product built on top of DocketClock, not part of it).
codex_steal: ParticipationWindow as the unit of trust — the insight that a deadline is a first-class object with provenance, confidence, and change history is exactly right. Rename to DeadlineRecord and flatten for v1, but the concept is the product.

Trust model: show uncertainty, suppress alerts when confidence drops, unknown/conflicting are first-class states. This is the differentiator against every existing single-source tracker. Keep this exactly as Codex stated it.

Federal-first expansion rule: do NOT add state/local until a paying customer funds a specific jurisdiction tranche. The mismatch-evidence research confirms state/local has no normalized cross-state rulemaking API. This rule should be written into the product spec as a literal 'thou shalt not.'

RuleBox pattern: LLM generates classification rules that run on cheap deterministic infra; LLM only adjudicates ambiguous cases. Apply directly to R5 extension-title disambiguation. The deny-list of false-positive terms (land withdrawal, permit term extension, public meeting extension) is the RuleBox output — generate it once with an LLM, run it deterministically forever.

Zod/JSON Schema + public OpenAPI: the B2B buyer's first question is 'can I see the API spec.' Generate the OpenAPI spec from Zod schemas on day 1. This is not optional for law-firm or regulatory-affairs buyers.

Postgres as the canonical store with JSONB raw observations: Codex's instinct to keep raw observations is correct. In v1, JSONB columns on the DeadlineRecord (raw_fr_payload, raw_regs_gov_payload) achieve the same auditability as a separate observation table without the schema complexity.

frDocNum as the primary join key between FR and Regulations.gov: the official-semantics research confirms this is the correct reconciliation key. Use it. Fall back to docket_id array overlap when frDocNum is missing (per ACUS: agencies sometimes omit it).

codex_drop.item: Immutable event log as a separate table / append-only store
codex_drop.why: JSONB columns on the DeadlineRecord (raw_fr_payload, raw_regs_gov_payload) plus Postgres updated_at triggers give sufficient auditability for a design partner. A separate append-only observation table adds schema complexity, migration overhead, and query indirection for zero additional buyer value in v1. Add it when a paying customer specifically requires a full audit log for compliance purposes — at that point it is a premium feature they will pay for.

codex_drop.item: Temporal / BullMQ / Cloud Tasks queue manager
codex_drop.why: A Postgres outbox table polled every 60 seconds handles the entire v1 webhook fan-out load. At ~1,000 open dockets and a plausible 100 design-partner watchlist subscriptions, the outbox never gets more than a few thousand rows. Temporal is the right answer at 10,000 subscriptions and SLA guarantees — not at the design-partner phase. Adding it now is three weeks of infrastructure work that produces no customer-visible feature.

codex_drop.item: OpenSearch / Elasticsearch
codex_drop.why: Postgres full-text search (tsvector on title + agency + docket_id) handles every query a design-partner needs: 'show me all EPA proposed rules closing in 14 days', 'find all dockets matching climate water'. At <10,000 DeadlineRecords, Postgres FTS returns results in <100ms. OpenSearch becomes the right tool when faceted search and relevance ranking across millions of historical documents become a buyer requirement — which is a phase-2 or phase-3 problem.

codex_drop.item: Human-review console as a built system
codex_drop.why: Retool connected to Postgres is the human-review console. Building a custom review UI is a week of engineering that produces nothing a design partner sees. The design-partner phase has <50 conflicting records per week — the founder reviews them in Retool in 20 minutes. Build the review console as a product feature only when a customer explicitly asks for it (e.g., their own team wants to resolve conflicts) — at which point it is a paid workflow feature.

codex_drop.item: Full normalizer graph (entity resolution across notices / dockets / agencies / CFR parts)
codex_drop.why: The full normalizer is architecturally correct for a mature product but is six weeks of engineering that does not change what the first customer can do. V1 normalizer is: (a) frDocNum join for FR-to-Regs.gov reconciliation, (b) docket_id array overlap for extension-chain detection, (c) ExtensionLink table for chained notices. That covers the real cases in the mismatch-evidence research (EPA NPDES three-notice chain, PHMSA 90-day extension, HUD outage extension). Full CFR-part normalization, RIN-to-docket deduplication, and multi-agency sub-docket entity resolution are deferred.

codex_drop.item: OCD-ID normalization as a v1 requirement
codex_drop.why: OCD-IDs are the right long-term substrate for downstream builders and the Watershed Watch vertical. They are irrelevant to the first B2B buyer (a regulatory-affairs team or law firm) who joins on docket_id or frDocNum, not on OCD-IDs. Add OCD normalization when a civic-app builder or Watershed Watch pilot requires it — that is explicitly a phase-2 concern.

codex_drop.item: Email digest delivery as a v1-built feature
codex_drop.why: Webhooks + Zapier/Make handle email delivery without any code. The design partner's workflow team will set up the Zapier webhook-to-email in 10 minutes. Building a digest mailer (template, unsubscribe, SPF/DKIM, bounce handling) is a week of work for a feature that Zapier provides for free. Add native email only when a customer explicitly cannot use Zapier (which means they are enterprise enough to pay for it).

key_risks.risk: Regulations.gov frDocNum join fails for a significant fraction of records (ACUS documents that agencies often omit or misconfigure identifiers), leaving a pool of unreconciled Regs.gov records with no FR counterpart and vice versa.
key_risks.mitigation: Fall back to docket_id array overlap join (Postgres && on text[] columns) when frDocNum is null. For records that cannot be joined by either key, emit confidence=medium with conflict_reason='no cross-source join possible' rather than dropping the record. Track join-failure rate as a daily metric — if it exceeds 20%, implement a fuzzy title-match fallback (trigram similarity on normalized title strings using pg_trgm).

key_risks.risk: The 5-rule heuristic generates too many false-positive CONFLICTING flags from the 1-day date difference that is likely just end-of-day Eastern vs UTC midnight normalization (the FR-2018-27875 example). Buyers lose trust in the conflict flag if it fires on noise.
key_risks.mitigation: R1 fires CONFLICTING only when dates differ by more than 1 day. A 1-day difference is classified as medium confidence with a specific conflict_reason of 'possible-timezone-normalization' — not as CONFLICTING. This distinction is explicitly surfaced in the API response. After 30 days of design-partner use, review the false-positive rate and adjust the threshold if needed.

key_risks.risk: Regulations.gov API key rate limit (1,000 req/hr) becomes a bottleneck if the number of open dockets grows or if the 4-hour polling cycle generates too many calls.
key_risks.mitigation: At ~1,000 open dockets and 4-hour cycles, the peak demand is ~250 document-detail calls per cycle plus ~50 list-query calls — well within the 1,000/hr limit. If the corpus grows, apply for a key-limit increase (GSA grants these case-by-case per the official API docs). Do not attempt to work around the limit with multiple keys — that violates ToS and risks access revocation, which would be fatal.

key_risks.risk: A wrong CONFLICTING flag on a high-stakes regulatory deadline (e.g., an FDA NPRM closing date) reaches a law-firm buyer who relies on it without verifying the govinfo PDF, causing them to miss a filing window and creating legal liability.
key_risks.mitigation: Every API response and every webhook payload includes govinfo_url and a disclaimer field ('This is a monitoring aid. Verify the operative deadline against the official Federal Register notice at the linked govinfo URL before legal reliance.'). The admin panel and any future UI surfaces this disclaimer prominently. DocketClock never claims to be the legal authority — it claims to surface conflicts that require human verification. This framing is the product's value proposition, not a hedge against it.

key_risks.risk: The design-partner customer is a law firm or regulatory-affairs team that requires SOC 2, security review, and vendor procurement before signing — blocking revenue for 6+ months.
key_risks.mitigation: Target the first customer at a small regulatory-affairs team or boutique policy shop where the buyer is also the technical evaluator (no IT procurement). Regulatory consultants and small-firm KM desks can sign an MSA on a credit card. Use Stripe for billing. The SOC 2 / enterprise procurement track is a phase-2 unlock after the first 3 paying customers validate the product thesis.

open_questions: Does frDocNum-based join actually work at >80% hit rate across the live corpus, or does the ACUS finding (agencies omit identifiers frequently) mean docket_id overlap is the primary key in practice? Validate with a 1-week live pilot against the Regulations.gov and FR APIs before designing the conflict classifier.

What is the actual daily volume of new extension/correction/reopening notices? The mismatch-evidence research shows several concrete examples but does not give a population base rate. This determines whether R5 (extension-chain detection) is a daily maintenance burden or a weekly edge case.

For the first design-partner target (law-firm KM desk or regulatory-affairs team at a life-sciences company), does the buyer need per-agency SLA guarantees ('if an FDA deadline changes, you will notify us within X hours') or is 4-hour refresh acceptable? The answer determines whether the polling cycle needs to shorten or whether a Regulations.gov webhook (if GSA ever exposes one) would be more appropriate.

Is the Watershed Watch vertical viable as a paying design-partner pilot before DocketClock has a paying B2B customer, given that the user has personal-network access to people in that persona? If yes, the geo-scoping layer (USGS WBD HUC-8/12 intersection) and the Open States v3 state-legislation layer could be fast-followed — but only if a Waterkeeper affiliate or Chesapeake Bay Foundation chapter commits to a pilot contract, not merely expresses interest.
deferred: OCD-ID normalization — correct long-term; irrelevant for first B2B contract
Immutable event log as a separate append-only table — JSONB columns suffice for v1 auditability
OpenSearch / Elasticsearch — Postgres FTS handles <10,000 records
Temporal / BullMQ / Redis queue — Postgres outbox handles design-partner load
Human-review console as a built product — Retool is sufficient for founder review
Email digest delivery — Zapier webhook handles this for design partners
GovInfo PDF parsing pipeline — govinfo_url as a clickable link is sufficient; parsing requires a full PDF pipeline
State / local sources — explicitly blocked until a funded customer commits to a specific jurisdiction
spicy-regs MCP API endpoint — the right interface for AI-agent buyers but a phase-2 customer segment
Full normalizer graph (CFR-part linkage, RIN-to-docket deduplication, multi-agency sub-docket entity resolution)
Pol.is consensus layer and Watershed Watch action loop — consumer/vertical product built on top, not part of DocketClock
Watershed Watch geo-scoping (USGS WBD HUC-8/12 intersection + Open States v3) — fast-follow only if a Waterkeeper affiliate or Chesapeake Bay Foundation chapter commits to a pilot contract
delivery: Week 1-2: Stand up Postgres schema (DeadlineRecord, ExtensionLink, WatchlistSubscription, deadline_events outbox). Write FR API and Regulations.gov v4 pollers. Upsert raw payloads. Validate frDocNum join hit rate against live corpus.
Week 3: Implement 5-rule conflict classifier in TypeScript. Integrate Claude Haiku for R5 disambiguation. Run classifier against Mirrulations/spicy-regs eval set (DuckDB locally) and tune deny-list.
Week 4: Ship Fastify REST API with OpenAPI spec. API key auth. Webhook outbox worker. CSV/ICS export endpoint.
Week 5: Wire Retool admin panel to Postgres. Daily founder review workflow operational. Ship govinfo_url on every record.
Week 6-8: Engage design-partner prospects. Share OpenAPI spec and a live demo watchlist. Iterate on conflict_reason copy and webhook payload shape based on prospect feedback.

Week 9-12: First design-partner contract signed. Iterate on delivery based on their actual workflow needs (do they want Slack via Zapier? a calendar embed? a Postgres direct connection via read-only credentials?).

critique.target: DocketClock v1 architecture proposal (SHIP-FAST PRAGMATIST stance)
strengths: The ParticipationWindow-to-DeadlineRecord rename and deliberate flattening is correct. For the first B2B contract, a single wide-row canonical object with JSONB raw payload columns is cheaper to query, cheaper to explain to a buyer during a demo, and sufficient for audit-trail purposes. The upstream research confirms the pain is real and the reconciliation gap is under-served; this architecture addresses that gap directly.

The five-rule heuristic conflict classifier is the right engine choice. The research confirms extensions, reopenings, corrections, and withdrawals are the primary staleness vectors. Deterministic SQL/TypeScript rules that run in the upsert transaction carry zero latency and zero per-call cost. The LLM is correctly scoped to the one genuinely ambiguous case (R5 keyword disambiguation) and correctly estimated at trivial cost (<$1/day). RuleBox discipline is applied well.

The ExtensionLink junction table is architecturally sound. The mismatch-evidence research shows multi-notice chains (EPA NPDES: original + extension + reopening) are the primary high-stakes staleness pattern. Modeling the chain explicitly rather than just overwriting resolved_close_date means a buyer can always ask 'why did this deadline change' and get a real answer. This is the trust primitive.

The 90-day, single-design-partner, $500–$2,000/month revenue target is calibrated to reality. The market-signals research shows standalone deadline feeds are self-serve/$50–$500/month territory unless they carry SLA, redistribution rights, and enterprise procurement support. Targeting a boutique regulatory-affairs team or law-firm KM desk with a credit-card MSA is the correct bypass for the 6-month SOC 2 procurement trap.

The federal-first hard wall is correct and correctly stated as a 'thou shalt not.' The research confirms state/local has no normalized cross-state rulemaking API. The unmaintainability trap is real. Writing it into the product spec as a literal constraint is the right move.

CONFLICTING as a first-class confidence state, with alert suppression when confidence=CONFLICTING or STALE, is the correct trust model and the actual product differentiator. Every existing single-source tracker silently publishes one date. Surfacing disagreement is the net-new value. The proposal states this clearly and builds the heuristics around it.

The govinfo_url-on-every-record call is the single most important trust signal for law-firm buyers and is handled correctly: include the URL, disclaim legal authority, and never claim to replace the official PDF. The research synthesis explicitly validates this framing.

Postgres-as-the-entire-infrastructure-in-v1 is the right call. Postgres outbox for webhooks, FTS for search, JSONB for raw payloads, and pg_trgm for fallback fuzzy matching all eliminate external dependencies. The design-partner-phase load (1,000 open dockets, 100 subscriptions) is entirely within Postgres's comfort zone.

The delivery schedule (Week 1-2: schema + pollers; Week 3: classifier; Week 4: API + webhooks; Week 5: admin; Week 6-8: design-partner engagement) is realistic for a solo TypeScript engineer and leaves 4 weeks of buffer before the 90-day contract target.

The frDocNum join with docket_id array overlap fallback, and the explicit acknowledgment of ACUS's identifier-omission finding, is sound. The proposal does not pretend the join is reliable — it builds a fallback and tracks failure rate as a metric.

weaknesses.issue: The document-vs-docket level modeling ambiguity is a latent data bug that will surface in Week 1 and is not fully resolved in the schema.
weaknesses.why: The research synthesis explicitly states: 'Model deadlines at the document level, not only docket level, because dockets can contain multiple documents with distinct comment periods.' The official-semantics research repeats this: 'comments are retrieved for each document by filtering comments with the document's internal objectId as filter[commentOnId].' The proposal's DeadlineRecord uses fr_doc_number as a natural primary key, which is document-level. But the WatchlistSubscription filter_type enum includes docket_id as a filter target. The EPA two-actions example — one Federal Register notice extending two separate comment periods with different new deadlines — is a concrete case where a docket contains multiple documents with distinct deadlines. The schema as designed does not clearly specify whether a docket_id subscription triggers on any document within that docket or only on specific documents. During design-partner engagement, a regulatory-affairs buyer who watches a docket expecting coverage of all its documents will get partial alerts unless this is handled explicitly. The fix is simple (join docket_id -> documents in the subscription fan-out worker) but it is not stated in the architecture and will cause a real support incident if left implicit.

weaknesses.issue: R1 conflict rule fires on a 1-day difference that may be a routine UTC/Eastern normalization artifact, but the 'more than 1 day' threshold proposed is too coarse and the 'possible-timezone-normalization' medium confidence reason will confuse law-firm buyers.
weaknesses.why: The known FR-2018-27875 example is FR 2019-03-14 vs Regs.gov 2019-03-15 — exactly a 1-day difference that the proposal correctly classifies as medium/timezone ambiguity rather than CONFLICTING. However, the NPS Alaska hunting/trapping example documents a real case where the electronic deadline is 11:59 p.m. Eastern and 7:59 p.m. Alaska time on the same calendar date — a within-day timezone split that has legal consequences. The proposal's timezone handling says 'store as timestamptz; Regs.gov commentEndDate is ISO 8601 with offset (typically UTC midnight); FR comments_close_on is date-only — store as date::date with a note that end-of-day Eastern is the implied semantics.' This is correct but incomplete. Regs.gov commentEndDate is 'typically UTC midnight' — but what if the agency enters 11:59 p.m. Eastern, which is 04:59 UTC the next day? That produces a genuine 1-day calendar-date difference in UTC that is NOT a data conflict — it is just timezone math. The proposal does not say how to detect this case before emitting conflict_reason='possible-timezone-normalization.'
