Status transitions: open -> closed -> (optionally) extended | reopened | withdrawn | finalized. This is the lifecycle closure Codex describes — KEEP it. Do not attempt to track final-rule outcomes or causal impact claims; that belongs to the vertical action layer.
pipeline_stages.tech: BullMQ delayed jobs scheduled at resolved_closes_at + 7d; FR API query by docket_id and regulation_id_number; status machine in Postgres

components.name: Source Adapters
components.responsibility: Federal Register API adapter (keyless, polls every 15m, fields: document_number, comments_close_on, dates, comment_url, docket_ids, regulations_dot_gov_info, correction_of, corrections, action, title, publication_date). Regulations.gov v4 adapter (keyed, 1,000 req/hr, fields: documentId, objectId, docketId, frDocNum, commentStartDate, commentEndDate, openForComment, withinCommentPeriod, allowLateComments, withdrawn, lastModifiedDate). GovInfo adapter (govinfo.gov PDF/XML links — used as provenance URL, not primary parse source). Mirrulations adapter is offline/batch only — used to seed the Observation table for historical records and build the conflict-classifier eval set. No state/local adapters until a funded source cluster exists.
components.tech: TypeScript; Zod schemas for each adapter output; BullMQ; Redis cursor store

components.name: Immutable Observation Log
components.responsibility: Append-only store of every raw observation from every source. Payload hashing for idempotency. Verbatim preservation of DATES text. Extension/correction/withdrawal notice flags parsed at insert. Never updated — only appended. The audit trail that makes deadline changes legally defensible.
components.tech: Postgres; row-level security; no UPDATE or DELETE grants on observations table; SHA-256 payload hash index

components.name: Reconciliation Engine (Confidence Engine)
components.responsibility: Deterministic JSON rulebook (RuleBox pattern) that reads the Observation chain for a ParticipationWindow and produces: resolved_closes_at, confidence, conflict_flags, status. LLM call (Claude Haiku via Anthropic API) is a fallback ONLY for ambiguous DATES text that cannot be parsed by the deterministic parser — logged and flagged for human review. Rule regression tests run against spicy-regs Parquet corpus before any rulebook update ships.
components.tech: TypeScript; JSON rulebook; Zod rule schema; DuckDB (spicy-regs) for regression testing; Anthropic API (Haiku) for LLM fallback

components.name: Enrichment API + Hook Registry
components.responsibility: After reconciliation, allows registered enrichment workers (built-in or tenant-supplied) to write tags to ParticipationWindow.tags within their allowed_tag_namespaces. Built-in enrichment: OCD agency slug normalization, CFR part extraction. Tenant enrichment: geo HUC codes, keyword topics, vertical-specific classifiers. This is the extensibility contract — DocketClock core never reads enrichment tags for reconciliation logic.
components.tech: Fastify sub-router; authenticated by tenant API key; namespace validation in middleware; Postgres JSONB tags column with GIN index

components.name: Human Review Console
components.responsibility: Thin Next.js admin UI surfacing only windows with confidence=conflicting or confidence=low or conflict_flags containing extension_chain or tz_ambiguous. Reviewer can inspect the full Observation chain, mark a resolved deadline as human-verified (adds a special Observation with source=human_review), or flag for escalation. This is NOT a public product — it is an internal trust tool for the DocketClock operator and design partners. Do not build features for end-users here.
components.tech: Next.js; Postgres; restricted to internal tenant_id; read-only observation display + human_review observation insert

components.name: Registry + Delivery API
components.responsibility: Public REST API (OpenAPI 3.1): GET /windows (search, filter by agency, confidence, status, tags, closes_before/after), GET /windows/{ocd_id} (full object + observations), GET /watchlists, POST /watchlists, GET /agencies, GET /feed (RSS/Atom), GET /calendar (ICS). Webhook delivery: per-Watchlist, HMAC-signed, retried with exponential backoff. MCP server: search_windows, get_window, list_observations, subscribe_watchlist tools. Bulk export: JSONL and CSV snapshots per agency or watchlist. Every response envelope includes disclaimer, api_version, and request_id.
components.tech: Fastify; OpenAPI 3.1 (generated from Zod schemas); BullMQ webhook delivery workers; MCP SDK server (stdio or SSE transport); HMAC-SHA256; ICS generation library; Postgres FTS (tsvector + tsquery)

data_sources.role: Primary discovery source for new notice publications. Key fields: document_number (join key to Regulations.gov frDocNum), comments_close_on (date-only — NEVER silently convert to midnight UTC), dates (verbatim DATES text — legally authoritative language lives here), comment_url, docket_ids, regulations_dot_gov_info (embedded Regulations.gov fields for cross-reference), correction_of (UNRELIABLE — often null even for corrections per FR GitHub issue #9, so also parse action/title text), corrections.
data_sources.gotchas: FederalRegister.gov is not legal notice — it is an unofficial XML rendition per their own reader-aids page. comments_close_on is date-only with no timezone — one-day discrepancy with Regulations.gov commentEndDate is documented (FR doc 2018-27875: FR says 2019-03-14, Regulations.gov says 2019-03-15). correction_of field is unreliable (documented null even for explicit corrections). Extension notices are SEPARATE documents that do not update the original document's comments_close_on — must be detected by action/title text ('extension of comment period') and linked by docket_id. Rate: keyless, generous.

data_sources.role: Primary status source. Key fields: commentEndDate (ISO 8601 with tz offset — timezone-stamped, preferred for resolved_closes_at), openForComment (boolean current status — use this alongside commentEndDate, not instead of it), withinCommentPeriod (filter only, filter[withinCommentPeriod]=false not accepted), allowLateComments, withdrawn, frDocNum (join key to FR document_number), objectId (internal ID for comment associations — use commentOnId=objectId to fetch comments, not documentId), lastModifiedDate (cursor for change detection — UTC/Eastern mismatch documented, must handle).
data_sources.gotchas: Rate limit: 1,000 req/hr per key. Commenting API stricter: 50 req/min, 500 req/hr. >5,000 comments requires lastModifiedDate cursor pagination with deduplication (GSA documents this as beta and may remove it). objectId vs documentId distinction is critical — comments are fetched by commentOnId=objectId, not documentId. lastModifiedDate is UTC in responses but Eastern in filter parameters (documented by GSA example and willjobs wrapper — this will cause missed records if not handled). Extension notice documents themselves may have commentEndDate=null and openForComment=false (documented in FR doc 2025-03547) — the extension applies to the ORIGINAL document, not the extension notice document. Agencies can update configurable fields (firstName, lastName, org, email) at any point.

data_sources.role: Legal provenance backstop. Every ParticipationWindow links to the govinfo PDF/HTML for the original FR document and any extension/correction notices. DocketClock does not parse govinfo — it links to it and embeds the URL in provenance. This is the disclaimer anchor: 'verify against official Federal Register editions on govinfo.gov.'
data_sources.gotchas: GovInfo requires an API key for bulk access. For provenance linking only, the public URL pattern (govinfo.gov/content/pkg/FR-{date}/html/{frDocNum}.htm) is sufficient without API calls.

data_sources.source: Mirrulations S3 Mirror + mirrulations-search Postgres schema
data_sources.role: Offline eval corpus ONLY. Used to seed historical Observations for records back to ~2012, build the labeled eval set for conflict-classifier regression tests, and mine extension/correction/withdrawal edge cases via DuckDB/SQL queries against the spicy-regs Parquet files. NOT a freshness source — Mirrulations ingestion lag makes it unsuitable for current-window tracking.
data_sources.gotchas: Mirrulations does not reduce the ongoing freshness cost. It is a one-time bootstrapping and testing asset. The mirrulations-search schema ships comment-window fields (is_open_for_comment, comment dates) that should be mapped to the Observation schema during seeding.

data_sources.role: SQL-queryable historical corpus for mining extension/correction chain patterns and running rule regression tests before shipping reconciliation rulebook updates. The MCP interface path: expose DocketClock's own Observation table via a spicy-regs-style DuckDB interface for AI-agent buyers who want to write SQL against the deadline history.
data_sources.gotchas: spicy-regs is a static corpus snapshot, not a live feed. Regression tests must be re-run against a refreshed snapshot periodically.

data_sources.source: USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD) via The National Map ArcGIS REST
data_sources.role: Geo primitive for Watershed Watch enrichment ONLY — not a DocketClock core source. The Watershed Watch enrichment worker resolves HUC-8/HUC-12 codes from agency/docket text and writes huc_8: and huc_12: tags to ParticipationWindow.tags via the enrichment API. DocketClock core is never aware of HUC codes.
data_sources.gotchas: This is a Watershed Watch concern, not a DocketClock concern. The substrate extensibility contract means DocketClock core never imports or queries WBD directly.

proposal.confidence_model: Five confidence levels, always exposed, never suppressed in API responses: HIGH (FR comments_close_on and Regulations.gov commentEndDate agree within 24h, no extension/correction/withdrawal notices in the Observation chain, Regulations.gov openForComment status consistent, resolved_closes_at = Regulations.gov commentEndDate with tz offset); MEDIUM (sources agree but one observation is 24-48h stale, or allowLateComments=true creates ambiguity about operative close); LOW (commentEndDate is null but openForComment=true, or only one source has a deadline value, or date-only FR value cannot be timezone-resolved); CONFLICTING (FR and Regulations.gov dates differ by >24h, OR an extension/correction notice exists in the chain but the operative deadline cannot be determined deterministically, OR withdrawn=true conflicts with openForComment=true — conflict_flags array populated with specific reason codes); STALE (last Observation for any source is >48h old for an open window — alert suppressed or downgraded per the trust model). The resolved_closes_at field is populated even for CONFLICTING records (best-available answer) but the conflict_flags array tells the consumer exactly why they should not trust it blindly. A bad deadline is worse than no alert — suppress webhooks/notifications when confidence drops to STALE or CONFLICTING unless the subscriber has explicitly opted into low-confidence alerts. Human review console surfaces CONFLICTING and LOW records; human-verified Observations upgrade confidence to HIGH with source=human_review provenance.

tech_stack.why: KEEP Codex's choice. Type safety on Zod schemas that generate both the OpenAPI spec and the DB column shapes is the correct call for a product whose core value is data contract precision.

KEEP Codex's choice. JSONB for raw Observation payloads (schema-flexible, indexable); typed columns for reconciled canonical fields; tsvector for full-text search on title/agency/dates text. Use Postgres row-level security for multi-tenancy. Defer OpenSearch until Postgres FTS is proven insufficient — the research shows ~1,000 documents open for comment at any time, which is trivially handled by Postgres FTS.

KEEP BullMQ over Temporal or Cloud Tasks. Temporal is operationally heavy for a 2-person team at MVP. BullMQ on Redis gives reliable queues, delayed jobs (for follow-up polling), dead-letter queues, and a good TypeScript SDK. Migrate to Temporal only if the workflow DAGs become complex enough to require it.

KEEP TypeScript API, CHANGE from generic 'TypeScript API' to Fastify specifically because of its schema-first plugin system. Zod schemas define canonical objects, generate OpenAPI 3.1 spec, and validate request/response at the API boundary. This enforces the public contract without duplication.

ADD — not in Codex's MVP stack. The MCP surface is a first-class deliverable for AI-agent buyers, not an afterthought. tools: search_windows, get_window, list_observations, subscribe_watchlist. SSE transport for persistent agent connections. This is what FiscalNote's PolicyNote MCP is doing — DocketClock must ship this on day one for the AI-agent buyer segment.

tech_stack.choice: Open Civic Data ID standard, federal namespace
tech_stack.why: ADD — not in Codex's MVP stack explicitly. Stable OCD-IDs (ocd-participation-window/federal/{frDocNum}) are the substrate's joinability promise. Verticals JOIN on OCD-IDs, not internal Postgres UUIDs. Generate on first Observation insert; never change.

KEEP RuleBox pattern from Codex. JSON-serialized rules that TypeScript evaluates deterministically. LLM (Claude Haiku via Anthropic API) is a fallback for ambiguous DATES text only — not for routine reconciliation. RuleBox means the rulebook can be updated and regression-tested without deploying new code.

KEEP Codex's 'small admin + design-partner watchlist UI' intent. CHANGE scope: this is an internal operator tool, not a public design-partner UI. Public design-partner access is via the API and webhook, not the console. Keeps UI scope minimal.

tech_stack.choice: Postgres FTS (tsvector + tsquery) at launch; OpenSearch deferred
tech_stack.why: CHANGE from Codex's 'Postgres FTS first then OpenSearch' — make the deferral a hard policy. With ~1,000 open windows at a time, Postgres FTS is sufficient indefinitely. OpenSearch is a significant operational burden; add it only when a specific query pattern (faceted search, relevance ranking beyond BM25) proves necessary.

ADD — not in Codex's stack. spicy-regs Parquet files are the regression test corpus for the reconciliation rulebook. DuckDB runs locally in CI — no infrastructure required. Mine extension/correction edge cases with SQL before writing rulebook entries.

tech_stack.choice: Fly.io (API + workers) + Upstash Redis + Supabase Postgres
tech_stack.why: Not in Codex. Concrete hosting choices matter for a 2-person MVP. Fly.io for TypeScript workers and Fastify API (cheap, deploys from Docker, good for BullMQ workers). Upstash for serverless Redis (pay-per-use, no Redis instance to manage). Supabase for Postgres (row-level security built in, branching for migrations).

proposal.mvp_scope: Federal-only. Scope: all Regulations.gov documents with openForComment=true or commentEndDate in the next 90 days. Deliver in this exact order: (1) Observation ingestion pipeline (FR + Regulations.gov adapters, immutable log, BullMQ) — this is working before anything else; (2) Reconciliation rulebook covering the four documented conflict patterns (date mismatch, extension chain, null-end-date-open-status, withdrawn-but-open) with regression tests against spicy-regs corpus; (3) REST API (GET /windows, GET /windows/{id}, GET /windows/{id}/observations) with OpenAPI 3.1 spec — this is the design-partner deliverable; (4) Webhook delivery for Watchlists; (5) MCP server with search_windows and get_window tools — ship this alongside the REST API, not after; (6) Human review console for CONFLICTING/LOW records — internal only. NOT in MVP: RSS/ICS (deferred to v1.1), bulk CSV export (deferred), public UI, state/local sources, geo enrichment (Watershed Watch builds this on top). Design-partner target: 2 law-firm current-awareness teams and 1 regulatory-affairs team in a life-sciences company, plus 1 AI-agent builder as an MCP beta partner. Success metric: design partners find at least 3 real-world extension/correction/withdrawal conflicts that existing single-source tools missed.
codex_steal: ParticipationWindow as the unit of trust — the name, the concept, and the discipline of treating it as a canonical object rather than a view over raw API data
Immutable event log with payload hashing and parser versioning — this is the architecture's backbone and Codex gets it exactly right
Trust model: unknown/stale/conflicting are first-class states; suppress alerts when confidence drops — do not publish fake certainty

RuleBox pattern for the reconciliation engine — LLM adjudicates only the ambiguous minority, deterministic rules handle the routine majority; costly LLM reserved for DATES text that the deterministic parser cannot resolve
MAPLE-pattern notification engine: follow->event->feed->digest; versioned public archive — steal the notification architecture even if not the Firestore stack

Federal-first expansion rule: do NOT add state/local sources until a funded source cluster and a paying customer justify the maintenance cost — this is the correct discipline and must be enforced as a policy, not a preference

5-stage pipeline concept (Discover -> Normalize -> Verify -> Publish -> Follow-up) — the stages are correct; this proposal renames Normalize to Observe and Verify to Reconcile for precision, but the logic is the same
Human review console scoped to uncertain/high-impact records only — do not build a general-purpose admin UI

codex_drop.item: Codex's 'Normalizer' as a monolithic entity-resolution graph
codex_drop.why: Codex describes the Normalizer as tying 'notices/dockets/agencies/CFR parts/comment URLs/corrections/reopenings into one canonical window graph.' This is over-engineered for MVP. The correct split: Observation insert handles raw parsing and notice-type flags; the Reconciliation Engine handles deadline resolution; the Enrichment API handles CFR part and agency slug normalization as pluggable enrichment hooks. A monolithic normalizer graph is a premature abstraction that will block the substrate from being extended by verticals without touching core.

codex_drop.item: Temporal for queue/workflow at MVP
codex_drop.why: Codex lists Temporal as the first queue option. Temporal is the right call at scale but wrong for a 2-person MVP — it requires a dedicated Temporal server, Go/Java server binary, and significant operational overhead. BullMQ on Redis gives 90% of the reliability guarantees with 10% of the ops cost. Revisit Temporal when workflow DAGs exceed what BullMQ can model cleanly.

codex_drop.item: Codex's framing of the 'Human review console' as a design-partner UI
codex_drop.why: The console must be internal-operator-only at MVP. Design partners get API access and webhooks, not access to the review console. Conflating the two creates a support and trust liability — design partners should not be approving deadline resolutions. They should be filing support tickets that become human-review queue items.

codex_drop.item: OpenSearch in the MVP stack
codex_drop.why: Codex lists 'Postgres FTS first then OpenSearch' as if OpenSearch is inevitable. With ~1,000 open comment windows at any time, Postgres tsvector handles all search needs indefinitely. OpenSearch is a significant operational burden (cluster management, index sync, dual-write). Hard-defer it unless a specific query pattern (faceted agency+topic search at scale) is proven necessary by real usage.

codex_drop.item: State/local sources as a named roadmap item
codex_drop.why: Codex correctly states the expansion rule but leaves state/local on an implicit roadmap. This proposal makes the ban explicit and enforced by architecture: the source adapter registry only accepts adapters for sources with a funded source cluster and a named paying customer. No speculative state/local adapters. The Legistar/Accela trap (no normalized comment-deadline field, fragmented municipal APIs) is well-documented — it consumes engineering bandwidth without producing a reliable substrate.

codex_drop.item: Named downstream users in the substrate design (pharmacy desks, childcare associations, journalists)
codex_drop.why: Codex lists downstream consumer personas inside the DocketClock architecture. This bleeds vertical concerns into substrate design. The substrate must be agnostic to end-user personas — verticals are built on top by separate teams (or by the same team in separate vertical products). Naming pharmacy desks in the DocketClock architecture creates pressure to add pharmacy-specific fields to the canonical object. The tags/enrichment-API pattern handles this without contaminating the core.

key_risks.risk: A published deadline that turns out to be wrong causes a design-partner to miss a comment window — legal and reputational consequence
key_risks.mitigation: Legal disclaimer embedded in every API response envelope. Confidence/conflict fields always present. Webhook suppression for CONFLICTING/STALE confidence unless subscriber opts in. Human review console for ambiguous records. govinfo PDF link in every window so the consumer can verify the primary source. Never claim to be the legal authority.

key_risks.risk: Regulations.gov API rate limits (1,000 req/hr) constrain freshness for high-volume polling during agency comment storms
key_risks.mitigation: Multi-key API key pool (DocketClock holds multiple api.data.gov keys registered to different addresses). Token bucket with per-key rate tracking in Redis. Prioritize polling recently-modified and near-deadline windows over historical closed records. Cache Observation responses and skip re-fetch if payload hash matches.

key_risks.risk: Extension/correction notice is a separate FR document that does not update the original Regulations.gov record — silent stale deadline is exactly the failure mode DocketClock claims to prevent
key_risks.mitigation: The reconciler treats any extension/correction notice in the Observation chain (detected at Observation insert time by action/title text parsing) as mandatory manual review unless the Regulations.gov commentEndDate has been updated to match the new date. This is documented in the research (FR doc 2025-03547: extension notice has commentEndDate=null and openForComment=false for the extension document itself). Confidence drops to CONFLICTING until the original document's Regulations.gov record reflects the new date.

key_risks.risk: Vertical enrichment workers write tags that pollute the canonical object or are used by other tenants' filter queries
key_risks.mitigation: Tag namespacing enforced by middleware: tenant A can only write tags in its allowed_tag_namespaces. Watchlist filter_spec can only query tags in the tenant's own namespace plus public built-in namespaces (agency:, cfr:, doc_type:). No cross-tenant tag visibility.

key_risks.risk: MCP surface becomes a liability if AI agents act on low-confidence deadlines without surfacing the confidence flag
key_risks.mitigation: MCP tool responses always include confidence and conflict_flags in the structured output. The MCP tool description explicitly states: 'Always display the confidence field and conflict_flags to end users before acting on a deadline.' Document this in the MCP tool schema description field, not just in external docs.

key_risks.risk: Watershed Watch enrichment geo-scoping misses dockets that affect a basin because agency text does not mention HUC codes or named water bodies
key_risks.mitigation: This is Watershed Watch's problem, not DocketClock's. The substrate delivers all federal open-comment windows; Watershed Watch's geo-enrichment worker is responsible for recall. DocketClock's job is to make the full federal corpus queryable — not to guarantee that every basin-relevant docket is tagged. Document this boundary explicitly in the extensibility contract.

open_questions: Should the Enrichment API allow tenants to WRITE resolved_closes_at overrides (e.g., a human-verified deadline from outside the standard sources) or only WRITE tags? Argument for overrides: a Watershed Watch design partner with direct agency contact might know the correct deadline before Regulations.gov updates. Argument against: allowing deadline overrides outside the standard reconciliation pipeline breaks the audit trail and creates a trust liability. Proposed answer: NO overrides via the Enrichment API — overrides go through the Human Review Console as a human_review Observation, which is then processed by the reconciler. This preserves the audit chain.

What is the right polling frequency for Regulations.gov given the 1,000 req/hr rate limit? For ~1,000 open windows, polling each once per hour consumes the entire rate budget on status checks alone, leaving no budget for discovery of new windows. Proposed answer: poll the withinCommentPeriod=true endpoint every 15 minutes (1 req) for the list, then fetch document detail only for records where lastModifiedDate has changed (differential polling using the cursor pattern). This dramatically reduces detail-fetch volume.

Should the OCD-ID be constructed from frDocNum alone (stable, unique) or from a combination of frDocNum + regsDocId (more specific but requires the join to succeed)? Proposed answer: frDocNum is the primary key for OCD-ID generation. If frDocNum is absent (Regulations.gov-only records), use regs:{regsDocId} as the OCD-ID suffix. Document the disambiguation rule in the public spec.

At what point does the human review console need to scale to external reviewers (design-partner staff)? The current design keeps it internal-only. If a design partner has a regulatory affairs analyst who should be approving deadline resolutions, the access model needs to expand without exposing the full operator console. Proposed answer: defer until a design partner requests it; when needed, add a tenant-scoped review queue with a separate UI (not the operator console).
deferred: RSS/ICS delivery (v1.1 — after REST API and webhooks are validated by design partners)
Bulk CSV/JSONL export (v1.1)
OpenSearch (indefinitely deferred pending Postgres FTS capacity proof)
State/local source adapters (until a funded source cluster and named paying customer)
Public consumer UI (this is a substrate, not a portal — verticals build UIs on top)
Watershed Watch geo-enrichment worker (built by Watershed Watch team on top of the enrichment API — not DocketClock core)
MAPLE-style digest email engine (v1.2 — initially webhooks handle notification delivery)
Pol.is consensus module (belongs to the action layer above the substrate)
Submission / comment-filing integration (belongs to the vertical action layer — DocketClock only links to submission_url, never proxies submissions)
Final-rule outcome tracking and causal impact claims (belongs to Comment Workshop / vertical products)
Multi-region / SOC 2 compliance (pre-enterprise-sale — validate product-market fit first)
delivery: Week 1-2: Postgres schema (ParticipationWindow, Observation, Watchlist, Tenant tables); FR and Regulations.gov adapters; BullMQ pipeline; immutable Observation log with payload hashing
Week 3-4: Reconciliation rulebook (4 core conflict patterns); regression test suite against spicy-regs Parquet corpus; confidence engine producing confidence + conflict_flags + resolved_closes_at
Week 5-6: Fastify REST API (GET /windows, GET /windows/{id}, GET /windows/{id}/observations); Zod-generated OpenAPI 3.1 spec; API key auth; rate limiting per tenant
Week 7: MCP server (search_windows, get_window tools); HMAC-signed webhook delivery for Watchlists
Week 8: Human review console (internal); design-partner onboarding for 2 law-firm teams and 1 regulatory-affairs team; OCD-ID generation and stable ID validation
Week 9-10: Enrichment API + hook registry; built-in agency-slug and CFR-part enrichment; tag-based Watchlist filtering; design-partner feedback loop
Week 11-12: Follow-up polling (post-close re-poll, final-rule linkage); confidence=stale detection; webhook suppression for low-confidence windows; v0.9 public API release to beta partners

strengths: Immutable observation log with payload hashing is architecturally sound and correctly treated as the backbone — this is the right foundation for an audit-defensible product and is non-negotiable for the trust model.

The separation of closes_at (raw parsed value) from resolved_closes_at (reconciled operative deadline) is a genuine insight. Most competitors silently choose one source; this surfaces the delta explicitly.

Explicit conflict_flags as a typed array rather than a single boolean is the right data model. It lets downstream consumers decide how to handle each failure mode rather than collapsing nuance into a confidence bucket.
The ban on state/local adapters until a funded source cluster exists is correctly enforced as architecture policy, not just a preference — and the reasoning (Legistar/Accela trap) is accurate.

Differential polling via lastModifiedDate cursor for Regulations.gov is the right call to stay within the 1,000 req/hr rate limit. Polling the withinCommentPeriod=true list endpoint once per cycle then fetching detail only on changes dramatically reduces API consumption.

The tags/enrichment-API extensibility pattern correctly isolates vertical concerns (HUC codes, CFR parts) from the canonical reconciliation logic. DocketClock core never has to understand what a HUC code means.
Dropping Temporal for BullMQ at MVP is the right call. Temporal's operational overhead is not justified for a 2-person team before the workflow DAG complexity warrants it.
The decision to scope the human review console as internal-operator-only at MVP is correct. Letting design partners approve deadline resolutions creates a support and trust liability.

Correctly identifying that extension notices in the Federal Register are SEPARATE documents that do not update the original Regulations.gov commentEndDate — this is the documented edge case (FR doc 2025-03547) and the architecture handles it by requiring a CONFLICTING flag until the source record updates.

RuleBox pattern for deterministic reconciliation with LLM fallback only for ambiguous DATES text is the right cost structure — LLM adjudication is expensive and should be reserved for the <5% edge cases.
The Regulations.gov objectId vs documentId distinction is correctly handled — comments fetched by commentOnId=objectId is a known developer trap that is explicitly surfaced in the adapter gotchas.
OCD-ID as the primary key for joinability across vertical products is a genuine substrate design decision, not a cosmetic one. Postgres UUIDs are not joinable across systems.
The delivery schedule is credible for a 2-person team — 12 weeks to a public API beta with design partners is tight but achievable if scope is held.

weaknesses.issue: The reconciliation rulebook rule (a) uses a 24-hour agreement window between FR and Regulations.gov to assign HIGH confidence, but this is exactly wrong. The known divergence (FR 2018-27875: FR says 2019-03-14, Regs.gov says 2019-03-15) IS a timezone/end-of-day normalization artifact that is 24 hours apart and does NOT represent a real conflict — it is a systemic, non-conflicting 1-day offset that will trigger CONFLICTING for a large fraction of routine records if the threshold is set at >24h. The rule should distinguish between a systemic 1-day timezone-normalization offset (expected, not a conflict) and a genuine extension/correction disagreement. Without this distinction, the CONFLICTING bucket floods with false positives, the human review queue is unworkable, and design partners lose trust in the confidence signal.
weaknesses.why: The synthesis and mismatch-evidence files both document that the 1-day discrepancy is a known normalization artifact, not a true mismatch. A 24-hour threshold will misclassify the majority of routine records as CONFLICTING, breaking the trust model the product is built on.

weaknesses.issue: The confidence model has no definition of what HIGH confidence actually means in operational terms for the buyer. A law firm staking a comment filing on a HIGH-confidence deadline needs to know: HIGH means both sources agree within X hours AND the Observation is fresh within Y minutes AND there are no extension/correction/withdrawal notices in the observation chain for the past Z days. None of these operational parameters are specified. Without SLA-like specificity, HIGH confidence is a marketing claim, not a trust primitive.
weaknesses.why: Buyers in the target segments (law firm KM, regulatory affairs) carry liability for missed deadlines. They will ask 'what exactly does HIGH mean?' and the answer cannot be 'the two sources agree within 24 hours' — that conflates freshness, source agreement, and extension-chain completeness into one underdefined level.

weaknesses.issue: The 'one notice extending two separate comment periods with two distinct new deadlines' pattern (EPA Feb 21, 2025, FR doc 2025-02910) is documented in the research but not addressed in the data model. The current model assumes a 1:1 mapping between a Federal Register document and a ParticipationWindow. A single FR notice can modify multiple dockets simultaneously. The entity model has no way to represent a single source document generating multiple ParticipationWindow updates without either (a) duplicating the observation or (b) building a many-to-many relationship between Observations and ParticipationWindows that is not in the schema.
weaknesses.why: This is a documented real-world case. If the reconciler sees the same FR document in the observation chain for two different ParticipationWindows, it does not know whether that is a data error or an intentional multi-docket extension. The schema needs an explicit observations_to_windows join table or a notice_applies_to array on Observation.

weaknesses.issue: The Regulations.gov lastModifiedDate cursor has a documented UTC-vs-Eastern timezone mismatch: response timestamps are UTC but the filter parameter uses Eastern time. The adapter gotchas section mentions this, but the proposed mitigation (UTC-to-Eastern deduplication) is underspecified. In practice, the mismatch creates a 4-5 hour overlap window (depending on DST) where records modified in that window will be missed on the next cursor-based poll unless the cursor is artificially pushed back by 5+ hours. This creates silent missed updates — exactly the failure mode DocketClock claims to prevent.
weaknesses.why: This is not a hypothetical risk — it is documented by GSA's own examples and by the willjobs wrapper developer. The architecture mentions the trap but does not specify the concrete mitigation: how many hours does the cursor overlap window need to be, and how does the deduplication handle the resulting duplicate observations?

weaknesses.issue: The BLM false-positive risk ('extension' in a notice title referring to a land withdrawal extension, not a comment-period extension) is documented in the research (FR doc 2023-27468) but the is_extension_notice flag is set at Observation insert time using 'lightweight regex rules on action, title, and dates text.' Regex on 'extension' in the title will produce a meaningful false-positive rate for non-comment-period extensions. The proposal says LLM adjudication is reserved for ambiguous DATES text, but the notice-type classification (is_extension_notice, is_withdrawal_notice) also needs context-aware classification — regex alone will misfire here. The confidence engine downstream will incorrectly flag these as CONFLICTING.
weaknesses.why: The research explicitly documents this case. A BLM notice titled 'Extension, Public Meetings and Correction' for a land withdrawal extension would be flagged is_extension_notice=true, triggering CONFLICTING confidence for a routine record. The false-positive rate for notice-type classification with regex is not bounded in the proposal.

weaknesses.issue: The Watchlist filter_spec queries ParticipationWindow.tags using tags populated by enrichment workers. But the proposal states that 'DocketClock core never reads enrichment tags for reconciliation logic.' This creates a bootstrapping problem at MVP: until Watershed Watch deploys its geo-enrichment worker and tags are populated, Watchlist filtering by huc_8: returns zero results. There is no fallback or stub enrichment for the design-partner onboarding period. Law firm design partners filtering by cfr: tags face the same gap until the built-in CFR part enrichment is shipped and validated.
weaknesses.why: Week 8 onboards design partners, but Week 9-10 is when the enrichment API is built. Design partners in week 8 have no tag-based filtering. The delivery schedule has the partners arriving before the filtering they need exists.

weaknesses.issue: The proposal states the Observations table is 'append-only — no updates, no UPDATE or DELETE grants.' But the Regulations.gov commentEndDate and openForComment fields are explicitly agency-configurable and can change at any time. The Observation for a given source document captures the state at fetch_at. There is no mechanism specified for detecting when an agency DECREASES a deadline (moves a comment period earlier) versus extends it. The reconciler always takes the 'most-recent operative deadline' but this assumes chronological monotonicity — in practice agencies can roll back dates or correct errors, and the most-recent observation may not be the operative one. A correction notice (is_correction_notice=true) should trigger a re-evaluation of the entire chain, but the proposal does not specify how the reconciler handles a new observation where extracted_closes_at is EARLIER than the previous resolved_closes_at.
weaknesses.why: The EPA example with original notice + extension + reopening is discussed, but reopening after an extension that was itself after the original close date creates non-monotonic chains. The reconciler logic for 'most-recent operative deadline' is underspecified for rollback cases.

weaknesses.issue: Multi-key API key pool for Regulations.gov rate limit mitigation is listed as a key risk mitigation, but api.data.gov's terms of service explicitly prohibit using multiple keys to exceed the rate limit for the same application/use case. The proposal frames this as 'multiple API keys registered to different addresses' — which is rate-limit circumvention. If GSA enforces this (they do for abusive patterns), DocketClock loses API access, which is an existential risk for the product. The correct mitigation is to apply for a rate limit increase from GSA (documented as available case-by-case) and to architect for the 1,000 req/hr budget.
weaknesses.why: This is not a hypothetical ToS risk — it is the kind of thing that gets API access revoked and was documented as a known enforcement mechanism. Building the core polling architecture around rate-limit circumvention is a liability the proposal should not carry.

weaknesses.issue: The MCP server is listed as a 'first-class deliverable for AI-agent buyers' to be shipped on day one alongside the REST API. But the MCP spec and tooling ecosystem are still evolving rapidly as of mid-2026, and the AI-agent buyer segment has not been validated. The proposal correctly identifies FiscalNote PolicyNote MCP as a comp, but FiscalNote is selling MCP as an extension of an existing full platform with thousands of customers — not as the primary revenue driver for a pre-revenue substrate. Shipping MCP as a Week 7 deliverable alongside webhook delivery (core functionality) adds operational surface area and maintenance burden before the primary buyer segments (law firms, reg affairs teams) have been onboarded. These buyers are not AI-agent builders.
weaknesses.why: The design partner targets are 2 law-firm current-awareness teams and 1 regulatory-affairs team — none of whom are the AI-agent MCP buyer. Shipping MCP in Week 7 consumes engineering time that the primary design partners don't need yet.

weaknesses.issue: The Fly.io + Upstash Redis + Supabase Postgres hosting stack has a known operational risk for an immutable observation log with legal-defensibility requirements: Supabase is built on Postgres but adds a managed abstraction layer that makes it harder to enforce the strict 'no UPDATE or DELETE grants on observations table' guarantees. Supabase's row-level security and role management can be configured correctly, but the platform's dashboard, migrations, and Studio UI create surface area for accidental mutations. For a product where the audit trail IS the trust primitive, the hosting choice should be evaluated against the risk of inadvertent observation table mutation.
weaknesses.why: This is an operational hygiene issue, not a fatal flaw. But for a product whose core value proposition is an immutable audit trail, the hosting choice should be explicitly hardened against mutation risk.

weaknesses.issue: The proposal drops 'named downstream user personas' from the architecture with the reasoning that naming pharmacy desks creates pressure to add pharmacy-specific fields. This is correct for the canonical object schema but overcorrects in the business context. The design-partner target is 2 law-firm teams and 1 regulatory-affairs team — but the proposal never specifies WHICH law firms, WHICH regulatory-affairs team, or what specific workflows they need the REST API to support. 'Law firm current-awareness' is a job-to-be-done label, not a design partner commitment. Without at least one named design partner with a specific workflow, the MVP scope is optimized for a hypothetical buyer rather than a real one.
weaknesses.why: The research notes that the design partner outreach has not yet happened. The architecture is ready for design partners, but the go-to-market plan assumes they will materialize at Week 8 onboarding. The risk is that law-firm current-awareness teams need specific features (redistribution rights, internal newsletter integration, Outlook calendar export) that are deferred to v1.1.

weaknesses.issue: The proposal's STALE confidence threshold is set at '>48h old for an open window — alert suppressed.' For a system polling every 15 minutes, a 48-hour stale threshold means the observation pipeline would have to completely fail for two full days before the staleness flag activates. This is too generous a window for near-deadline records. A window closing in 24 hours that has not been re-polled in 48 hours is extremely dangerous. The staleness threshold should be dynamic based on proximity to resolved_closes_at: windows closing within 72 hours should go STALE after 4 hours of no fresh observation.
weaknesses.why: A static 48-hour stale threshold is appropriate for windows closing in 30+ days but catastrophically wrong for windows closing in hours. The confidence model should include temporal proximity as an input to the STALE threshold.

missing: Concrete specification of how the reconciler handles non-monotonic deadline chains (agencies correcting a date downward or withdrawing an extension)
A bounded false-positive rate estimate or test plan for the is_extension_notice regex classifier before it goes into production
The concrete UTC-to-Eastern cursor overlap specification for the Regulations.gov differential polling strategy (how many hours of overlap, deduplication logic)
Rate limit increase application process as the primary mitigation for the Regulations.gov 1,000 req/hr constraint, replacing the ToS-violating multi-key pool approach
Dynamic STALE threshold logic based on time-to-deadline, not a fixed 48-hour clock
The many-to-many observation-to-window relationship for single FR notices that extend multiple dockets simultaneously
Named design partners (even under NDA) before Week 8 onboarding — the architecture is complete but the go-to-market assumption is unvalidated
An operational definition of HIGH confidence that specifies freshness SLA, source-agreement tolerance, and extension-chain lookback period as discrete measurable parameters
A clear go/no-go criterion for when to ship MCP vs defer it — the current framing ('ship on day one') is not justified by the design partner profile
critique.verdict:
This is a technically sophisticated architecture with a correct core thesis, a sound immutable-log foundation, and genuinely good calls on scope (BullMQ over Temporal, Postgres FTS over OpenSearch, federal-only). The extensibility contract via tags/enrichment-API is the right design pattern for a substrate product. However, there are several issues that need to be resolved before the architecture is production-ready.

The fatal flaw is the 24-hour agreement threshold in reconciliation rule (a): it will misclassify the systemic 1-day timezone-normalization artifact between FR and Regulations.gov (which is expected and documented) as CONFLICTING. This floods the human review queue with false positives, degrades the confidence signal, and destroys the product's core claim. The rule needs a separate code path for the known systemic 1-day offset versus genuine extension/correction-driven disagreements.

The four high-severity issues (confidence definition underspecified, multi-docket notice fan-out not modeled, lastModifiedDate cursor gap underspecified, multi-key rate-limit circumvention) are fixable in design before code is written — none require rearchitecting. The medium-severity issues (notice-type regex false positives for non-comment-period extensions, enrichment bootstrapping gap, rollback handling, MCP priority, staleness threshold) are real but manageable with targeted rule additions.

From the skeptical buyer perspective: the architecture correctly identifies the right B2B buyer (law firm KM, regulatory affairs), but the product value proposition — 'we show you when FR and Regulations.gov disagree' — is only compelling if the CONFLICTING signal is rare and actionable. If the system cries CONFLICTING on 40% of records due to routine timezone normalization, buyers will treat the confidence layer as noise. The entire revenue thesis depends on the CONFLICTING bucket being the meaningful minority.

The codex steal/drop decisions are largely sound. The biggest correct drop is the monolithic Normalizer — splitting it into Observation flags plus Reconciliation Engine plus Enrichment Hooks is the right decomposition. The biggest correct steal is the trust model (unknown/stale/conflicting as first-class states).

Score: 6.5/10. The bones are correct, the critical flaw is in the reconciliation rule specification, and the go-to-market assumption (design partners appear at Week 8) is unvalidated. Fix the reconciliation threshold logic and get at least one named design partner before starting the engineering sprint.

fit.rationale: Watershed Watch should rent DocketClock for the federal-rulemaking slice of its feed (Regulations.gov dockets + Federal Register notices scoped to a basin) and NOT reimplement reconciliation, but it cannot be "yes-fully" because its single most geo-bearing, deadline-bearing source — the EPA EIS database with its fixed 45-day-draft / 30-day-final clock — is explicitly NOT a DocketClock core source. The architecture lists window_type enum values eis_draft|eis_final ("Typed so EIS-clock verticals can layer on without core changes") but the source adapters are only FR API, Regs.gov v4, GovInfo (link-only), and Mirrulations (offline). So DocketClock gives Watershed Watch the schema slot for EIS but no EIS ingestion, no reconciliation of EIS notices, and no confidence flagging for them. That is the bulk of Watershed Watch's emotional value and it lives outside the substrate today. The honest read: build the federal-docket portion ON DocketClock via the OCD-ID + tag/enrichment contract exactly as architected; build the EPA EIS source, the geo-scoping, and Open States v3 as Watershed Watch's own pipeline. Hence partial. Going standalone would mean re-paying for reconciliation/confidence/provenance the architecture already solved well; going fully-on would require either pushing EIS into the substrate (which violates the federal-rulemaking-first, vertical-agnostic discipline) or pretending EIS doesn't matter (which guts the wedge). Partial is the truthful boundary.
fit.wedge_reasoning: Watershed Watch is a genuine instance of the vertical-wedge pattern, but it is a CONSERVATION/environmental wedge, not strictly a "land-use" wedge — and the dossier's self-critique ("just DocketClock + a Waterkeeper skin") must be confronted, not waved away. It is NOT just a skin IF and ONLY IF it earns three things the bare registry provably cannot: (1) Geo-recall — turning "this docket mentions the Choptank / HUC-8 02060005" into a first-class scoping decision. The architecture is explicit that "geo recall is Watershed Watch's problem, not the substrate's"; the substrate "never imports/queries WBD." So resolving basin membership from docket text is net-new vertical IP, not a re-skin. (2) The EPA EIS source + its fixed comment clock, which the substrate does not ingest at all. (3) The action/receipt loop — plain-language brief, structured-evidence comment composer that files to Regulations.gov, honest procedural receipts (filed-on-time, comment counts, draft-to-final diffs, never causal) — which the architecture explicitly classes as "vertical action layer, not substrate" and defers (Pol.is, comment submission, final-rule impact all deferred from core). Where the "skin" accusation has teeth: if Watershed Watch shipped ONLY basin-filtered federal dockets with no EIS source, no geo-recall LLM scoping, and no action loop — i.e. GET /windows?tags=huc_8:X wrapped in green branding — then it IS a skin and should not be funded as a separate product. The dossier's own residual-risk note is correct that the highest-emotion fights (local floodplain rezoning) are exactly what a federal-first substrate can LEAST surface, so the wedge must be honest that it covers federal/state environmental review well and local land-use poorly (NoticeNail community-signal, deferred). It qualifies as a land-use-adjacent wedge because EIS/NEPA review and basin-scoped permitting ARE land/water-use decisions; it is a real wedge because geo-recall + EIS + action are substantive additions; it is at RISK of being a skin precisely on its weakest (local) flank.

docketclock_provides: Reconciled federal ParticipationWindow objects (Regs.gov dockets + FR notices) with resolved_close_utc, resolved_close_display, status, version, change_history
Stable OCD-IDs as the cross-system join key Watershed Watch keys on (never internal UUIDs) so a basin watchlist survives extensions/reopenings

The confidence/conflict model (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/CONFLICTING/STALE/UNKNOWN) + typed conflict_flags + GET /conflicts proof feed — the honest 'do not publish fake certainty' layer Watershed Watch licenses sideways to newsrooms/nonprofits
Webhooks (HMAC, document-level docket fan-out, conflict-dual-fire, undelivered visibility), RSS/ICS/CSV delivery surfaces
govinfo_url + disclaimer on every record (legal-reliance backstop so Watershed Watch never claims to be authority)
window_type enum already including eis_draft|eis_final as the schema slot for EIS windows (slot only, not ingestion)
The opaque tags array + post-reconcile enrichment hook into which Watershed Watch writes huc_8:/huc_12: tags WITHOUT touching reconciliation
Extension/correction/reopening reconciliation so a basin alert reflects the OPERATIVE deadline, not a stale original
watershed_adds: Geo-scoping: USGS WBD (HUC-8/12) via The National Map ArcGIS REST as a VERTICAL-ONLY source; basin picker; resolve intersecting states/counties — the substrate is explicitly WBD-agnostic
Geo-recall: RuleBox-style LLM rules that decide whether a docket's text/CFR-parts/named water bodies fall in the chosen basin (the hard, net-new recall problem the substrate disclaims)

The EPA EIS database adapter + its fixed 45-day-draft / 30-day-final clock — NOT a DocketClock core source; Watershed Watch must ingest, normalize, and (ideally) push these through DocketClock's reconciliation contract or its own equivalent
Open States v3 state environmental legislation (state/local is a substrate thou-shalt-not until funded)

The action/receipt loop: plain-language basin brief, structured-evidence comment composer filing to Regulations.gov, honest procedural receipts (filed-on-time, comment counts, draft-to-final diffs — never causal)
Member-reported local long tail (NoticeNail photo-of-notice, parcel geocode) labeled 'community signal, not comprehensive' — DEFERRED past MVP
Coalition/channel distribution + business model: foundation/EJ grants fund free Tier-1, paid org seats to staffed land trusts/coalitions, the Chesapeake/Waterkeeper design-partner relationship
Pol.is consensus reserved for genuinely contested multi-faction fights (deferred)

requirements_on_docketclock: A third-party/vertical-writable enrichment hook registry with allowed_tag_namespaces — currently DEFERRED in the MVP boundary ('follows when Watershed Watch commits'). Watershed Watch's existence is the trigger to build it; until then only built-in agency/CFR enrichment exists.

Tag-based filtering on GET /windows (filter by tags including huc_8:/huc_12:) and tag-scoped webhook subscriptions/watchlists — so a basin watch fans out only to in-basin documents. The architecture lists tags as a /windows filter, which is sufficient IF webhook fan-out also honors tag filters (must be confirmed, not assumed).

An ingestion path or contract for EPA EIS notices to become first-class windows of window_type eis_draft|eis_final. This is the design-changing ask: either (a) DocketClock adds an EIS source adapter funded by the Watershed Watch pilot (consistent with its 'fund a specific source tranche' rule), or (b) DocketClock exposes a way for a vertical to PUSH externally-fetched notices into the observation log / enrichment so they get OCD-IDs, confidence, and reconciliation. Today neither exists — the enum slot is present but inert.
Agency-slug and CFR-part tags exposed (already built-in) so Watershed Watch's geo-recall can use agency (EPA/USACE/NPS/BLM) and CFR-part as cheap pre-filters before LLM basin scoping.

Confidence/conflict_flags surfaced in every API/webhook payload (already contract) so Watershed Watch can downgrade or suppress basin alerts honestly and so the sideways confidence feed to newsrooms is licensable.
resolved_close_display preserved verbatim (already designed) so basin briefs show legally-correct timezone/channel language, not a collapsed UTC stamp.

Net effect on substrate design: the vertical does NOT change core reconciliation, but it DOES pull forward two deferred items (third-party enrichment registry; an EIS ingestion contract) from 'later' to 'when Chesapeake commits' — and it stress-tests whether webhook fan-out respects tags, not just the REST filter.
fit.counterfactual_if_standalone: Build Watershed Watch standalone (not on DocketClock) when ANY of these hold. (1) DocketClock does not exist yet at pilot time and the user's real Chesapeake/Waterkeeper design-partner access creates a now-or-never window — the architecture itself orders Watershed Watch as a FAST-FOLLOW pursued only AFTER the first B2B substrate contract, so if a chapter commits before the substrate is live, a thin standalone (EIS + Regs.gov + FR fetched directly, basin-scoped, with the action loop) ships faster and the user should NOT block a funded pilot on substrate readiness. (2) The federal-docket portion turns out to be a small fraction of in-basin activity versus EPA EIS + state notices — i.e. most value comes from sources DocketClock does not carry — making the substrate dependency mostly overhead. (3) Reconciliation/confidence proves to matter little for the conservation persona: volunteer groups acting on a 45-day EIS clock may not need the extension/correction/timezone reconciliation that liability-bearing reg-affairs buyers do; if conflict cases are rare in environmental dockets (a Week-1-measurable question), the substrate's marquee value is muted for this vertical. (4) Coupling risk: if depending on a single-founder pre-revenue substrate would jeopardize a grant-funded commitment to a coalition, standalone de-risks delivery. The synthesis: the DEFAULT is build-on (partially) to avoid re-paying for reconciliation and to keep the OCD-ID join + sideways confidence-feed revenue; go standalone ONLY if design-partner timing outruns the substrate or if measurement shows federal-docket reconciliation is a minor share of basin value. Even standalone, preserve the OCD-Event output shape so it can later join back to DocketClock — never fork the data model, only the deployment.
risks: 'Waterkeeper skin' failure mode: if MVP ships only basin-filtered federal dockets without EIS ingestion, geo-recall, and the action loop, it is provably just GET /windows?tags=huc_8:X re-branded — and the dossier's own residual-risk note becomes the verdict. The wedge must lead with EIS + action + geo-recall, not federal-docket filtering.

EIS gap: the substrate does NOT ingest EPA EIS (only an inert window_type slot exists). Watershed Watch must build/fund this; if it instead leans on federal Regs.gov dockets alone, it misses the most basin-relevant, deadline-bearing stream and underwhelms exactly where the mission is strongest.

Geo-recall accuracy: deciding whether a docket falls in a HUC from text is hard; false negatives = missed fights (catastrophic for trust with a passionate base), false positives = noise/burnout. The keyword_false_positive conflict_flag exists in the substrate but basin scoping is the vertical's own unsolved recall problem.

Local long-tail mismatch: the most emotionally charged fights (local floodplain rezoning, parcel-level permits) are state/local — a substrate thou-shalt-not and a vertical NoticeNail deferral. Volunteers may judge the product by exactly the fights it can LEAST surface.

Ordering/coupling risk: architecture gates Watershed Watch behind the first B2B substrate contract; the user's live design-partner access may arrive first, forcing either a premature substrate dependency or a standalone fork — manage explicitly so a fundable pilot is not blocked.

Deferred-dependency risk: the third-party enrichment hook registry and tag-scoped webhook fan-out are not in the substrate MVP; Watershed Watch cannot ship cleanly until they exist, so committing a chapter pulls unplanned substrate work forward.

Causal-impact temptation: passionate users and grant funders want 'you changed this outcome'; the honest-receipts discipline (filed-on-time, comment counts, draft-to-final diffs only) must hold or the product loses the provenance credibility that is the whole house's moat.

Channel-economics risk: free grant-funded Tier-1 for volunteers + paid org seats must actually fund the EIS/geo pipeline; if grants are the only revenue, the vertical may not 'earn distribution the bare registry can't,' which is the bar the dossier sets for it to exist.

architecture.title: Watershed Watch: A Basin-Scoped Environmental Deadline-and-Action Radar (Thick Vertical, Selective Reuse of DocketClock)
architecture.one_liner: A HUC-scoped environmental participation radar for volunteer conservation coalitions that owns the three things the bare registry provably cannot — EPA EIS clock management, basin geo-recall, and an honest monitor→act→receipt loop — while renting DocketClock's reconciled federal-rulemaking windows (Regs.gov + FR) via the OCD-ID + tag/enrichment contract, never reimplementing reconciliation.
architecture.architecture_thesis: Watershed Watch is a THICK VERTICAL with SELECTIVE REUSE — the fit-analysis verdict ('yes-partially') is the correct and only honest layer boundary, and the thick proposal's 6.5 beats the thin proposal's 6.0 for one structural reason: the thin framing treats DocketClock as 'the feed and everything rides on it,' which makes the EIS gap and the deferred enrichment registry into existential single points of failure; the thick framing treats DocketClock as ONE upstream source among three (DocketClock-federal, EPA-EIS-owned, Open-States-owned), which is what the source topology actually is. The 'just a Waterkeeper skin' critique is answered not by branding or by listing three components, but by a single FALSIFIABLE anti-skin test run BEFORE the pilot commitment: 'Can we show the partner a quarter of in-basin EIS windows plus extension-corrected EIS deadlines that GET /windows?tags=huc_8:X cannot produce, AND a measured basin-recall number on a labeled Chesapeake set?' If that test fails, the product is a skin and should not ship. The architecture earns distribution the registry cannot in three ways the registry is structurally barred from: (1) EIS ingestion + an EIS-vs-FR extension reconciliation layer — the substrate is federal-rulemaking-first and vertical-agnostic by discipline, so it will never carry EIS; (2) geo-recall, turning 'this docket touches HUC-8 02060005' into a first-class scoping decision the substrate explicitly disclaims ('geo recall is the vertical's problem'); (3) the action/receipt loop the substrate classes as 'vertical action layer, not substrate' and defers entirely. But the synthesis also absorbs every critique that scored 'fatal' or 'high' and converts them into hard gates: the two unverified kill-shots (Regulations.gov POST /comments availability post-Aug-2025; EPA EIS machine-readability) become Week-1 one-day spikes with named fallbacks, NOT one-line gotchas; the EIS-vs-FR extension reconciliation becomes an explicit owned component (the thick proposal's single worst under-specification); the EIS action/receipt degradation (link-out vs Regs.gov submission ID) is surfaced honestly in the UI as a known second-class receipt; and the value-density question (expected novel in-basin Tier-1 windows/month for a Chesapeake HUC-8) becomes a measured pre-commitment number, because if it is 2-3/quarter the paid-seat thesis is dead regardless of how clean the architecture is. Federal/EIS coverage is honest and strong; local land-use (the most emotionally salient fights) is honestly weak and labeled so — the coverage-tier system is the load-bearing trust device, not a disclaimer footnote.
architecture.relationship_to_docketclock: Partial build-on, not full — and structured so Watershed Watch (WW) survives DocketClock not being live. WHAT WW CONSUMES FROM DOCKETCLOCK (rents, never reimplements): reconciled federal-rulemaking ParticipationWindows (Regs.gov dockets + FR notices) with resolved_close_utc + verbatim resolved_close_display, the full confidence/conflict model (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/CONFLICTING/STALE/UNKNOWN) + typed conflict_flags + the conflict-dual-fire webhook behavior, stable OCD-IDs as the durable cross-system join key (so a basin watch survives extensions/reopenings without WW tracking the chain), govinfo_url as the legal-reliance backstop, and built-in agency-slug + CFR-part tags used as cheap pre-filters before LLM basin scoping. WHAT WW OWNS (the anti-skin IP, never in the substrate): (a) the EPA EIS adapter + its 45/30-day statutory clock AND its EIS-vs-FR extension reconciliation — DocketClock carries only an inert window_type=eis_draft|eis_final enum slot, no ingestion, no EIS reconciliation, no EIS confidence; (b) USGS WBD/HUC geo-scoping (substrate is WBD-agnostic by design and never imports/queries WBD); (c) the basin-relevance classifier (geo-recall — the substrate explicitly disclaims this as 'the vertical's problem'); (d) Open States v3 state environmental legislation (state/local is a substrate thou-shalt-not); (e) the monitor→act→receipt loop and coverage-tier labeling (substrate classes these as 'vertical action layer, not substrate'). THE SEAM: WW's enrichment worker writes huc_8:/huc_12: tags into DocketClock within an agreed allowed_tag_namespace; corrections to deadlines NEVER flow back through tags — they route through DocketClock's human_review observation path, preserving the substrate's audit invariant. TWO DEFERRED SUBSTRATE ITEMS WW PULLS FORWARD when a Chesapeake chapter commits (this is exactly the substrate's 'paying customer funds a specific tranche' trigger): the third-party/vertical-writable enrichment hook registry, and CONFIRMED tag-scoped webhook fan-out (the substrate lists tags as a /windows REST filter but does NOT confirm webhooks honor tag scopes — this must be verified, not assumed). DEGRADED MODE until those ship: WW polls GET /windows filtered by agency (EPA/USACE/NPS/BLM/NOAA) + CFR parts (33/40/43) and runs basin scoping client-side — functional but a pure-consumer mode with no write path, so the eventual cutover is a known second-build-of-the-enrichment-worker risk that is budgeted, not a surprise. STANDALONE CONTINGENCY: if a chapter commits before DocketClock has its first B2B customer, WW ships a thin standalone that fetches FR + Regs.gov directly and reimplements ONLY the minimum environmental-docket reconciliation (fetch both sources per docket, compare comments_close_on vs commentEndDate normalized to America/New_York, flag Eastern-day disagreement as CONFLICTING, poll FR for extension/correction by docket_id/RIN) — explicitly scoped to ~1 engineer-week, never allowed to grow into a parallel DocketClock — and emits OCD-Event-shaped records from day one so the federal adapter can be swapped for the DocketClock feed later without touching the EIS pipeline, geo-classifier, or action loop. Never block a fundable pilot on substrate readiness; never fork the canonical object schema, only the deployment.

components.name: Basin Picker + HUC Resolver
components.responsibility: One-time per-org setup: user selects a watershed by name search or map click; resolves to HUC-8 (primary monitoring unit) and optional HUC-12 codes; enumerates intersecting state FIPS, county FIPS, named tributaries/water bodies, and EPA Region. Outputs a versioned basin_profile (huc_8[], huc_12[], state_fips[], county_fips[], named_waters[], epa_region, wbd_version) that every downstream filter consumes. The ONLY component that touches USGS WBD — nothing else in the stack queries The National Map.
components.tech: TypeScript service calling The National Map ArcGIS REST WBD Feature Service; PostGIS for HUC polygon storage + ST_Intersects basin membership; MapLibre GL (no Mapbox key) for the picker UI; basin_profile cached in Postgres after first resolution.
components.notes: Cache aggressively — WBD changes rarely. wbd_version field detects boundary revisions (last major: WBD 2.x) so a re-scope is explicit, not silent.

components.name: EPA EIS Adapter + Clock + Extension-Reconciliation Engine
components.responsibility: THE primary anti-skin component and the first thing built. Ingests EPA EIS draft/final records; applies the statutory clock (45-day Draft / 30-day Final under 40 CFR 1506.10), computing comment_close_date = clock_start + 45/30. CRITICAL ADDITION over the thick proposal: an explicit EIS-vs-FR reconciliation sub-layer — EIS comment-period extensions/shortenings are frequently published in the Federal Register, NOT updated in the EPA EIS system, so the engine watches FR (via the DocketClock feed or the standalone FR adapter) for extension/correction notices linked by EIS project number / docket linkage / agency+title, and reconciles them against the EPA-system clock. Maintains its own append-only observation log (same DB-trigger pattern as DocketClock). Emits a CONFLICTING state when the EPA-system clock and an FR extension disagree, and uses FR publication date as the authoritative clock_start when the EPA notice date and FR date differ (the same Eastern-date artifact DocketClock handles).
components.tech: TypeScript; EPA EIS source polled every 4h; Postgres append-only observation table; RuleBox JSON rulebook for EIS edge cases (emergency-shortened clocks under 1506.10(d), late notices, extension chains) with Haiku only for ambiguous chains; OCD-ID namespace ocd-participation-window/eis/{epa_eis_number}.
components.notes: Reliability gated on the Week-1 EPA-EIS-machine-readability spike. If it is a scraper, this component ships with a health-check alert + manual-data-entry fallback path so the pilot never silently stops receiving EIS notices.

components.name: Basin Relevance Classifier (Geo-Recall Engine)
components.responsibility: Decides whether a federal docket or EIS record is in-basin — the hardest net-new IP and the central anti-skin argument. Two-pass: (1) cheap deterministic pre-filter using DocketClock agency/CFR-part tags + named-water-body substring/fuzzy match against basin_profile.named_waters[] + state/county intersection; (2) Haiku structured-JSON classification for pre-filter passers that are textually ambiguous, returning basin_relevance (confirmed|probable|possible|out-of-basin) + evidence (matched_water_bodies[], matched_cfr_parts[], classification_reason). Tuned toward RECALL — false negatives (missed fights) are catastrophic for this persona; false positives are mutable noise. Stores every (window, basin) classification for rulebook iteration. ADDRESSES the critique's symmetric false-negative trap: EIS records often describe impacts by county/project name, not named tributaries, so the pre-filter ALSO runs county_fips/state intersection and project-description matching, not just named_waters[], so county-described upstream projects still reach the Haiku pass.
components.tech: TypeScript; RuleBox JSON rulebook + Zod; Haiku for ambiguous cases only (est. <10% of all records); PostGIS for geometry-backed membership; classifications cached by (window_ocd_id, basin_huc_8). Must pass an offline precision/recall run on a labeled Chesapeake set BEFORE pilot go-live.
components.notes: 'Report a missed notice' UI link turns every member-caught false negative into a labeled regression example.

components.name: DocketClock Enrichment Worker (tag writer) / Degraded-Mode Poller
components.responsibility: When the substrate enrichment registry exists: writes huc_8:/huc_12: tags into DocketClock for windows the classifier confirms in-basin (idempotent; never touches reconciliation; corrections route via human_review observations). In degraded mode (registry not yet shipped): runs as a pure consumer — polls GET /windows by agency/CFR-part pre-filter, scopes basins client-side, manages its own change-detection. Explicitly a temporary state with a budgeted cutover.
components.tech: TypeScript; DocketClock enrichment REST endpoint + tenant API key when available; HMAC-verified webhook consumer; client-side dedupe in degraded mode.
components.notes: Second-build risk acknowledged: degraded mode has no write path, so adding the tag-write path at cutover is a planned, story-pointed task.

components.name: Open States v3 Adapter (State Env Legislation — Tier-2)
components.responsibility: Polls Open States v3 for state environmental legislation in basin intersecting states; extracts committee hearing/event dates; applies the Basin Relevance Classifier. HONEST CONSTRAINT enforced in code AND in the user-facing label: Open States covers LEGISLATION (bills, hearings, votes), NOT state-agency rulemaking comment periods (MDE/DEQ NPDES/stormwater/wetland permits — which is what most watershed fights actually are). Confidence defaults LOW with conflict_reason=state_bill_timeline_volatile.
components.tech: TypeScript; Open States v3 GraphQL (free-tier key); window_type hearing|comment; OCD-ID namespace ocd-participation-window/state/{ocd_bill_id}/{event_type}.
components.notes: Per critique: hearing dates are surfaced as INFORMATIONAL context with an explicit 'verify directly with the committee before acting' gating step before any reminder fires — a volunteer who takes a day off to testify must not be misled by a shifted date. No automated lead-time push for Tier-2 without opt-in.

components.name: Alert Engine + Notification Service
components.responsibility: Aggregates in-basin windows from three sources (DocketClock-federal, EIS adapter, Open States) into one basin event stream keyed on OCD-IDs; applies coverage-tier labels before surfacing; manages per-user/per-org watchlists; fires lead-time reminders (14d/7d/48h/same-day). Honors DocketClock conflict-dual-fire: on CONFLICTING/STALE, SUPPRESS closing-soon AND fire a needs-verification notification with both source observations + govinfo_url — suppression is never silence. Generates a plain-language basin brief per window.
components.tech: TypeScript; HMAC-verified DocketClock webhook consumer (idempotent); Postgres outbox + pg_cron for reminders (no BullMQ/Redis at MVP); Haiku for briefs (async, cached, off the hot path); React Email + Resend for digests; RSS/ICS per basin.
components.notes: WW has a consumer audience that expects email — this is a deliberate divergence from DocketClock's webhook-to-Zapier deferral.

components.name: Action + Receipt Loop
components.responsibility: Structured-evidence comment composer pre-filled with basin evidence (water body names, HUC codes, docket impact language from the brief). Files to Regulations.gov for eligible federal windows; for EIS windows, directs to the lead agency's stated NEPA submission channel (email/portal) from the EIS record. Emits HONEST procedural receipts ONLY: filed timestamp, Regs.gov submission ID (where applicable), period_open_at_filing (boolean vs resolved_close_utc), comment_count_at_filing (totalNumComments), and a draft-to-final linkage ('the agency addressed X topics in the final rule that draft comments raised') — NEVER 'your comment caused this.' SURFACES HONESTLY (per critique) that EIS submissions produce a SECOND-CLASS receipt (link-out, no submission ID, no verified-on-time) before the user clicks file — the exact window type the vertical exists for has the weakest receipt, and hiding that would be dishonest.
components.tech: TypeScript + React form; Regulations.gov commenting API (separate submitter key; 50/min, 500/hr) IF available — see top_risks; Postgres receipt table keyed on OCD-ID; govinfo_url on every receipt.
components.notes: Entire Regs.gov-filing path gated on the Week-1 POST /comments availability spike. If GSA has closed it to non-government submitters, the composer degrades to 'draft + copy-paste into portal with a manual filed-confirmation receipt' for ALL windows, not just EIS — and the value prop is re-evaluated, not silently shipped.

components.name: Coalition Admin + Org Dashboard
components.responsibility: Paid-seat surface for staffed land trusts/coalitions: in-basin windows by tier, comment-receipt history, aggregate member activity, basin coverage health (last successful EIS poll, DocketClock feed freshness, Open States sync), and grant-reporting CSV export (windows monitored, comments filed, procedural receipts). Auth-gated; not public.
components.tech: Next.js /admin route; Clerk org-based multi-tenancy; Postgres read replica; CSV export.
components.notes: Org admins are external paying customers needing grant exports — a Clerk-gated Next.js route, not a Retool internal tool.

components.name: Member-Reported Local Signal (NoticeNail) — DEFERRED
components.responsibility: Photo-of-posted-notice capture, parcel geocode to HUC-12, manual local-permit deadline entry. Labeled 'community signal, not comprehensive monitoring.' DEFERRED past MVP; described in the UI as a planned future feature so members understand local coverage is best-effort, addressing the local-long-tail mismatch honestly rather than over-promising.
components.tech: DEFERRED: mobile camera + OCR; parcel lookup (Regrid/Parcel Atlas); no state/local API normalization attempted.
components.notes: The exact fights volunteers care most about (local floodplain rezoning, parcel permits) are what this tier would cover and what the federal-first architecture can least surface — deferral is honest, not a capability claim.

geo_scoping.primitive: USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD), HUC-8 as the primary monitoring unit with HUC-12 as an optional finer scope, accessed via The National Map ArcGIS REST WBD Feature Service.
geo_scoping.how: At org setup (one-time, not per-query), a user selects their basin by name search or map click; the system queries WBD to resolve the chosen HUC to its polygon and to enumerate intersecting state FIPS, county FIPS, named tributaries/water bodies, and EPA Region. All of this is frozen into a versioned basin_profile stored in PostGIS. Downstream: state_fips[] scopes the Open States query; named_waters[] + county_fips[] + state_fips[] are the cheap deterministic pre-filter inputs to the Basin Relevance Classifier BEFORE any LLM call; epa_region pre-filters EIS records. The substrate never touches WBD — the only artifact DocketClock ever sees is the resulting huc_8:/huc_12: tag. HUC-8 is the right granularity: fine enough to be meaningful to a local coalition, coarse enough that federal dockets naming a basin or tributary system fall inside it. First basin: Chesapeake Bay (Choptank, Patuxent, Susquehanna, Potomac, James HUC-8 subbasins).
geo_scoping.gotchas: (1) The National Map ArcGIS REST is free/keyless but rate-limited and can be slow on complex polygon queries during interactive basin-picker sessions — cache basin_profile after first resolution; never call WBD on the alert hot path. (2) Geo-RECALL (does a docket MENTION/affect this basin?) is a text-classification problem, NOT a geo-query problem — do not conflate WBD point-in-polygon with relevance scoring; the classifier is where recall is won or lost. (3) Named-water matching alone causes BOTH false positives (a USACE 'Chesapeake Bay Harbor deepening' notice that is actually a Baltimore Harbor dredge outside the Choptank HUC-8 — the classifier sees 'Chesapeake Bay' and fires) AND false negatives (an EIS describing an upstream project by COUNTY/PROJECT name with no named tributary — the named_waters filter misses it entirely). Mitigate by running county/state intersection + project-description matching in the pre-filter, and by tuning toward recall with member 'report a missed notice' correction. (4) Large multi-state basins like Chesapeake (6 states, dozens of HUC-8 subbasins) produce a high irrelevant tail at the basin scale — scope alerting to the org's SPECIFIC HUC-8 subbasins, not the whole bay, or the volunteer org will distrust a 40%-irrelevant feed. (5) WBD boundaries are revised periodically; store wbd_version in basin_profile so a boundary change triggers an explicit re-scope rather than silent drift.

data_sources.role: Primary WW-exclusive source; the clearest anti-skin pillar. Draft/Final EIS records with publication dates drive the deterministic 45/30-day clock under 40 CFR 1506.10. Searchable by state/EPA region for cheap geographic pre-filtering. Where the most basin-emotionally-salient federal notices live (NEPA review for water infrastructure, wetlands fill, dam relicensing, pipeline crossings).
data_sources.gotchas: GO/NO-GO: the EPA EIS interface is a legacy JSF/Java HTML application with NO confirmed documented REST API — a one-day Week-1 spike must determine machine-readable endpoint vs scraper BEFORE any other EIS work. If scraper: ship with health-check + manual-entry fallback; expect breakage on EPA UI migrations. EIS clocks can be shortened by emergency procedure (1506.10(d)) or extended by notice. CRITICAL: EIS comment-period extensions are frequently published in the FEDERAL REGISTER, not updated in the EPA EIS system — so a naive publication_date+45 produces a WRONG deadline for exactly the high-stakes extended EIS; the EIS-vs-FR reconciliation sub-layer is mandatory, not optional. EPA notice date vs FR publication date can differ 1-2 days (Eastern-date artifact) — use FR date as authoritative clock_start when available.

data_sources.source: DocketClock REST API + webhooks (federal Regs.gov + FR windows, HUC-scoped)
