data_sources.role: Federated source for all federal-rulemaking windows (EPA NPDES/CWA rulemakings, USACE §404 general rules, NPS/BLM/NOAA water-adjacent actions) that surface on Regs.gov/FR. Provides reconciled deadlines + the full confidence/conflict model + OCD-IDs + conflict-dual-fire + govinfo_url so WW never reimplements FR↔Regs.gov reconciliation.
data_sources.gotchas: Third-party enrichment hook registry + confirmed tag-scoped webhook fan-out are DEFERRED in DocketClock MVP — must be pulled forward at pilot commitment or basin-scoped subscriptions cannot exist. Until then, degraded mode = poll GET /windows with agency/CFR-part pre-filter + client-side basin scoping (explicitly temporary; budget the cutover). USACE §404 individual permits are the highest-stakes Chesapeake windows but may NOT flow through Regs.gov/FR as standard notice-and-comment — confirm the USACE ingestion path; some are public-notice postings, not rulemaking dockets.

data_sources.source: USGS WBD via The National Map ArcGIS REST
data_sources.role: Geo primitive only — HUC-8/12 polygons, named tributaries, intersecting jurisdictions, EPA Region. Used only in Basin Picker + Classifier pre-filter; never touched by DocketClock core.
data_sources.gotchas: Free/keyless but rate-limited; cache basin_profile. Boundary revisions occur — version them. Membership-by-geometry is not membership-by-text-mention; do not conflate.

data_sources.role: Tier-2 state environmental LEGISLATION + committee hearing schedules for basin states. Honest gap: covers bills/hearings/votes, NOT state-agency rulemaking comment periods (MDE/DEQ permits), which is what most watershed fights actually are.
data_sources.gotchas: No normalized comment-deadline field; hearing dates inferred from volatile event records → LOW confidence by default, conflict_reason=state_bill_timeline_volatile. Coverage varies (MD/VA/PA good; WV thinner). Free-tier key + rate limits. Subject taxonomy varies by state — always run the classifier text pass, never rely on subject tags alone. USER-FACING LABEL must distinguish 'state legislative hearing' from 'state permit comment period (NOT covered)' — the thin proposal's critique flagged this confusion as immediate.

data_sources.role: The 'Act' step's filing path for eligible federal windows; returns a submission ID that becomes the honest receipt. Read-side monitoring comes via DocketClock, not a direct WW read key.
data_sources.gotchas: GO/NO-GO: prior-art scan indicates GSA may have closed the public POST /comments endpoint to non-government submitters (~Aug 2025) — a Week-1 spike must confirm CURRENT availability before building the composer. If closed: the entire action loop degrades to draft + manual copy-paste with a self-confirmed receipt, sharply reducing differentiation — re-evaluate, do not silently ship. If open: separate submitter key (50/min, 500/hr — NOT the read key); agency-configurable submitter fields (fall back to universal free-text); 24-48h to public posting (receipt shows 'submitted', not 'published').

data_sources.role: Community signal for local planning-board/newspaper/posted-stake notices; the local long tail the federal-first architecture cannot systematically surface.
data_sources.gotchas: No normalized cross-state local API; Legistar has no comment-deadline field; parcel permits = 50+ county schemas in the Chesapeake alone. Defer until a paying county/coalition funds a specific jurisdiction integration. Label 'community signal, not comprehensive' when shipped.

action_loop.what: The basin feed surfaces all in-basin windows from three sources (DocketClock-federal, EIS adapter, Open States) by coverage tier, each carrying window_type, basin_relevance (confirmed/probable/possible), days_remaining, the verbatim resolved_close_display (never a collapsed UTC string), confidence, and govinfo_url or the EPA EIS link as the authoritative source. Lead-time reminders fire at 14d/7d/48h/same-day for Tier-1. DocketClock CONFLICTING/STALE honors conflict-dual-fire: closing-soon is SUPPRESSED and a needs-verification notification fires instead with both source observations + govinfo_url — suppression is never silence. EIS-only windows (no cross-source) carry an explicit 'EIS clock — verify at EPA for amendments' label, and any FR-detected EIS extension flips the window to CONFLICTING with a side-by-side. Tier-2 state hearings are informational with an explicit 'verify with the committee before acting' gate before any reminder.

Each in-basin window gets a plain-language basin brief (Haiku, generated async at classification, regenerated on window.changed): what the rule/EIS proposes, why it matters to THIS basin (citing the classifier's matched_water_bodies/CFR-parts evidence), the operative deadline (verbatim display), how to file, and what evidence is most persuasive for this docket/EIS type. Brief is labeled with confidence and is explicitly NOT a legal opinion; links govinfo_url (federal) or the EPA EIS filing + lead-agency NEPA contact (EIS).

Comment composer prompts for the user's position + supporting evidence (water-quality data, site observations, scientific references, local knowledge) and pre-fills basin context (water body names, HUC codes, impact language from the brief). For eligible federal Regs.gov windows: structures the draft and files via the Regulations.gov submitter API IF the Week-1 spike confirms it is open; otherwise degrades to draft + copy-paste-into-portal with a manual filed-confirmation. For EIS windows: directs to the lead agency's stated NEPA submission channel (email/portal) — and the UI states UP FRONT that EIS filing produces a second-class link-out receipt, not a verified Regs.gov submission ID. Drafts saved locally before submission; org admins can see member drafts to coordinate a coalition response before individual filing.

On filing: receipt shows filed timestamp, Regs.gov submission ID (where applicable; absent for EIS link-outs — stated honestly), period_open_at_filing (boolean vs resolved_close_utc), comment_count_at_filing (totalNumComments), and — when a final rule links back — a draft-to-final linkage framed strictly as 'the agency addressed these topics in the final rule that draft comments raised', never 'your comment caused this.' Org dashboard aggregates receipts for grant reporting (windows monitored, comments filed this quarter, dockets participated in). The honest frame — 'you participated, on time, in a real federal process' — holds regardless of outcome and is the receipt the dossier requires. The sideways DocketClock confidence feed (in-basin CONFLICTING windows) is licensable to newsrooms/nonprofits as a separate read-only signal (deferred until DocketClock's public /conflicts feed is stable).

coverage_tiers.what: Federal EIS notices (EPA EIS database, 45/30 statutory clock with EIS-vs-FR extension reconciliation) and federal-rulemaking dockets (DocketClock: Regs.gov + FR, fully reconciled) that the classifier rates confirmed or probable for the org's specific HUC-8 subbasin(s). Deterministic, verifiable deadlines; DocketClock confidence HIGH/MEDIUM (or EIS clock with explicit amendment-uncertainty when an extension is unresolved). Automated lead-time reminders. Comment filing automated where Regs.gov submission is available.
coverage_tiers.labeling: Badge: 'Verified federal deadline' with DocketClock confidence (High/Medium) OR 'EIS 45/30-day clock'. govinfo_url or EPA EIS link on every record. Disclaimer on every record: 'Watershed Watch is not a legal authority — verify the official notice before relying on this deadline.' The promise is bounded and explicit: 'You will not miss a federal EIS or major rulemaking in your basin during its open comment window — we do NOT systematically monitor local permits.'

coverage_tiers.what: State environmental LEGISLATION hearing schedules (Open States v3) for basin states, and federal dockets the classifier rates 'possible' (passed pre-filter, low LLM confidence). State bill dates shift without notice; Open States coverage varies by state. Possible-tier federal windows may be tangential to the basin.
coverage_tiers.labeling: Badge: 'State bill — dates may change' or 'Possible basin relevance' at LOW confidence. EXPLICIT user-facing distinction (per critique): 'State LEGISLATIVE hearing — this is NOT a state permit comment period (MDE/DEQ permits are not covered).' No automated push for state windows; a 'hearings require direct verification' gating step fires before any reminder — a volunteer must not travel to testify on a stale date. Possible-tier federal windows collapsed by default.

coverage_tiers.what: Member-submitted local notices (planning-board postings, newspaper legal notices, posted-stake permits) via the NoticeNail pattern, geocoded to HUC-12. NOT verified by Watershed Watch — community intelligence, not systematic monitoring. Deferred; described in the UI as a planned future feature so members understand local coverage is member-dependent and best-effort, never a promise.
coverage_tiers.labeling: DEFERRED. When shipped: 'Reported by a member — not independently verified. Deadline may be inaccurate; confirm with your local agency.' No confidence model, no automated reminders, no filing integration. This is the honest answer to the local-long-tail mismatch: the most emotionally charged fights live here and the architecture cannot systematically surface them — say so plainly in onboarding, not after a disappointed member.

tech_stack.why: Shares Zod schema types with DocketClock for the OCD-ID contract and tag-namespace interface; schema-first validation on all source payloads.

PostGIS is the one genuine divergence from DocketClock's pure-relational Postgres and the correct tool for HUC polygon storage + ST_Intersects basin membership. Also hosts the EIS append-only observation log (DB-trigger pattern), basin_profile, receipts, and the alert outbox. Negligible ops burden on managed Postgres (Supabase supports PostGIS; Render managed does not — use Supabase or self-managed on Fly).
Same schema-first discipline as DocketClock; basin-feed, comment-submission, and receipt endpoints publish OpenAPI so coalition partners/funders can inspect the integration.

tech_stack.choice: The National Map ArcGIS REST (USGS WBD) + PostGIS
tech_stack.why: Free, national, authoritative; no bespoke boundary drawing, no third-party tile dependency. Cache basin_profile; ST_Intersects for fast membership.

Deterministic pre-filter at zero LLM cost for the majority; Haiku only for ambiguous classification and ambiguous EIS extension chains (<10% of records). Structured JSON output; regression-tested against a labeled Chesapeake set.

3-sentence volunteer-readable brief from DATES text + abstract; generated off the hot path, once per (window, basin), cached. (Sonnet is an option if brief quality proves load-bearing in the pilot — start with Haiku for cost.)
Same as DocketClock and for the same reason — at design-partner scale the outbox stays in the low thousands of rows. Revisit BullMQ/Temporal only past ~10k subscriptions.
WW has a consumer audience that expects email (a deliberate divergence from DocketClock's webhook-to-Zapier deferral); Resend handles SPF/DKIM/bounce without in-house mail infra.
Org-based multi-tenancy (org = coalition/land trust, many members), role-based access (member / org admin / WW operator), tier gating via metadata.

tech_stack.choice: Regulations.gov submitter API (if confirmed open) with a draft+copy-paste fallback
tech_stack.why: Honest contingency for the GSA-closed-the-endpoint risk; never assume availability.

tech_stack.choice: Render or Fly.io (API + workers) + managed Postgres/PostGIS
tech_stack.why: Zero-ops containers; ~$40-80/mo at design-partner phase.
No infra; mines Chesapeake-region extension/correction/withdrawal chains as labeled eval data for both the classifier and the EIS reconciliation rulebook; runs in CI.

in_scope: Basin Picker resolving the org's specific Chesapeake HUC-8 subbasin(s) via USGS WBD (scoped to the org's tributaries, NOT the whole bay)
EPA EIS Adapter + 45/30 clock + the EIS-vs-FR extension-reconciliation sub-layer (with the Week-1 machine-readability spike result determining API vs scraper-with-fallback)
Basin Relevance Classifier (deterministic pre-filter + Haiku), recall-tuned, with an offline precision/recall run on a labeled Chesapeake set BEFORE go-live

DocketClock integration in degraded mode: poll GET /windows by agency (EPA/USACE/NPS/BLM/NOAA) + CFR parts (33/40/43), client-side basin scoping, consuming reconciled deadlines + confidence + OCD-IDs + govinfo_url
Coverage-tier labeling (Tier-1 EIS + federal rulemaking; Tier-2 Open States for MD/VA/PA/WV with the legislation-vs-permit distinction in the label; no Tier-3 at launch)
Alert feed with lead-time reminders + conflict-dual-fire suppression
Plain-language basin briefs (Haiku, cached)
Comment composer + Regulations.gov filing (IF Week-1 spike confirms POST /comments open; else draft+copy-paste fallback) + EIS link-out with honest second-class receipt
Honest procedural receipts (filed-on-time, submission ID where applicable, comment_count_at_filing)
Org dashboard with aggregate grant-reporting CSV export
The falsifiable anti-skin test run BEFORE pilot commitment: a measured count of novel in-basin Tier-1 windows/quarter + a measured classifier recall number
deferred: NoticeNail / Tier-3 community local signal (labeled a planned future feature, not a promise)
Tag-scoped DocketClock webhook subscription + the enrichment-worker write path (pulled forward only when the substrate ships the enrichment registry; degraded polling until then)
Open States v3 to Sprint-2 depth / additional states beyond the core Chesapeake four
Pol.is consensus (reserved for genuinely contested multi-faction fights)
Mobile native app (responsive web + email digest first)
Draft-to-final diff in receipts (until DocketClock ships window.finalized linkage + FR preamble extraction)
Public accuracy dashboard (until EIS classifier recall is validated)
State comment auto-filing (no normalized state comment API; guided link-out only)
Sideways DocketClock confidence-feed license to newsrooms (until DocketClock's public /conflicts feed is stable and a newsroom commits)
SF Bay / Baykeeper second basin (until Chesapeake pilot is stable and onboarding is documented)
Stripe billing (pilot is grant-funded; the design partner IS the paying customer via a staffed chapter, billing infra in a later sprint)
mvp_boundary.first_partner: A STAFFED Chesapeake Bay region partner the user can reach through their personal network — a Chesapeake Bay Foundation regional office or a funded Waterkeeper affiliate (e.g. a Riverkeeper on the Choptank, Patuxent, Potomac, or James). It MUST be staffed, not purely volunteer, so the design partner IS the paying customer (paid org seats / grant-funded pilot), not a charity case — this is the channel-economics requirement that makes the wedge 'earn distribution the registry cannot.' Multi-state Chesapeake exercises cross-jurisdiction logic; dense federal EIS/NPDES activity gives value density; the user's network access makes the pilot reachable now. Named alternative if the Chesapeake partner is not ready: SF Bay via Baykeeper. The partner relationship carries a NEGOTIATED obligation: the partner contributes institutional knowledge to label a historical Chesapeake EIS/docket set for classifier recall validation — a contract term, not an assumption.

build_sequence.milestone: Week 1 — Three go/no-go spikes + value-density measurement (gate)
build_sequence.deliverable: (1) EPA EIS machine-readability spike: documented REST endpoint vs scraper — determines whether the EIS adapter is a 4h-poll service or a scraper-with-health-check-and-manual-fallback. (2) Regulations.gov POST /comments availability spike: confirm whether non-government submission is open post-Aug-2025 — determines whether the action loop files automatically or degrades to copy-paste. (3) DocketClock readiness check: is the substrate live with a B2B customer? If not, trigger the standalone contingency. (4) Value-density measurement: count novel in-basin Tier-1 windows/quarter for the target HUC-8 (split EIS vs Regs.gov) from history — if it is 2-3/quarter, the paid-seat thesis is reconsidered BEFORE building. The anti-skin test and these four answers gate all further work.

build_sequence.milestone: Week 2-3 — Geo + EIS spine (the anti-skin pillars first)
build_sequence.deliverable: Basin Picker + HUC Resolver (WBD → versioned basin_profile in PostGIS). EPA EIS Adapter with append-only observation log, 45/30 clock, and the EIS-vs-FR extension-reconciliation sub-layer. EIS OCD-ID generation. This deliberately builds the two hardest net-new components before any alert UI exists — per the anti-skin discipline.

Basin Relevance Classifier (RuleBox pre-filter + Haiku), with the offline precision/recall run on the partner-labeled Chesapeake set as a hard gate. DocketClock degraded-mode poller (agency/CFR pre-filter + client-side basin scoping) consuming reconciled deadlines + confidence + OCD-IDs. Open States v3 adapter (Tier-2).

Alert Engine (3-source aggregation, coverage-tier labels, lead-time reminders, conflict-dual-fire suppression, Resend digests). Haiku plain-language briefs. Action + Receipt Loop (Regs.gov filing if open, else fallback; EIS honest link-out receipt; procedural receipts). govinfo_url + disclaimer on every record.

Coalition admin dashboard with grant-reporting CSV export. Onboard the staffed Chesapeake partner: configure their specific HUC-8 subbasins, walk the coverage tiers explicitly (including the local-long-tail gap and the EIS second-class receipt) in the onboarding conversation BEFORE the first disappointed member. Run the anti-skin test live: show in-basin EIS windows + extension-corrected EIS deadlines the bare DocketClock API cannot produce.

build_sequence.milestone: Week 9-10 — Validate pilot success metrics + iterate
build_sequence.deliverable: Confirm the success metrics: partner staff catch >=2 in-basin EIS/federal windows they would have missed; >=5 member comments filed through the action loop with honest receipts; members correctly understand the tier boundaries. Every member-reported missed notice becomes a classifier regression test. Tune recall.

If DocketClock ships the enrichment registry, build the enrichment-worker write path and cut over from degraded polling to tag-scoped subscription. If running standalone, keep the OCD-Event output shape join-compatible. Convert the pilot to a paid case study (X comments on Y windows the org would have missed) to justify paid seats for the coalition's member chapters.

top_risks.risk: Regulations.gov POST /comments may be closed to non-government submitters (~Aug 2025), gutting the 'Act' step — the prior-art scan flags this as a potential kill-shot the thin proposal assumed away.
top_risks.mitigation: Week-1 go/no-go spike confirms current availability BEFORE building the composer. If closed, the action loop degrades to structured draft + copy-paste-into-portal with a self-confirmed receipt for ALL windows; the value prop is re-evaluated (the brief + geo-recall + EIS clock still stand) rather than silently shipped as broken.

top_risks.risk: EPA EIS database has no confirmed machine-readable API — if it is a fragile scraper, the first anti-skin pillar becomes infrastructure that breaks on EPA's next UI change.
top_risks.mitigation: Week-1 one-day spike: documented endpoint vs scraper. If scraper, ship the EIS adapter with a health-check alert + manual-data-entry fallback so the pilot never silently stops receiving EIS notices; monitor EPA ECHO/EnviroFacts for a structured endpoint; file a data request with EPA once the pilot has credibility.

top_risks.risk: EIS comment-period extensions are published in the Federal Register, not the EPA EIS system — a naive clock produces a WRONG deadline for exactly the high-stakes extended EIS, reproducing DocketClock's core problem at the vertical layer (the thick proposal's worst under-specification).
top_risks.mitigation: The EIS-vs-FR extension-reconciliation sub-layer is a first-class owned component, not a one-liner: watch FR (via the DocketClock feed / standalone FR adapter) for extension/correction notices linked by EIS project number / docket linkage / agency+title; reconcile against the EPA-system clock; emit CONFLICTING + dual-fire when they disagree; use FR date as authoritative clock_start. Regression-tested against historical Chesapeake EIS chains in DuckDB.

top_risks.risk: Geo-recall false negatives (missed in-basin fights the org never hears about) destroy trust with a passionate base; false positives at a large multi-state basin scale produce a 40%-irrelevant tail the org learns to distrust.
top_risks.mitigation: Tune toward recall (false positives are mutable noise; false negatives are catastrophic). Pre-filter runs named_waters + county/state intersection + project-description matching so county-described EIS still reach the Haiku pass. Scope alerting to the org's SPECIFIC HUC-8 subbasins, not the whole bay. Offline precision/recall run on a partner-labeled set is a go-live gate. 'Report a missed notice' turns every miss into a regression test.

top_risks.risk: 'Just a Waterkeeper skin' — even with three components shipped, value density may be too thin (2-3 novel in-basin Tier-1 windows/quarter) to justify paid org seats.
top_risks.mitigation: Make the anti-skin test FALSIFIABLE and run it BEFORE pilot commitment: measure novel in-basin Tier-1 windows/quarter (split EIS vs Regs.gov) from history AND classifier recall on a labeled set. Build the EIS adapter + classifier as the FIRST two components so the test can be run early. If value density fails the threshold, reconsider the wedge rather than ship a skin. The action loop + EIS-vs-FR reconciliation + geo-recall are net-new value the bare GET /windows?tags=huc_8:X cannot produce — that is the bar, and it is measured, not asserted.

top_risks.risk: Local long-tail mismatch — the most emotionally charged Chesapeake fights (local floodplain rezoning, county stormwater exemptions, parcel permits) are state/local and exactly what the federal-first architecture can LEAST surface; the org may value the tool for grant-reporting but not as core workflow, undermining paid-seat conversion.
top_risks.mitigation: Name the gap in the product UI and in the onboarding conversation from day one via the coverage-tier system ('Federal EIS and rulemaking only — we do not systematically monitor local permits'). NoticeNail is a labeled future feature, not a promise. Set the pilot success metric on Tier-1 fights the org would have missed, and confirm with the partner BEFORE sign-on that federal/EIS coverage is useful absent local coverage — if they say no, the wedge is wrong for them.

top_risks.risk: DocketClock's enrichment registry + tag-scoped webhook fan-out stay deferred too long, and the user's design-partner window opens before the substrate has a B2B customer — forcing a premature dependency or a fork.
top_risks.mitigation: Degraded-mode polling ships a working v0 with no substrate write path; budget the enrichment-worker write-path cutover as a known second-build task. If a chapter commits before DocketClock is live, ship the ~1-engineer-week standalone (FR+Regs.gov direct, minimum environmental-docket reconciliation only, OCD-Event output shape preserved) and swap to the DocketClock feed later without touching the EIS pipeline, classifier, or action loop. Never block a fundable pilot on substrate readiness; never fork the canonical object schema.

top_risks.risk: Causal-impact pressure from grant funders — boards will ask 'how many outcomes did you change?' and competing tools claim dubious causal influence; yielding destroys the provenance credibility that is the whole house's moat.
top_risks.mitigation: The receipt data model makes causal claims impossible by construction (records what happened, never attributes causation). Educate pilot partners that procedural participation receipts ('our coalition participated in X federal processes, filing Y comments during open periods') are genuinely fundable without unprovable causation. Draft-to-final linkage, when it ships, is framed as 'the agency addressed these topics', never 'because of you'. Enforced as a UI-copy review gate before every release.

open_questions: Is the Regulations.gov POST /comments endpoint currently open to non-government submitters (post-Aug-2025)? Binary kill-shot on the automated action loop — must be answered Week 1 before any composer work.
Does the EPA EIS database expose a machine-readable endpoint, or is ingestion a scraper? Determines EIS adapter reliability and the first anti-skin pillar's durability — one-day Week-1 spike.

What is the expected count of NOVEL (previously unknown to the org) in-basin Tier-1 windows per quarter for a specific Chesapeake HUC-8 subbasin, split EIS vs Regs.gov? Below ~a handful/quarter, the paid-seat thesis is speculative regardless of architecture quality — measure before committing.

What classifier recall can we achieve at HUC-8 granularity on a labeled Chesapeake set, and will the staffed partner contribute the institutional knowledge to build that labeled set (a contract term, not an assumption)?

What is the USACE §404 individual-permit ingestion path? These are the highest-stakes Chesapeake permits but may be public-notice postings, not Regs.gov/FR rulemaking dockets — the ingestion pattern is unspecified.
Will DocketClock be live with a B2B customer when the design-partner window opens, and does GSA grant a tag-scoped-webhook / enrichment-registry timeline — or does the standalone contingency trigger?

Does the staffed Chesapeake partner explicitly confirm that federal + EIS coverage is valuable to them even ABSENT local-permit coverage? If their highest-pain fights are all local, the wedge may be wrong for this partner.

proposal.stance: THIN CLIENT ON DOCKETCLOCK — Watershed Watch is a geo-filter + plain-language brief + action/receipt UI + coalition distribution channel that sits on top of DocketClock's federal-docket feed via the OCD-ID/tag contract. It builds its own EPA EIS adapter and its own basin-scoping layer; everything else it rents from the substrate. Its job is to earn distribution and funding that the bare registry provably cannot — not to re-implement reconciliation.
proposal.thesis: Watershed Watch is viable as a thin client on DocketClock for the federal-rulemaking slice, plus a purpose-built EIS adapter and geo-recall layer, plus an action/receipt loop — and it is only a real wedge (not a Waterkeeper-branded skin) if all three of those additions ship. The MVP is: basin picker (HUC-8 via USGS WBD) + DocketClock webhook subscription filtered by huc_8 tags + EPA EIS ingestion with fixed 45/30-day clocks + RuleBox-style basin-relevance classifier + plain-language brief generator + honest-receipt comment-filing workflow. Nothing more. The design-partner is a Chesapeake Bay region Waterkeeper affiliate or CBF chapter reachable through the user's personal network, and that relationship is the only thing that makes this sequence viable before the substrate reaches its first B2B contract.
proposal.relationship_to_docketclock: Watershed Watch is a tag-scoped vertical tenant on DocketClock. It consumes the DocketClock REST API and webhook stream filtered by huc_8:/huc_12: tags it writes via the enrichment API. It relies on DocketClock for: reconciled resolved_close_utc + resolved_close_display, stable OCD-IDs as the durable join key (survives extensions/reopenings without Watershed Watch tracking them), confidence/conflict_flags so basin alerts downgrade honestly, govinfo_url + disclaimer on every record, and the document-level docket fan-out webhook. It does NOT rely on DocketClock for: EPA EIS ingestion (substrate does not have it, only an inert window_type slot), geo-scoping (substrate is explicitly WBD-agnostic), basin-relevance classification of docket text (the substrate disclaims geo-recall as the vertical's problem), Open States v3 state legislation, or the action/receipt loop. The third-party enrichment hook registry and tag-scoped webhook fan-out are currently deferred in the DocketClock MVP boundary — a Chesapeake chapter committing to a pilot is the trigger that pulls them forward. If DocketClock is not yet live at the moment the design-partner window opens, Watershed Watch should ship a thin standalone (EIS + Regs.gov + FR fetched directly, basin-scoped, OCD-Event output shape preserved) rather than block a fundable pilot on substrate readiness.

components.name: Basin Picker + HUC Resolver
components.responsibility: User selects their watershed from a searchable map or name search. Resolves the chosen basin to one or more HUC-8 (and optionally HUC-12) codes and the intersecting states/counties. Writes huc_8: and huc_12: tags to the DocketClock enrichment API for each docket in scope. This is a one-time setup per org, not a per-query operation. The resolver is the only component that calls USGS WBD via The National Map ArcGIS REST — nowhere else in the stack touches WBD.
components.tech: Next.js route calling USGS National Map ArcGIS REST (https://hydro.nationalmap.gov/arcgis/rest/services/wbd/MapServer). Leaflet or MapLibre GL for the basin picker UI. Postgres stores (org_id, huc_codes[], intersecting_states[], display_name). No self-hosted geo stack — query The National Map at setup time and cache the HUC polygon centroid + bounding box.

components.name: DocketClock Tag-Enrichment Worker
components.responsibility: After DocketClock's built-in agency/CFR enrichment runs, Watershed Watch's enrichment worker receives a post-reconcile webhook for each new window and decides whether it is in-basin. It runs the basin-relevance classifier (see next component) and, if the window passes, writes huc_8:XXXXXXXX and huc_12:XXXXXXXXXXXX tags via the DocketClock enrichment API. Windows that fail relevance are not tagged; they do not appear in any basin watchlist. This worker is the implementation of what DocketClock calls the 'vertical separate deployment calling the enrichment API' — it is deployed by Watershed Watch, not DocketClock.
components.tech: TypeScript worker (same repo as Watershed Watch backend). Called by a DocketClock webhook (window.new + window.changed) or polled via GET /windows filtered by agency tags (EPA, USACE, NPS, BLM) as a cheap pre-filter before relevance scoring. Writes tags via POST /windows/{ocd_id}/tags with the Watershed Watch tenant API key.

components.name: Basin-Relevance Classifier (RuleBox-style)
components.responsibility: Decides whether a given federal docket or EIS notice is relevant to a specific HUC-8 basin. This is the hard geo-recall problem the substrate explicitly disclaims. First pass: cheap deterministic rules (CFR parts, agency slug, named water body keywords extracted from docket title/DATES text, state intersection). Second pass: for ambiguous cases, an LLM call (Haiku) with a structured basin-scoping prompt that returns yes/no/uncertain + cited evidence (named water body, geographic reference, CFR part) — never a free-form paragraph. Uncertain returns are surfaced in the review queue, not auto-suppressed. False negatives (missed fights) are the catastrophic failure mode for volunteer trust; the classifier should err toward inclusion and let the brief-quality step filter noise.
components.tech: JSON RuleBox (same pattern as DocketClock's reconciliation engine): versioned rule file checked into git, run deterministically, regression-tested against a labeled set of Chesapeake-region dockets. Haiku fallback for ambiguous cases. spicy-regs/Mirrulations Parquet (DuckDB) used offline to mine Chesapeake-region extension/correction/withdrawal chains as labeled eval data.

components.name: EPA EIS Adapter
components.responsibility: The single largest net-new backend component. Polls the EPA EIS database (https://cdxapps.epa.gov/cdx-enepa-II/public/action/eis/search) for draft and final EIS filings. Applies the fixed comment clock: 45-day comment period for draft EIS (NEPA), 30-day comment period for final EIS. Normalizes each EIS to a ParticipationWindow-shaped object with window_type=eis_draft or eis_final, computed close date (published_date + 45 or 30 days), submitting_agency, lead_agency, project_name, EPA_EIS_number, state, description. Pushes these to DocketClock via the ingestion contract (if DocketClock exposes a vertical-push path) or maintains them in Watershed Watch's own Postgres table with OCD-Event-compatible output shape. Applies the basin-relevance classifier using named water bodies, project state, county, and description text.
components.tech: TypeScript poller hitting the EPA EIS search endpoint (no official API key required; HTML scrape or the CDX SOAP/REST interface). Postgres table (eis_filings) with EPA_EIS_number as the stable key. Polled daily (EIS filings do not change minute-by-minute). If DocketClock does not yet have a vertical-push/ingestion contract, Watershed Watch maintains its own confidence model for EIS windows: HIGH if computed from published_date + fixed clock with no amendment noticed, LOW if an EIS amendment or notice of extension is detected in the text.

components.name: Basin Alert Feed + Watchlist Engine
components.responsibility: Aggregates in-basin windows from two sources: (1) DocketClock webhooks filtered by huc_8 tags — these carry the reconciled confidence, conflict_flags, and OCD-ID; (2) EIS adapter output scoped to the basin. Deduplicates on OCD-ID (federal dockets that appear in both Regs.gov and EPA EIS). Computes lead-time buckets (>30 days, 7-14 days, 72h) and fires reminders. Applies DocketClock's confidence rules honestly: CONFLICTING/STALE suppress closing-soon and fire a conflict-needs-verification alert instead. EIS-only windows (no DocketClock cross-source) carry a prominent 'EIS clock only — verify at EPA for extensions' label. Org admins configure their watchlist (which HUCs, which agencies, minimum confidence level).
components.tech: Postgres (alerts, watchlist_subscriptions, basin_windows view joining eis_filings + docketclock_windows tables). pg_cron for lead-time bucket checks every 6h. Webhook receiver (Fastify) for incoming DocketClock events. Push: email digest (Resend/Postmark) + optional Slack webhook configured per org.

components.name: Plain-Language Brief Generator
components.responsibility: For each in-basin window, generates a plain-language brief: what the rule/EIS proposes, why it matters to this basin, the operative deadline (verbatim resolved_close_display from DocketClock, never collapsed to UTC), how to file, and what evidence would be most persuasive. The brief is always labeled with the DocketClock confidence level and links the govinfo_url as the authoritative source. For EIS windows, links the EPA EIS filing directly. The generator uses Claude Sonnet (not Haiku — quality matters here) in a structured-output mode that separates: summary_sentence, what_changes, why_basin_relevant (citing the classifier's evidence), how_to_comment, deadline_display, confidence_label, sources[].
components.tech: Claude Sonnet via Anthropic API, called once per new in-basin window. Structured JSON output enforced via tool_use / response_format. Result stored in Postgres (briefs table, keyed on ocd_id + version). Brief is regenerated when the underlying window changes (DocketClock window.changed webhook). Cost: ~$0.01-0.03 per brief at Sonnet pricing; at ~50 new in-basin windows/month for a Chesapeake org, this is negligible.

components.name: Comment Composer + Filing Receipt
components.responsibility: Structured-evidence comment drafting that files to Regulations.gov. Prompts the user for their position and supporting evidence (water quality data, site observations, scientific references). Assembles a draft comment in the format Regulations.gov expects. Files via the Regulations.gov v4 comment submission API (POST /comments). On success, stores the honest procedural receipt: submitted_at timestamp, comment_tracking_number, docket_id, window_ocd_id, submitting_org, submitting_user. NEVER makes causal claims ('you changed this outcome'). The receipt UI shows: filed on time (yes/no relative to resolved_close_utc), comment tracking number (linkable), how many total comments were filed on this docket (fetched from GET /dockets/{docketId}/documents), and when the final rule is linked (via DocketClock window.finalized webhook) a link to the final rule — no diff claim, no impact claim.
components.tech: Regulations.gov v4 comment API (POST /comments, requires a separate submitter API key). React form. Postgres (comment_receipts table). Draft stored locally before submission so users do not lose work. Rate limit: Regs.gov commenting sub-API is 50/min and 500/hr — irrelevant at Watershed Watch's volunteer volume.

components.name: Coalition Channel UI + Org Seats
components.responsibility: The distribution layer. Org admins (paid seats at staffed land trusts/coalitions like CBF chapters) manage their basin configuration, see their members' filing receipts, and export comment counts for grant reporting. Free Tier-1 members (volunteer orgs) see alerts and briefs but not the org admin dashboard or the comment composer (filing requires a free personal account, not an org seat). The sideways confidence-feed license (for newsrooms/nonprofits who want a raw feed of in-basin CONFLICTING windows) is a separate API key with read-only GET /basin-conflicts access.
components.tech: Next.js app with Clerk auth. Org-based multi-tenancy (each org is one or more HUC-8 basins). Tier gating via Clerk metadata. Stripe for org seat billing. The confidence-feed license is a DocketClock API key scoped to the relevant agency/HUC tags, re-exported under a Watershed Watch wrapper.

proposal.geo_scoping: USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD) via The National Map ArcGIS REST (https://hydro.nationalmap.gov/arcgis/rest/services/wbd/MapServer). HUC-8 is the primary granularity for alerting (roughly county-to-multi-county scale, 2,300 in the US — manageable). HUC-12 is optional for orgs that want finer resolution. Basin picker resolves the chosen HUC to intersecting states and counties at setup time, which are used as cheap pre-filters (agency slug, state field in EIS filings, FR document comment_url jurisdiction) before the LLM basin-relevance classifier runs. The substrate is explicitly WBD-agnostic — core DocketClock never imports or queries WBD. The enrichment tag (huc_8:02060005 for the Choptank sub-basin of Chesapeake) is the ONLY artifact the substrate sees. Geo-recall accuracy (deciding from docket text whether a rule touches a specific HUC) is Watershed Watch's own unsolved problem and the primary source of missed-fight risk. The classifier errs toward inclusion; the brief-quality step does the final human filtering. The National Map ArcGIS REST is free, no key required, national coverage, and the authoritative federal hydrologic boundary source — no bespoke boundary drawing, no third-party tile service dependency.

data_sources.source: DocketClock API + Webhooks (federal dockets: Regs.gov + FR)
data_sources.role: Primary source for all federal rulemaking windows with reconciled deadlines, confidence, and conflict flags. Consumed via GET /windows?tags=huc_8:XXXXXXXX and window.new/window.changed/window.conflict webhooks. This covers EPA regulations, USACE permits published as federal rules, NPS/BLM/FWS actions, NOAA fisheries rules — anything that goes through Regs.gov/FR and is tagged as in-basin by Watershed Watch's enrichment worker. Provides the stable OCD-ID that survives extensions without Watershed Watch tracking the extension chain.
data_sources.gotchas: DocketClock's third-party enrichment hook registry and tag-scoped webhook fan-out are currently DEFERRED in the substrate MVP. A Chesapeake chapter committing to a pilot is the explicit trigger that pulls them forward. Until then, Watershed Watch polls GET /windows with agency pre-filter and runs basin-relevance itself. Tag-scoped webhook fan-out must be confirmed to honor tags in addition to the REST filter — the architecture lists tags as a /windows filter but does not explicitly confirm webhook fan-out respects tags.

data_sources.role: The primary basin-bearing, deadline-bearing source that DocketClock does NOT ingest. Every draft EIS triggers a 45-day public comment period; every final EIS triggers a 30-day period. These are fixed NEPA clocks, not FR/Regs.gov-published deadlines, so they require a separate adapter. EIS filings include lead agency, state, county, project name, description — the geo-scoping inputs. This is the source that makes Watershed Watch more than a DocketClock skin.
data_sources.gotchas: No official REST API with structured JSON output — the CDX interface is SOAP or HTML. Scrape the search results or use the CDX REST endpoint if available. EIS amendment notices (which can extend or shorten the comment period) do not update the original record — Watershed Watch must monitor for amendment notices as a separate EIS_number-linked document, mirroring exactly the same extension-chain problem DocketClock solves for federal rules. At MVP, treat any amendment as LOW confidence until manually verified. EIS filings are state-reported and lag the Federal Register sometimes by days.

data_sources.role: State bill tracking for the intersecting states of a basin (e.g. MD, VA, PA, DC, WV, NY for Chesapeake). Provides state-level environmental legislation comment periods and hearings. Genuinely important for the mission but NOT in the substrate (state/local is a DocketClock thou-shalt-not). Watershed Watch can call the Open States GraphQL API directly scoped to the basin's intersecting states.
data_sources.gotchas: Open States covers legislation, not state agency rulemaking comment periods — the comment-period fields on state rules are not normalized cross-state (Legistar has no comment-deadline field). This means Open States surfaces bill hearings and votes but NOT the analog of a federal Regs.gov comment window at the state level. Be honest about this gap in the coverage tiers: state rulemaking deadlines are Tier-3 best-effort, not Tier-1 automated. Open States API key required; free tier has rate limits.

data_sources.source: USGS National Map ArcGIS REST (WBD HUC-8/12)
data_sources.role: Basin geometry and HUC codes for the basin picker. Called once at org setup, not on every alert. Returns the HUC polygon, name, intersecting states, and HUC-8/12 codes that become the enrichment tags. Free, keyless, national coverage.
data_sources.gotchas: REST endpoint can be slow for complex polygon queries. Cache the HUC geometry + intersecting states at setup time in Postgres — do not call ArcGIS REST on every alert. Watershed Watch's geo-recall (whether a docket MENTIONS the basin) is a text-classification problem, not a geo-query problem — do not conflate the two.

data_sources.role: Filing comments on federal dockets as part of the action/receipt loop. POST /comments with the user's drafted comment text and their Regulations.gov-required metadata. Returns a comment tracking number that becomes the receipt. Read-only monitoring (GET /dockets, GET /documents, GET /comments for comment counts) is covered by the DocketClock API key; filing requires a SEPARATE submitter API key registered to Watershed Watch's organization.
data_sources.gotchas: Commenting sub-API rate limit: 50/min and 500/hr — irrelevant at volunteer organization volume but must not be confused with the monitoring key's 1,000/hr limit. Anonymous filing is allowed for individuals but org-authenticated filing is better for coalition credibility. Regulations.gov can take 24-48h to post a comment publicly — receipt should show 'submitted' not 'published'. Some dockets restrict comment submission to specific formats (PDF, Word) — the composer must check the docket's submission requirements.

action_loop.what: Basin watchlist receives DocketClock webhooks (huc_8-tagged federal dockets: reconciled deadline, confidence, conflict_flags, OCD-ID, govinfo_url) plus EIS adapter output (EPA EIS filings within the basin's states/counties, fixed-clock deadlines). Alert feed sorts by time-to-close and fires lead-time reminders at >30 days, 7-14 days, and 72h. CONFLICTING/STALE windows suppress closing-soon and fire a conflict-needs-verification alert instead (mirroring DocketClock's dual-fire rule). EIS-only windows carry an explicit 'EIS clock — verify at EPA for amendments' label. All windows show the verbatim resolved_close_display (never a collapsed UTC string), the confidence level, and the govinfo_url or EPA EIS link as the authoritative source.

Each in-basin window has a plain-language brief (generated by Claude Sonnet at window creation, regenerated on window.changed): what the rule/EIS proposes, why it matters to this specific basin (citing the classifier's named water body / CFR part evidence), the operative deadline, how to file, and what types of evidence are most persuasive for this docket type. Brief is labeled with confidence level. For EIS windows, links the EPA EIS filing and the lead agency's NEPA contact. Brief is NOT a legal opinion and the UI makes this explicit.

Comment composer prompts the user for their position and supporting evidence (water quality data, site photos, scientific references, local knowledge). Assembles a structured comment draft following the format Regulations.gov expects. User reviews and approves before filing. Files via Regulations.gov v4 comment API. For EIS windows (where Regulations.gov may not be the filing channel), provides the EPA NEPA comment email/portal link instead. Draft is saved locally before submission. Org admins can see drafts from their members to coordinate a coalition response before individual filing.

On successful filing: displays the comment tracking number (linkable to Regs.gov), the submitted_at timestamp, whether the comment was filed on time relative to resolved_close_utc, and the total public comment count on this docket (fetched from Regs.gov GET /dockets/{docketId}/documents/comments count). When DocketClock emits window.finalized (a final rule is linked), shows a link to the final rule with a label: 'Final rule published — review what changed.' No diff claim. No causal claim. No 'you changed this.' Org dashboard shows: total comments filed this year, dockets commented on, windows monitored — for grant reporting. The confidence feed licensed to newsrooms shows in-basin CONFLICTING windows as a separate signal: 'X dockets in the Chesapeake basin have conflicting deadlines this week.'

coverage_tiers.what: Federal rulemaking: EPA regulations, USACE Section 404/10 permits published as federal rules, NPS/BLM/FWS/NOAA actions in Regs.gov/Federal Register, scoped to the basin via huc_8 tags. EPA EIS draft and final notices (NEPA 45/30 clock), scoped by state/county/named water body. These sources have documented APIs or structured feeds. Deadlines are reconciled (for federal dockets, via DocketClock with honest confidence flags; for EIS, via the fixed NEPA clock with an explicit amendment-uncertainty label). Lead-time reminders are automated. Comment filing via Regulations.gov API is automated.
coverage_tiers.labeling: Shown to users as: 'Federal review — verified deadline.' Confidence level (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/CONFLICTING from DocketClock, or EIS clock) always displayed. govinfo_url or EPA EIS link always present.

coverage_tiers.what: State environmental legislation hearings via Open States v3 for the basin's intersecting states (e.g. MD, VA, PA, DC for Chesapeake). Bill hearings and floor votes — NOT state agency rulemaking comment periods (which are not normalized cross-state). Useful for tracking state Clean Water Act implementation bills, state permit reform, and state nutrient-trading legislation. Deadlines are hearing dates from Open States, not comment-period closes — the distinction is surfaced in the UI.
coverage_tiers.labeling: Shown to users as: 'State legislative hearing — dates from Open States, verify with state agency.' No automated filing; provides the committee contact and bill link.

coverage_tiers.what: Member-reported local notices: permit applications at the county/municipal level, posted notices at boat ramps or public buildings, local zoning variance hearings. Submitted by members via a simple form (title, date, link or photo, parcel address). Geocoded to HUC-12 via reverse geocode on the parcel address. NOT comprehensive — these are local fights the federal substrate cannot surface. Displayed with a prominent 'community-reported, not verified' label. DEFERRED past MVP — do not build this until at least one Tier-1 design-partner sprint is complete.
coverage_tiers.labeling: Shown to users as: 'Member-reported — not independently verified. Deadline may be inaccurate. Contact your local agency to confirm.' No automated reminders. No filing integration.

tech_stack.why: Fastest path to a usable coalition org UI with server components for the alert feed, client components for the comment composer. Clerk for auth (org-based multi-tenancy, tier gating via metadata).

tech_stack.choice: Next.js API routes (MVP) or Fastify (if worker/webhook volume warrants separation)
tech_stack.why: At Chesapeake pilot scale (one coalition org, ~50 windows/month), Next.js API routes are sufficient. Fastify upgrade path if multiple orgs onboard. Same Zod-schema discipline as DocketClock for payload validation.

Tables: orgs, basin_configs (huc_codes[], intersecting_states[]), docketclock_windows (local cache of tagged windows), eis_filings, basin_relevance_decisions, briefs, comment_receipts, alert_log. JSONB for DocketClock window payloads (no schema migration on every DocketClock field addition). GIN index on huc_codes array for basin scoping.

EPA EIS poll (daily), DocketClock window poll (every 15 min for the tagged subset — a fallback when webhook fan-out is not yet tag-scoped), lead-time reminder check (every 6h), brief regeneration on window.changed events. Same Postgres-outbox pattern as DocketClock — no BullMQ/Redis at MVP.

tech_stack.choice: Fastify endpoint (or Next.js API route) with HMAC-SHA256 verification matching DocketClock's signature scheme
tech_stack.why: Receives window.new, window.changed, window.conflict, window.finalized events from DocketClock. HMAC verification is mandatory — the webhook is the trust boundary.

tech_stack.choice: JSON RuleBox (versioned rule file) + Claude Haiku fallback
tech_stack.why: Same RuleBox pattern as DocketClock's reconciliation engine. Deterministic rules run on every window; Haiku adjudicates ambiguous cases. Regression-tested against labeled Chesapeake-region dockets mined from spicy-regs.

tech_stack.choice: Claude Sonnet via Anthropic API, structured JSON output via tool_use
tech_stack.why: Quality matters here — this is the user-facing value beyond a raw alert. Sonnet gives better named-water-body identification and more persuasive comment-strategy suggestions than Haiku. Structured output (summary_sentence, what_changes, why_basin_relevant, how_to_comment, deadline_display, confidence_label, sources[]) stored in Postgres and served statically — not generated on every page load.

tech_stack.choice: TypeScript + Playwright (headless) or direct CDX REST if available
tech_stack.why: The EPA CDX EIS interface does not publish a stable documented REST API. Playwright headless scrape of the search results is the reliable path at MVP. Daily poll is sufficient — EIS filings do not change minute-by-minute. Long-term: monitor EPA ECHO/EnviroFacts for a structured EIS endpoint.

Lead-time reminders and conflict-needs-verification alerts delivered by email to coalition members. Resend handles SPF/DKIM/bounce. No in-house email infra. Slack webhook as an optional per-org integration.
Org seat billing for staffed land trusts/coalitions. Free tier (Tier-1 volunteer access) gated by Clerk metadata without Stripe.

tech_stack.choice: MapLibre GL JS + USGS National Map ArcGIS REST
tech_stack.why: Basin picker map. No Mapbox dependency. USGS WBD tiles served free from The National Map. MapLibre is open-source and has no key requirement.
Zero-ops at pilot scale. Vercel handles the Next.js frontend and API routes; Render hosts the long-running EPA EIS poller and DocketClock webhook receiver. ~$30-60/mo total at design-partner scale.

proposal.mvp_scope: One design-partner org (Chesapeake Bay region Waterkeeper affiliate or CBF chapter, reached via the user's personal network). MVP delivers: (1) Basin picker: org configures one or more Chesapeake HUC-8 codes via the USGS WBD map; (2) DocketClock integration: Watershed Watch's enrichment worker tags relevant federal windows with huc_8 codes and subscribes to DocketClock webhooks (falling back to polling GET /windows by agency tag until the substrate's tag-scoped webhook fan-out ships); (3) EPA EIS adapter: daily poll of EPA EIS database for draft/final EIS filings in Chesapeake states, fixed 45/30-day clock, basin-relevance classifier applied; (4) Alert feed: combined view of tagged DocketClock federal windows + EPA EIS windows, sorted by time-to-close, with lead-time reminders at >30 days / 7-14 days / 72h; (5) Plain-language brief: Claude Sonnet-generated structured brief per in-basin window, labeled with confidence level and govinfo/EPA link; (6) Comment composer + receipt: Regulations.gov comment filing with honest procedural receipts (tracking number, filed-on-time flag, total comment count — no causal claims); (7) Coverage tier labels: every window shows its tier (Tier-1 automated / Tier-2 state legislative / Tier-3 community-reported) with honest language about what can and cannot be verified. DEFERRED from MVP: Tier-3 community reporting (NoticeNail), Open States v3 state legislation (add in sprint 2 after Tier-1 is validated), Pol.is consensus, the sideways confidence-feed license to newsrooms (add when the design partner's coalition has contacts at a regional news outlet), multi-org onboarding, Stripe billing (pilot is grant-funded free access).

key_risks.risk: 'Waterkeeper skin' failure mode: MVP ships only basin-filtered federal dockets without EIS ingestion, geo-recall, or the action loop — making it provably GET /windows?tags=huc_8:X in green branding.
key_risks.mitigation: EIS adapter, geo-recall classifier, and comment composer are all MVP scope — not deferred. If any one of these three is missing at the design-partner pilot, the product should not be positioned as a wedge. The MVP is small but complete on these three axes.

key_risks.risk: Geo-recall false negatives: the basin-relevance classifier misses a high-stakes fight (e.g. a USACE Chesapeake Bay dredging permit with an obscure CFR citation and no named water body in the title), destroying volunteer trust.
key_risks.mitigation: Classifier errs toward inclusion. Cheap pre-filter (agency slug: EPA, USACE, NPS, BLM, NOAA; state intersection) casts a wide net. LLM adjudicates ambiguous cases with evidence citation. Design-partner org reviews the first 30 days of classifier output and labels false negatives — every false negative becomes a regression test. The dossier's own note: false negatives are catastrophic, false positives are annoying — tune accordingly.

key_risks.risk: EPA EIS adapter fragility: no official REST API, scraping the CDX HTML interface, EIS amendment notices not linked to the original filing.
key_risks.mitigation: Playwright headless scrape is the pragmatic path at MVP. EIS amendment monitoring is a second daily query for amendment-type filings by the same EPA_EIS_number. Amendments cause the EIS window to drop to LOW confidence with an explicit 'amendment detected — verify deadline with lead agency' label. Monitor EPA ECHO/EnviroFacts for a structured endpoint; file a comment/data-request with EPA if the pilot gains credibility.

key_risks.risk: DocketClock's third-party enrichment hook registry and tag-scoped webhook fan-out are not in the substrate MVP — Watershed Watch cannot ship the cleanest version of the tag contract until they exist.
key_risks.mitigation: Watershed Watch uses the polling fallback (GET /windows filtered by agency tags + its own basin-relevance classifier) until the substrate ships the enrichment registry. The design-partner pilot commitment is the explicit trigger that pulls these substrate features forward — budget this work in the DocketClock sprint plan as a 'Watershed Watch fast-follow' item. This is a known dependency, not a surprise.

key_risks.risk: Local long-tail mismatch: the most emotionally charged Chesapeake fights (shoreline armoring permits, county stormwater exemptions, local floodplain rezoning) are state/local and are exactly what Tier-1 can LEAST surface.
key_risks.mitigation: Be brutally honest with the design-partner org about coverage tiers from day one. Tier-3 community reporting (NoticeNail) is labeled 'deferred' and framed as the mechanism for surfacing local fights that federal/EIS monitoring misses — not a promise for MVP. The pilot success metric is Tier-1 fights (federal rules + EIS) where the volunteer org filed a comment it would have missed — not comprehensive local coverage.

key_risks.risk: Ordering risk: the user's Chesapeake/Waterkeeper design-partner access may open before the DocketClock substrate has its first B2B contract, forcing a choice between blocking a fundable pilot and shipping a standalone fork.
key_risks.mitigation: If a chapter commits before DocketClock is live, ship a thin standalone: Regs.gov + FR fetched directly, EPA EIS adapter, basin-relevance classifier, plain-language brief, comment composer — all with OCD-Event-compatible output shape. This is faster than waiting. The standalone preserves the OCD-ID join key so it can snap onto DocketClock when the substrate is ready. Never block a funded coalition pilot on substrate readiness.

key_risks.risk: Causal-impact temptation: grant funders and passionate volunteers will push for 'you changed this rule' claims; yielding destroys the provenance credibility that is the whole house's moat.
key_risks.mitigation: Honest-receipts discipline is a hard product constraint enforced in the UI copy review before each release. The receipt shows: filed on time, tracking number, total comment count, link to final rule when published. The UI copy explicitly says 'We cannot claim your comment caused this outcome' — but it also shows the correlation ('You filed. The agency responded with X.') without asserting causation. This is the same discipline as the DocketClock 'do not publish fake certainty' rule applied to impact claims.

key_risks.risk: Channel-economics: free grant-funded Tier-1 for volunteers does not generate revenue; the paid org seat model depends on staffed coalitions converting, which requires the product to have demonstrated value to the volunteer base first.
key_risks.mitigation: The Chesapeake pilot org should be a staffed chapter (CBF regional office or a funded Waterkeeper affiliate), not a fully volunteer group — so the design partner IS the paying customer, not just a user. Grant funding covers the pilot; the pilot deliverable is a case study showing X comments filed on Y dockets the org would have missed, used to justify $Z/year in paid seats for the coalition's member chapters.

deferred: Tier-3 community reporting (NoticeNail photo-of-posted-notice, parcel geocode) — deferred past MVP; labeled 'community signal, not comprehensive'
Open States v3 state environmental legislation (Sprint 2 after Tier-1 is validated with the design partner)
Pol.is consensus for multi-faction contested fights — deferred until product has active multi-stakeholder usage
The sideways confidence-feed license to newsrooms/nonprofits — add when the design-partner coalition has a regional news contact and DocketClock's GET /conflicts feed is production-stable
Multi-org onboarding + Stripe billing — pilot is grant-funded free access; billing infrastructure in Sprint 3
NoticeNail local notice photo submission and geocoding — deferred past Tier-3 community reporting feature
Causal impact attribution (draft-to-final diffs, outcome tracking beyond a link to the final rule) — permanently deferred; honest receipts are the ceiling
Direct email/SMS to individual members (pilot uses org-admin-mediated distribution via existing coalition email lists)
Mobile app (responsive web is sufficient for the pilot; native app is post-product-market-fit)
State agency rulemaking comment periods (no normalized cross-state API; not in scope until a funded state tranche)

strengths: Honest coverage-tier labeling is genuinely rare in civic tech — the three-tier model (Tier-1 automated federal/EIS, Tier-2 best-effort state legislation, Tier-3 community-reported) with explicit language about what cannot be verified is a defensible anti-pattern against the usual civic-tech false-certainty trap.

The EIS adapter is a real net-new contribution. The 45/30-day fixed NEPA clock is structurally different from the FR/Regs.gov reconciliation problem and makes Watershed Watch more than a DocketClock filter. This is the strongest single architectural decision in the proposal.

The fallback-to-standalone clause ('if DocketClock is not yet live, ship a thin standalone with OCD-Event output shape preserved') prevents sequencing deadlock and is the correct pragmatic call given that a design-partner window may open before the substrate is ready.

The honest-receipts discipline is explicit and hard-coded — 'never causal claims' plus what the receipt actually shows (tracking number, filed-on-time flag, total comment count, link to final rule when published) is the right ceiling and avoids the 'you changed this' trap that would destroy credibility with both users and agency reviewers.

Classifier-errs-toward-inclusion as the explicit tuning direction for the basin-relevance classifier is architecturally correct for the trust model — false negatives (missed fights) are catastrophic; false positives (irrelevant alerts) are merely annoying.

The design-partner-as-paid-customer framing (a staffed CBF chapter or Waterkeeper affiliate, not a volunteer org) is commercially sound. It means the pilot IS the paying customer, not a charity case, and it produces a case study with quantifiable value for grant reporting.

Channel-led distribution (foundations, coalitions, land trusts as the acquisition path) is the correct answer to the civic-tech cold-start problem. It avoids direct-to-consumer acquisition where all known civic-tech products go to die.

DocketClock dependency is cleanly bounded — the proposal explicitly lists what Watershed Watch does NOT rely on the substrate for (EIS ingestion, geo-scoping, basin-relevance classification, Open States, action/receipt loop). This dependency hygiene is good architecture.

weaknesses.issue: The Regulations.gov comment submission API (POST /comments) may no longer be publicly available
weaknesses.why: The prior-art adversarial scan documented that GSA shut off the public comment-submission endpoint for non-government users in August 2025. The proposal's entire 'ACT' step in the action loop assumes POST /comments with a tracking number is available. If it is not, the comment composer degrades to 'draft text and manually paste into the portal,' which dramatically reduces differentiation vs. a simple alert email. The proposal lists the Regulations.gov v4 comment API as a dependency without flagging this as a known risk or confirming current availability. Before building this component, API access must be confirmed with GSA — not assumed.

weaknesses.issue: EPA EIS scraping via Playwright is fragile and likely to break on a 6-12 month horizon
weaknesses.why: The proposal correctly notes there is no official CDX REST API with structured JSON output and reaches for Playwright headless scraping. But this is a government CDX SOAP/HTML interface that can change without notice, has no versioning commitment, and carries no API key contract that would trigger a deprecation warning. Historical civic-tech scraper fleets rot at exactly this rate. The proposal gestures toward 'monitor EPA ECHO/EnviroFacts for a structured endpoint' but provides no fallback for when the scrape breaks in production. At a minimum, the EIS adapter needs a health-check alert and a manual-data-entry fallback path so the design-partner pilot does not silently stop receiving EIS notices.

weaknesses.issue: Basin-relevance classification against the BLM/extension/correction false-positive trap is understated
weaknesses.why: The mismatch-evidence research found a concrete BLM case (doc 2023-27468) where the title says 'Extension, Public Meetings and Correction' but the 'extension' refers to a public-land withdrawal term, NOT a comment-period extension. The proposal acknowledges keyword false positives but only in the DocketClock context. The same trap applies to the basin-relevance classifier: a USACE notice about 'Chesapeake Bay Harbor deepening' may actually be a Baltimore Harbor dredge project wholly outside a Choptank sub-basin HUC-8, but the classifier will see 'Chesapeake Bay' and fire. The classifier design — deterministic rules on agency slug + state intersection + named water body keywords — will produce high false-positive rates for any large basin (Chesapeake spans six states and dozens of HUC-8 sub-basins). The design-partner org will quickly learn to distrust alerts if 40% are irrelevant to their specific tributary. The proposal acknowledges this risk but the mitigation ('errs toward inclusion; brief-quality step does the human filtering') effectively moves the problem downstream rather than solving it. At the HUC-8 granularity, 'Chesapeake Bay' mentions in federal dockets will produce a large irrelevant tail that the brief-quality step cannot scalably manage for a volunteer org.

weaknesses.issue: The EIS amendment-extension chain problem is architecturally identical to the core DocketClock problem and is not actually solved
weaknesses.why: The proposal correctly notes that EIS amendment notices do not update the original EIS record and that Watershed Watch must monitor for amendment-type filings linked by EPA_EIS_number. But the mitigation — 'treat any amendment as LOW confidence until manually verified' — is exactly the same 'human review for the hard cases' solution that DocketClock applies to extension/reopening notices. For a volunteer watershed org, 'LOW confidence — verify at EPA for extensions' on an EIS that is the most important fight of the season is not a useful state. The proposal does not specify: who does the manual verification, how quickly, and through what workflow. This is a silent bottleneck that will surface in the first high-stakes pilot use.

weaknesses.issue: Open States v3 is deferred but its coverage gap is not honest about what is actually available for Tier-2
weaknesses.why: The proposal correctly notes Open States covers state legislation (bills, hearings, votes) but NOT state agency rulemaking comment periods. The consequence is that Tier-2 'state environmental legislation' is actually state legislative hearings — which are low-priority for most watershed groups whose fights are about state agency permits (NPDES, stormwater, wetland delineation) not legislation. In Maryland and Virginia, the most impactful state-level interventions for a Chesapeake Bay watershed group are state MDE and DEQ permit hearings and state rulemaking comment periods, which Open States does not surface. The proposal labels this honestly in the data sources section but the coverage-tier description at Tier-2 ('state environmental legislation hearings') will mislead the design-partner org into thinking state permit fights are covered. This distinction needs to be in the user-facing tier label, not just the technical notes.

weaknesses.issue: Local long-tail mismatch is worse than the proposal admits and is the core adoption/retention risk
weaknesses.why: The adversarial scan and the proposal's own risk section acknowledge that 'the most emotionally charged fights (local floodplain rezoning) are exactly the ones Tier-1 can LEAST surface.' But the proposal's mitigation ('be brutally honest from day one, Tier-3 community reporting deferred') underestimates the adoption damage. A Chesapeake watershed volunteer's FIRST question when evaluating this tool is 'will it catch the county stormwater exemption hearing that burned us last spring?' The answer is definitively no until Tier-3 ships and even then only if members manually submit notices. The design-partner pilot success metric ('comments filed on federal/EIS dockets the org would have missed') is the right technical validation, but it may not be the right commercial validation: if the org's highest-pain fights are local and the tool only helps with federal/EIS, the org may value it as a grant-reporting tool but not as a core workflow, which undermines the paid-seat conversion thesis.

weaknesses.issue: The tag-scoped webhook fan-out dependency on DocketClock is a deferred substrate feature that could block the cleanest architecture for an indeterminate period
weaknesses.why: The proposal correctly flags that the third-party enrichment hook registry and tag-scoped webhook fan-out are currently deferred in the DocketClock MVP. The fallback is polling GET /windows with agency pre-filter every 15 minutes. But a 15-minute polling loop against DocketClock's entire federal window set — even filtered by agency slug — will quickly hit the Regulations.gov rate limit (1,000 req/hr) if DocketClock's API is itself rate-limited at the source level, and will create duplicate-detection complexity that the tag-scoped webhook would have avoided. The proposal treats this as a known dependency but does not specify how long the polling fallback is viable before it becomes a maintenance burden.

weaknesses.issue: The 'Waterkeeper-branded skin' failure mode is correctly identified but the mitigation is tautological
weaknesses.why: The proposal says 'EIS adapter, geo-recall classifier, and comment composer are all MVP scope — not deferred' and that if any one is missing the product 'should not be positioned as a wedge.' This is correct but does not address the deeper risk: even with all three components shipped, the product may still be perceived as a skin if the EIS adapter surfaces only 2-3 relevant notices per quarter for a specific HUC-8 sub-basin (which is plausible — EIS filings are relatively rare for small tributaries), and the federal docket feed surfaces mostly national EPA rules that the org is already tracking through other channels. The net-new value at the HUC-12 / tributary scale may be too thin to justify paying org seats at $X/year, even with all three components working. The proposal does not estimate the expected volume of novel (previously unknown to the org) in-basin Tier-1 windows per month for a typical Chesapeake sub-basin org.

missing: Confirmation of Regulations.gov POST /comments API availability post-August 2025 — this is a binary kill shot on the action loop and must be verified before any work on the comment composer component

Expected volume estimate for novel in-basin Tier-1 windows per month for a specific Chesapeake HUC-8 sub-basin, broken down by source (Regs.gov federal dockets vs. EPA EIS) — without this number the value density proposition is unvalidated and the paid-seat conversion thesis is speculative
EIS adapter fallback path when the Playwright scrape breaks — a health-check alert and manual-data-entry path should be in the MVP spec, not deferred
Manual verification workflow for EIS LOW-confidence windows (amendment detected) — who does it, within what SLA, and what happens to lead-time reminders during unresolved LOW-confidence states

Distinction in the Tier-2 user-facing label between state legislation (Open States: hearings, votes) and state agency rulemaking (MDE/DEQ permits: NOT covered) — this confusion will surface immediately with any Chesapeake design-partner org

Quantified precision/recall target for the basin-relevance classifier at HUC-8 granularity, with at least one offline evaluation run against labeled Chesapeake-region dockets from the spicy-regs corpus before the design-partner pilot goes live

Explicit handling of the USACE Section 404 permit pathway — these are the highest-stakes Chesapeake basin permits (dredging, fill, wetland delineation) and they may not flow through Regulations.gov/FR in the same pattern as notice-and-comment rulemaking; the proposal lists USACE as in scope but does not specify the ingestion path
critique.verdict: Watershed Watch is architecturally sound as a thin client and the three additions (EIS adapter, geo-recall classifier, action/receipt loop) are correctly identified as the necessary conditions to avoid being a skin. The honest-receipts discipline, coverage-tier honesty, and channel-led distribution are all right. However, the proposal has one potentially fatal unverified dependency (Regulations.gov POST /comments availability), one structural weakness that will surface in the first pilot month (basin-relevance false positives at HUC-8 scale for a large basin like Chesapeake producing an irrelevant-alert tail the volunteer org will distrust), and one adoption risk that the proposal's own risk section understates (local long-tail mismatch will likely mean the org values the tool for grant-reporting not core workflow, which undermines paid-seat conversion). The proposal should be approved for a design-partner scoping call but NOT for sprint planning until: (1) Regulations.gov POST API availability is confirmed, (2) an offline classifier evaluation against labeled Chesapeake dockets establishes a baseline recall number, and (3) the design-partner org explicitly confirms that federal/EIS coverage is useful to them even absent local coverage. Score: 6/10 — stronger than most civic-tech proposals, weaker than it needs to be on the three specific points that will determine whether it converts from pilot to paid product.

proposal.stance: THICK VERTICAL WITH SELECTIVE REUSE — Watershed Watch owns EPA EIS ingestion, HUC/WBD geo-scoping, basin relevance classification, the action/receipt loop, and coverage-tier labeling as its own defensible service. DocketClock is one upstream source for federal-rulemaking windows (Regs.gov + FR reconciliation), consumed via its OCD-ID + tag/enrichment contract. The vertical is NOT a skin: it earns its existence through geo-recall, EIS-clock management, and honest procedural receipts — three things the bare registry provably cannot deliver.
proposal.thesis: Watershed Watch is a basin-scoped environmental deadline-and-action radar for volunteer conservation coalitions. It earns its right to exist as a separate product — not a DocketClock skin — by owning: (1) the EPA EIS source adapter with its fixed 45-day-draft/30-day-final comment clock, which DocketClock carries only as an inert window_type enum slot with no ingestion; (2) HUC-8/12 geo-scoping via USGS WBD with a RuleBox-style LLM basin-relevance classifier that decides whether a docket's text/CFR-parts/named water bodies fall inside the chosen basin — a hard recall problem the substrate explicitly disclaims; (3) the monitor→act→see-impact loop with structured-evidence comment composition and honest procedural receipts (filed-on-time, comment counts, draft-to-final diffs, never causal claims). DocketClock handles the reconciliation/confidence/provenance layer for federal-rulemaking windows so Watershed Watch does not reimplement it; Open States v3 and the EPA EIS pipeline are vertical-owned. The product is channel-led: foundation/EJ grants fund free Tier-1 for volunteers, paid org seats to staffed land trusts and coalitions; a sideways DocketClock confidence feed is licensed to nonprofits and newsrooms.
proposal.relationship_to_docketclock: Partial build-on, not full. Watershed Watch calls DocketClock's REST API and subscribes to tag-scoped webhooks for federal-rulemaking windows (Regs.gov dockets + FR notices scoped to huc_8:/huc_12: tags written by the Watershed Watch enrichment worker). It relies on DocketClock for: reconciled resolved_close_utc/resolved_close_display, the confidence/conflict model (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/CONFLICTING/STALE/UNKNOWN) + typed conflict_flags, stable OCD-IDs as the cross-system join key, the conflict-dual-fire webhook behavior, govinfo_url legal-reliance backstop, and the built-in agency/CFR-part tag enrichment used as cheap pre-filters before LLM basin scoping. It does NOT rely on DocketClock for: EPA EIS ingestion (not a substrate source), Open States v3 (substrate thou-shalt-not), geo-scoping/WBD (substrate is WBD-agnostic by design), the action/receipt loop, or coverage-tier labeling. Two deferred DocketClock items are pulled forward when a Chesapeake chapter commits: (a) the third-party/vertical-writable enrichment hook registry (currently deferred; needed so the WW enrichment worker can write huc_8:/huc_12: tags); (b) confirmed tag-scoped webhook fan-out (architecture lists tags as a /windows REST filter; webhook fan-out must also honor tag scopes — must be verified, not assumed). An EPA EIS ingestion contract is the design-changing ask: either DocketClock adds an EIS adapter funded by the WW pilot (consistent with its fund-a-specific-source-tranche rule), or DocketClock exposes a vertical-push path so externally fetched EIS notices can receive OCD-IDs, confidence scoring, and reconciliation. Until one of these exists, WW runs its own EIS pipeline and outputs OCD-Event-shaped records, preserving the join surface without requiring the substrate to absorb EIS.

components.name: Basin Picker + HUC Resolver
components.responsibility: Lets a user select their watershed by name or map click; resolves to one or more HUC-8/12 codes; uses USGS WBD via The National Map ArcGIS REST to enumerate intersecting states, counties, named water bodies, and EPA Region. Outputs a basin profile (huc_8[], huc_12[], state_fips[], county_fips[], named_waters[], epa_region) stored as the org/group's watching scope. This is Watershed Watch's first-class geo primitive — the substrate never touches it.
components.tech: TypeScript service calling The National Map ArcGIS Feature Service (https://hydrowfs.nationalmap.gov/arcgis/rest/services/wbd/MapServer); PostGIS for basin polygon storage and point-in-polygon intersection queries; Mapbox GL or Leaflet for the basin picker UI.

components.name: EPA EIS Adapter + Clock Engine
components.responsibility: Ingests the EPA EIS database (https://cdxnodengn.epa.gov/cdx-enepa-public/action/eis/search), parses draft and final EIS notice records, and applies the fixed statutory clock: 45-day public comment period for Draft EIS, 30-day for Final EIS (40 CFR 1506.10). Emits EIS participation window objects with window_type eis_draft or eis_final, computed comment_close_date (publication_date + 45 or 30 days), and the OCD-Event shape for join-back compatibility. Maintains its own append-only observation log for EIS records (same pattern as DocketClock, independently operated). Because the 45/30 clocks are statutory and unambiguous, the confidence model is simpler: HIGH when publication_date is confirmed, LOW when only a notice date is available without Federal Register verification.
