components.tech: TypeScript; EPA EIS search API polled every 4 hours; Postgres append-only observation table (same trigger pattern as DocketClock); RuleBox JSON rulebook for EIS-specific edge cases (emergency procedures shortening the clock under 40 CFR 1506.10(d), late notices); OCD-ID namespace: ocd-participation-window/eis/{epa_eis_number}.

components.name: Basin Relevance Classifier (Geo-Recall Engine)
components.responsibility: Decides whether a given federal docket or EIS record is relevant to a chosen basin. This is the hardest net-new IP in the vertical and the central anti-skin argument. Operates as a two-pass filter: (1) cheap keyword/CFR-part pre-filter using built-in DocketClock agency/CFR-part tags (EPA, USACE, NPS, BLM, FHWA as primary agencies; water-bearing CFR parts 33, 40, 43 as priority) plus named-water-body substring match against the basin profile's named_waters list; (2) RuleBox-style LLM classification (Claude Haiku structured JSON) for records that pass the pre-filter but where text/boundary membership is ambiguous. Outputs a basin_relevance score (confirmed | probable | possible | out-of-basin) with evidence (matched_water_bodies[], matched_cfr_parts[], classification_reason). False negatives (missed fights) are worse than false positives (noise) for this persona, so the threshold is tuned toward recall; users can mute topics. Every classification is stored so the rulebook can be iterated from labeled corrections.
components.tech: TypeScript; RuleBox JSON rulebook + Zod rule schema; Claude Haiku via Anthropic API for ambiguous cases only (estimated 20-30% of pre-filter passthrough, less than 10% of all records); named-water-body lookup against PostGIS basin geometry; classification results cached by (window_ocd_id, basin_huc_8) pair; regression-tested against a labeled set of known in-basin and out-of-basin dockets seeded from Chesapeake Bay region federal activity.

components.name: Open States v3 Adapter (State Environmental Legislation)
components.responsibility: Polls Open States v3 GraphQL API for state environmental legislation relevant to the basin's intersecting states (from the basin profile state_fips[]). Filters by subject tags (environment, water, conservation, wetlands, land use) and applies the Basin Relevance Classifier to further scope to the basin. Produces participation window objects with window_type hearing or comment for public comment and hearing periods on state bills. NOTE: Open States v3 has no normalized comment-deadline field — hearing dates and committee deadlines are extracted from bill event records, and confidence is LOW by default with a display note that state bill timelines shift frequently. This is a known coverage gap accepted honestly in Tier-2 labeling.
components.tech: TypeScript; Open States v3 GraphQL (https://openstates.org/graphql); Postgres; window_type: hearing | comment; confidence default: low with conflict_reason: state_bill_timeline_volatile; OCD-ID namespace: ocd-participation-window/state/{ocd_bill_id}/{event_type}.

components.name: Alert Engine + Notification Service
components.responsibility: Aggregates in-basin windows from DocketClock (via tag-scoped webhooks, huc_8:/huc_12: subscriptions), the EIS Adapter, and Open States into a unified basin event stream. Applies coverage-tier labels (Tier-1/Tier-2/Tier-3) before surfacing. Manages per-user and per-org follow/watch lists keyed on OCD-IDs; emits lead-time reminders (14-day, 7-day, 48-hour, same-day) suppressed when DocketClock confidence is CONFLICTING/STALE (dual-fire conflict notification instead, same as DocketClock contract). Computes plain-language basin brief for each alert using a Haiku call against the DATES text + docket summary. Delivers via email digest, push notification, and a basin feed (RSS/ICS).
components.tech: TypeScript; DocketClock webhook consumer (HMAC-verified, idempotent); Postgres outbox for internal alert fan-out; pg_cron for lead-time reminder scheduling; Claude Haiku for plain-language brief generation; React Email + Resend for digest; Expo push for mobile (if native app ships later); RSS/ICS per basin.

components.name: Action + Receipt Loop
components.responsibility: Structured-evidence comment composer that helps a user draft a comment tied to a specific basin window. Pre-fills with basin-relevant evidence (water body names, HUC codes, named impacts from the docket brief). Files comments to Regulations.gov Comment API for eligible federal windows. Emits honest procedural receipts upon submission: filed timestamp, Regulations.gov submission ID, comment period still open at time of filing (yes/no), comment count at time of filing (from Regulations.gov totalNumComments), draft-to-final diff when a final rule links back to a commented draft. NEVER claims causal impact (never says 'your comment changed the outcome'). Tracks receipt records per user/org keyed on OCD-ID so they survive across sessions.
components.tech: TypeScript + React form; Regulations.gov commenting API (50 req/min, 500/hr — only for authenticated submitters, separate from the read-only key); Postgres receipt table (ocd_id, user_id, submitted_at, regs_submission_id, period_open_at_filing, comment_count_at_filing, draft_final_link); govinfo_url embedded in every receipt.

components.name: DocketClock Enrichment Worker (tag writer)
components.responsibility: Separate lightweight service that calls the DocketClock enrichment API to write huc_8: and huc_12: tags for federal-rulemaking windows that the Basin Relevance Classifier has confirmed as in-basin. Operates within DocketClock's allowed_tag_namespaces (watershed_watch: or huc: namespace, agreed at pilot contract). This is the seam that makes tag-scoped DocketClock webhooks and GET /windows?tags=huc_8:X filtering work. Does not touch reconciliation — corrections still route through DocketClock's human_review observation path.
components.tech: TypeScript; calls DocketClock enrichment REST endpoint; tenant-API-key auth; idempotent (tag already present = no-op); runs as a post-classification hook after Basin Relevance Classifier emits confirmed/probable for a (window, basin) pair.

components.name: Member-Reported Local Signal (NoticeNail) — DEFERRED
components.responsibility: Photo-of-posted-notice capture (planning board postings, local newspaper notices), parcel geocode, manual entry of local permit comment deadlines. Labeled clearly as community-signal, not comprehensive monitoring. DEFERRED past MVP — described in the UI as a future feature so members understand local coverage is best-effort, not systematic. This prevents the product from over-promising on exactly the fights (local floodplain rezoning, parcel-level permits) it can least reliably surface.
components.tech: DEFERRED: mobile camera + Google Vision OCR for notice text; Regrid or Parcel Atlas for parcel lookup; no state/local API normalization attempted.

components.name: Coalition Admin + Org Dashboard
components.responsibility: Org-level dashboard for staffed land trusts and coalitions (the paid seat tier). Shows all in-basin windows by coverage tier, comment receipt history, member activity (how many members filed comments on each window, aggregate not individual), basin coverage health (last successful EIS poll, DocketClock feed freshness, Open States sync status). Exports for grant reporting (total windows monitored, total comments filed, procedural receipts). Read-only Postgres replica access for power users. NOT a public surface — auth-gated.
components.tech: Next.js admin route; Clerk auth; Postgres read replica; CSV export for grant reporting; no public Retool exposure.

proposal.geo_scoping: USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD) via The National Map ArcGIS REST Feature Service (https://hydrowfs.nationalmap.gov/arcgis/rest/services/wbd/MapServer) is the authoritative geo primitive. A user selects their basin by name search or map click on the basin picker UI; the system resolves to HUC-8 (subbasin, ~700-2,000 sq mi) as the primary monitoring unit, with HUC-12 (subwatershed) as the finer optional scope. HUC-8 is the correct granularity for Watershed Watch: fine enough to be meaningful for a local coalition but coarse enough that federal dockets mentioning a basin name or tributary system fall within it. The WBD query returns intersecting state FIPS codes, county FIPS codes, named tributaries and water bodies, and EPA Region number — all stored as the org's basin_profile used by every downstream filter. Intersecting states feed the Open States v3 query scope. The Basin Relevance Classifier uses named_waters[] from the basin profile as the first-pass keyword filter before LLM disambiguation. The substrate (DocketClock) is explicitly WBD-agnostic; geo-scoping is entirely a Watershed Watch concern resolved in the enrichment worker and the classifier. MVP first basin: Chesapeake Bay watershed, covering the Choptank, Patuxent, Susquehanna, Potomac, and James HUC-8 subbasins — selected because it is dense with federal/state EIS and NPDES permit activity, spans multiple states (exercising the cross-jurisdiction logic), and is the home territory of the Waterkeeper affiliate / Chesapeake Bay Foundation chapter the user can reach for a design-partner pilot. SF Bay (Baykeeper) is the named alternative if the Chesapeake partner is not ready.

data_sources.role: The primary Watershed Watch-exclusive source. Draft and Final EIS records with publication dates; the fixed statutory 45-day (draft) and 30-day (final) comment clocks under 40 CFR 1506.10 produce deterministic comment_close_date values. Searchable by state and EPA region, enabling cheap geographic pre-filtering before basin-relevance classification. This is where the most basin-emotionally-salient notices live (NEPA review for water infrastructure, wetlands fill, dam relicensing, pipeline crossings).
data_sources.gotchas: The EPA EIS search is an HTML/form interface with a limited machine-readable API layer — scraping may be required for some fields; validate the machine-readable endpoint before committing. The 45/30 clocks can be shortened by EPA under emergency procedures (40 CFR 1506.10(d)) or extended by notice — the EIS Adapter must watch for extension/correction notices in the EPA system the same way DocketClock watches FR extension notices. Publication date vs. Federal Register publication date can differ by 1-2 days (same timezone artifact as the FR-Regs.gov gap); use Federal Register publication date when available as the authoritative clock start.

data_sources.source: DocketClock REST API + webhooks (federal Regs.gov + FR windows, huc-scoped)
data_sources.role: Federated source for all federal-rulemaking windows (EPA NPDES permits, Clean Water Act rulemakings, USACE Section 404 permit general rules, NPS/BLM water-adjacent rulemakings) that surface on Regulations.gov or the Federal Register. Consumed via tag-scoped webhook subscription (huc_8:XXXXXXXX) and REST polling. Provides reconciled resolved_close_utc, the full confidence/conflict model, govinfo_url, OCD-IDs as stable join keys, and the conflict-dual-fire behavior so Watershed Watch does not have to reimplement FR-vs-Regs.gov reconciliation.
data_sources.gotchas: Third-party enrichment hook registry and confirmed tag-scoped webhook fan-out are deferred in DocketClock MVP — these must be explicitly pulled forward when a Chesapeake chapter commits to a pilot, or the basin-scoped webhook subscription cannot exist. Until the enrichment hook registry ships, the Watershed Watch enrichment worker cannot write huc_8: tags into DocketClock, and the integration degrades to polling GET /windows with an agency/CFR-part pre-filter and client-side basin scoping. This fallback must be explicitly tested and documented as a temporary state, not a permanent architecture.

data_sources.source: USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD) via The National Map ArcGIS REST
data_sources.role: Geo primitive only — provides HUC-8/12 polygon geometry, named tributaries, intersecting jurisdictions. Used exclusively in the Basin Picker and Basin Relevance Classifier. Never touched by DocketClock core.
data_sources.gotchas: The National Map ArcGIS REST is free and keyless but has rate limits that can be hit during basin-picker interactive sessions with many simultaneous users. Cache basin_profile records in Postgres after first resolution — the WBD does not change frequently. HUC boundary updates do occur (last major revision: WBD version 2.4, 2023); implement a versioned basin_profile with a wbd_version field so boundary changes can be detected and re-scoped.

data_sources.role: State environmental legislation and committee hearing schedules for the basin's intersecting states. Provides bill subject tags, sponsor info, hearing event records from which committee hearing deadlines are extracted. Honest coverage gap: Open States v3 has no normalized comment-deadline field — hearing dates come from event records that can shift on short notice. Labeled Tier-2 explicitly.
data_sources.gotchas: No normalized comment-deadline field — the adapter must infer public participation windows from committee hearing event records, which are unreliable and volatile. State bill timelines shift without notice; confidence is LOW by default with a conflict_reason field of state_bill_timeline_volatile. Some states are better covered than others in Open States (Maryland, Virginia, Pennsylvania — all Chesapeake states — have good coverage; West Virginia is thinner). The Open States v3 API requires an API key (free tier available). Subject tag taxonomy varies by state; do not rely on subjects alone, apply the Basin Relevance Classifier text pass.

data_sources.source: Regulations.gov v4 API (via DocketClock, not directly)
data_sources.role: Federal rulemaking comment windows consumed exclusively through DocketClock's reconciled output — Watershed Watch does NOT maintain its own Regulations.gov adapter. This avoids competing for the same 1,000 req/hr key budget and re-implementing reconciliation. The only exception: the comment-submission sub-API (50/min, 500/hr) for the Action loop, which Watershed Watch calls directly with its own API key because DocketClock is read-only by design.
data_sources.gotchas: Regulations.gov commenting API requires submitter authentication and has stricter rate limits than the read API (50 req/min, 500/hr). Watershed Watch needs its own data.gov API key for comment submission — separate from the read key used for DocketClock's feed. The submitter-field schema varies by docket (agency-configurable); the comment composer must handle schema variations gracefully and fall back to the universal free-text comment field.

data_sources.role: Community-signal for local planning board postings, newspaper legal notices, posted-on-a-stake subdivision permits. Labeled explicitly as not comprehensive. Deferred past MVP to avoid over-promising on the local long-tail (the exact fights volunteers care most about but that the federal-first architecture can least systematically surface).
data_sources.gotchas: No normalized cross-state API for local land-use decisions. Legistar has no comment-deadline field. Parcel-level permit tracking would require county assessor integrations (50 different schemas across the Chesapeake watershed alone). Defer until a paying county government or coalition funds a specific jurisdiction integration.

action_loop.what: The basin feed surfaces all in-basin participation windows by coverage tier with confidence/conflict indicators inherited from DocketClock (for federal-rulemaking windows) or computed by the EIS Adapter's own confidence model (for EIS windows). Lead-time reminders fire at 14-day, 7-day, 48-hour, and same-day thresholds. When DocketClock emits a CONFLICTING or STALE signal, the conflict-dual-fire contract is honored: the closing-soon reminder is suppressed and a needs-verification notification fires instead with both source observations and the govinfo_url. The feed shows window_type, coverage_tier, basin_relevance (confirmed/probable/possible), days_remaining, and a plain-language brief (Haiku-generated from DATES text + docket abstract). Users can follow individual windows (keyed on OCD-ID) or follow all Tier-1 windows in a basin automatically.

For eligible federal windows (openForComment=true from DocketClock, or within the EIS 45/30 clock), the comment composer pre-fills structured evidence: the water body names in the basin, HUC codes, named tributaries, docket-specific impact language from the plain-language brief. The user writes their comment; the composer structures it into sections aligned with the agency's docket questions where available (scraped from the docket abstract). On submit, Watershed Watch calls the Regulations.gov commenting API with the user's registered name and address. For EIS windows, the comment is directed to the lead agency's stated submission address (from the EIS record — often a NEPA project manager email or agency web portal, not Regulations.gov). State bill hearing comments are guided (link to state legislature testimony portal) but not auto-filed, because no normalized state comment API exists.

Honest procedural receipts only — no causal claims ever. Receipt record includes: filed-on-time (boolean: was the comment period still open when Watershed Watch submitted?), Regulations.gov submission ID (or EIS submission confirmation), comment period status at time of filing, comment_count_at_filing (totalNumComments from Regulations.gov at submission time), draft-to-final diff when a final rule cites the same docket (computed by diffing the preamble text of the draft vs final Federal Register notice, surfaced as 'the agency addressed X topics in the final rule that were raised in draft comments'). The org dashboard shows aggregate receipts (total in-basin comments filed this quarter, windows where Watershed Watch members commented) for grant reporting. NEVER: 'your comment caused this change,' 'the agency agreed with you,' or any outcome attribution. The honest framing — 'you participated, on time, in a real federal process' — is the receipt the dossier requires, and it holds regardless of whether the agency changed its position.

coverage_tiers.what: Federal EIS notices (EPA EIS database, fixed 45/30 clock) and federal-rulemaking dockets (DocketClock: Regulations.gov + Federal Register, fully reconciled) that the Basin Relevance Classifier rates confirmed or probable for the basin. Comment deadlines are deterministic and verifiable. DocketClock confidence HIGH or MEDIUM. Lead-time reminders fire automatically. This is the product's core promise: you will not miss a federal EIS or major rulemaking in your basin during its public comment window.
coverage_tiers.labeling: Badge: 'Verified federal deadline' with the DocketClock confidence indicator (High/Medium) or 'EIS 45-day clock' label. govinfo_url linked on every record. Disclaimer: 'Watershed Watch is not a legal authority — always verify the official notice before relying on this deadline.'

coverage_tiers.what: State environmental legislation hearing schedules (Open States v3) for the basin's intersecting states, and federal dockets that the Basin Relevance Classifier rates possible (passed the pre-filter but LLM confidence is lower). State bill hearing dates can shift without notice; Open States coverage varies by state. Federal dockets in the possible tier may be tangentially related to the basin. Both are shown but clearly downgraded.
coverage_tiers.labeling: Badge: 'State bill — dates may change' or 'Possible basin relevance.' Confidence shown as Low. Explicit UI note: 'State legislative timelines shift frequently. Verify directly with the committee before acting.' No automated lead-time push reminders for state windows — user must opt in explicitly. Federal possible-tier windows appear in the feed but are collapsed by default.

coverage_tiers.what: Member-submitted local notices (planning board postings, newspaper legal notices, posted-on-a-stake permits) entered via the NoticeNail pattern. These are not verified by Watershed Watch — they are community intelligence, not systematic monitoring. DEFERRED past MVP; described in the UI as a future feature so members understand local coverage is best-effort and member-dependent, not a promise.
coverage_tiers.labeling: DEFERRED. When shipped: Badge: 'Reported by a member — not independently verified.' Timestamp of report. Member's display name (with consent). No confidence model applied — these are raw community signals. Users can upvote/confirm to increase visibility. Explicitly not in the automated alert system.

tech_stack.why: Consistent with DocketClock's stack so the enrichment worker can share Zod schema types for the OCD-ID contract and the tag namespace interface. Zod schema-first for all source payloads.

PostGIS is required for HUC polygon storage and point-in-polygon basin intersection queries — this is the one place WW genuinely diverges from DocketClock's pure-relational Postgres. Also handles the EIS append-only observation log (same trigger pattern), basin_profile table, receipt records, and alert outbox. PostGIS adds negligible ops burden on top of standard Postgres.

Same as DocketClock, preserving schema-first discipline. The basin-scoped alert feed, comment submission endpoint, and receipt API all publish OpenAPI 3.1 specs so coalition partners and grant funders can inspect the integration.

tech_stack.choice: The National Map ArcGIS REST Feature Service (USGS WBD)
tech_stack.why: Free, national, authoritative, no bespoke boundary drawing. Cached basin_profile records in Postgres after first resolution to avoid re-querying for every classification. PostGIS ST_Intersects for fast point-in-polygon and polygon-in-polygon basin membership checks.

tech_stack.choice: RuleBox JSON rulebook + Claude Haiku via Anthropic API
tech_stack.why: Same RuleBox pattern as DocketClock's chain classifier. Cheap keyword/CFR-part/named-water pre-filter runs deterministically at zero LLM cost for the majority of records. Haiku only for ambiguous cases (estimated 20-30% of pre-filter passthrough). Structured JSON output: {basin_relevance, matched_water_bodies, matched_cfr_parts, classification_reason}. Regression-tested against a labeled Chesapeake-region dataset.
Converts DATES text + docket abstract into a volunteer-readable 3-sentence brief. Runs once per window per basin (cached). Not on the alert hot path — generated asynchronously after classification.

Same choice as DocketClock and for the same reason: at design-partner scale (hundreds of basin windows, tens of orgs) the outbox never exceeds thousands of rows. Lead-time reminder scheduling is a pg_cron job scanning windows by days_remaining. Revisit BullMQ only if notification fan-out exceeds 10k subscriptions.

Basin weekly digest for members. React Email gives type-safe templates; Resend handles SPF/DKIM/bounce without building mail infra. Not a DocketClock concern (DocketClock defers email to webhook-to-Zapier); WW has a consumer audience that expects email.

Org-level multi-tenancy (one org = one coalition/land trust, multiple member users). Clerk handles org switching, invitations, and role-based access (member vs org admin vs Watershed Watch operator). Consistent with DocketClock's internal console choice.

tech_stack.choice: Render or Fly.io (API + workers), managed Postgres with PostGIS
tech_stack.why: Zero-ops containers. PostGIS is available on managed Postgres providers (Supabase supports PostGIS; Render managed Postgres does not — use Supabase or self-managed on Fly). Approximately $40-80/month at design-partner phase.

Org admins need more than a Retool internal tool — they are external paying customers who need grant-reporting exports, member activity views, and basin coverage health status. A Clerk-gated Next.js route is the minimum viable org dashboard without building a full product management UI.

proposal.mvp_scope: MVP = the Chesapeake Bay design-partner pilot with a Waterkeeper affiliate or CBF chapter. Scope: (1) Basin Picker resolving Chesapeake HUC-8 subbasins (Choptank, Susquehanna, Potomac, Patuxent, James) via USGS WBD. (2) EPA EIS Adapter ingesting Chesapeake-region EIS records with 45/30 clock. (3) Basin Relevance Classifier (keyword pre-filter + Haiku) for both EIS and DocketClock-federated federal windows. (4) DocketClock integration: REST polling GET /windows filtered by agency (EPA, USACE, NPS, BLM) and CFR parts (33, 40, 43) as a temporary pre-filter until the tag-scoped webhook and enrichment hook registry ship from DocketClock. (5) Coverage-tier labeling (Tier-1 EIS + federal-rulemaking, Tier-2 Open States for MD/VA/PA/WV, no Tier-3 at launch). (6) Alert feed with lead-time reminders and conflict-dual-fire suppression. (7) Plain-language basin brief (Haiku-generated). (8) Comment composer + Regulations.gov submission for federal windows; guided link-out for state hearings. (9) Honest procedural receipts (filed-on-time, submission ID, comment_count_at_filing). (10) Org dashboard with aggregate receipt export for grant reporting. Deferred from MVP: tag-scoped DocketClock webhook (depends on DocketClock enrichment hook registry shipping; use REST polling fallback until then), NoticeNail local-signal tier, Pol.is consensus, mobile app, draft-to-final diff (requires final rule linkage to be built in DocketClock first), public accuracy dashboard. Success metrics for the pilot: the chapter's staff report catching at least 2 in-basin EIS or federal docket comment windows they would have missed otherwise; at least 5 member comments filed through the action loop with honest receipts; the coverage-tier labeling is legible enough that members correctly understand what is and is not systematically monitored.

key_risks.risk: Waterkeeper-skin failure: MVP ships only basin-filtered federal dockets with no EIS source, no geo-recall LLM classifier, and no action loop — reducing Watershed Watch to GET /windows?tags=huc_8:X in green branding.
key_risks.mitigation: Lead with EIS ingestion and the Basin Relevance Classifier as the first two shipped components before the alert UI exists. The anti-skin test is empirical: can you show a pilot-partner coalition a list of in-basin EIS windows that DocketClock's bare API cannot produce? If yes, the vertical is real. If no, stop and reconsider the architecture. The action loop (comment composer + receipts) is the second defensibility layer; it must ship in MVP, not be deferred.

key_risks.risk: Local-long-tail mismatch: the most emotionally charged fights for volunteer conservationists (local floodplain rezoning, parcel-level permits, planning board variances) are state/local — the substrate's thou-shalt-not and the vertical's NoticeNail deferral. Volunteers may judge the product by exactly the fights it can least surface, and churn when they realize the MVP cannot alert them about the subdivision going in behind their house.
key_risks.mitigation: Name this gap explicitly in the product UI from day one. The coverage-tier system is the mechanism: Tier-1 is labeled 'Federal EIS and rulemaking only — we do not systematically monitor local permits.' Set this expectation in the design-partner onboarding conversation and in the product's onboarding flow. Do not hide the gap behind vague 'comprehensive monitoring' language. The Tier-3 NoticeNail deferral should be mentioned as a planned future feature so members understand the roadmap, not as a current promise.

key_risks.risk: Geo-recall accuracy: the Basin Relevance Classifier produces false negatives (missed in-basin dockets) that the coalition never finds out about because they were never alerted. For a volunteer group that has missed a fight, a missed alert is worse than no product.
key_risks.mitigation: Tune the pre-filter threshold toward high recall (flag more for LLM review rather than fewer). Log every out-of-basin classification so it can be audited. Provide a 'report a missed notice' link in the product so members can flag dockets the classifier missed — these become labeled training examples for rulebook improvement. Track classifier recall empirically against a manually-curated set of known Chesapeake-region dockets from the last 2 years (seed this from the Waterkeeper pilot partner's institutional knowledge).

key_risks.risk: DocketClock enrichment hook registry stays deferred too long: the third-party enrichment hook registry and tag-scoped webhook fan-out are deferred in DocketClock MVP. If a Chesapeake chapter commits to a Watershed Watch pilot before these ship, the integration degrades to client-side REST polling with agency/CFR-part pre-filters — functional but not the clean tag-scoped subscription architecture.
key_risks.mitigation: Document the degraded-mode fallback explicitly (REST polling GET /windows with agency/CFR-part filters, client-side basin scoping) and ship it as a working if inelegant v0. When committing to the Chesapeake pilot, immediately coordinate with DocketClock to pull forward the enrichment hook registry as a funded feature of the pilot contract — this is exactly the 'paying customer funds a specific source tranche' trigger the architecture describes. Never block a fundable pilot on substrate readiness; run degraded mode and upgrade in place.

key_risks.risk: EIS statutory clock exceptions are missed: EPA can shorten the 45-day Draft EIS clock under emergency procedures (40 CFR 1506.10(d)), and extensions occur. If the EIS Adapter applies a naive publication_date + 45 formula without watching for extension/emergency notices, it produces incorrect deadlines — exactly the problem DocketClock solves for federal rulemaking, now Watershed Watch's own problem for EIS.
key_risks.mitigation: The EIS Adapter must maintain its own observation log and watch for extension/correction notices in the EPA EIS database (look for records with the same project number and a later effective date, or explicit 'extension of comment period' in the record title). Apply the same RuleBox pattern: deterministic rules for common cases, LLM adjudication for ambiguous chains. The confidence model for EIS must include a CONFLICTING state for cases where the original clock and an extension notice disagree. Regression-test against the Chesapeake region's historical EIS record.

key_risks.risk: Causal-impact temptation: grant funders and coalition boards will ask 'how many outcomes did you change?' The honest-receipts discipline (filed-on-time, comment counts, draft-to-final diffs only) must hold against organizational pressure to claim more.
key_risks.mitigation: The receipt data model is designed to make causal claims impossible: it records what happened (filed, when, how many others also filed, what the agency later published) but never attributes causation. Educate pilot partners in the onboarding conversation that procedural participation receipts are the honest metric — and that they are genuinely valuable for grant reporting ('our coalition participated in X federal processes last year, filing Y comments during open periods') without requiring unprovable causation claims. Draft-to-final diffs (when available) are the closest the product gets to impact evidence, and they are framed as 'the agency addressed these topics in the final rule' not 'the agency changed because of you.'

key_risks.risk: Ordering / standalone timing: the DocketClock architecture gates Watershed Watch as a fast-follow after the first B2B substrate contract. But the user's live Chesapeake/Waterkeeper design-partner access may arrive before the substrate has paying customers — creating pressure to either block a fundable pilot or fork to a standalone deployment.
key_risks.mitigation: If a Chesapeake chapter commits before DocketClock has its first B2B customer, build a thin standalone: EIS Adapter + Regulations.gov + Federal Register fetched directly (re-implementing only the minimum reconciliation needed for environmental dockets, not the full cross-source confidence engine). Output OCD-Event-shaped records from day one so the data model is join-compatible when DocketClock is ready. Never fork the canonical object schema — only fork the deployment. Plan an explicit migration path: once DocketClock has the enrichment hook registry and tag-scoped webhooks, swap the standalone federal adapter for the DocketClock feed without touching the action loop, geo-classifier, or EIS pipeline.

deferred: NoticeNail member-reported local signal (Tier-3) — deferred past MVP; labeled as planned future feature in product UI
Tag-scoped DocketClock webhook subscription (depends on DocketClock enrichment hook registry shipping; REST polling fallback used until then)
Pol.is consensus for multi-faction contested fights — deferred; reserved for genuinely contested multi-stakeholder scenarios
Mobile native app (Expo) — deferred; email digest + responsive web first
Draft-to-final diff in receipts — deferred until DocketClock ships window.finalized linkage and FR preamble text extraction
Public accuracy dashboard — deferred until EIS classifier recall is validated against the Chesapeake labeled dataset
State comment auto-filing — deferred; no normalized state comment API exists; guided link-out only at launch
Sideways DocketClock confidence feed licensed to nonprofits/newsrooms — deferred until DocketClock publishes its public GET /conflicts feed and a newsroom expresses interest
SF Bay / Baykeeper second basin — deferred until Chesapeake pilot is stable and org onboarding is documented

critique.target: Watershed Watch architecture proposal (THICK VERTICAL WITH SELECTIVE REUSE stance)
strengths: The anti-skin claim is substantially earned at the architectural level. The EPA EIS adapter with its own observation log and 45/30-day statutory clock is genuine net-new IP that DocketClock provably does not carry — it is the clearest anti-skin argument and the right first component to build.

The two-pass basin relevance classifier (cheap keyword/CFR-part/named-water pre-filter, then Haiku only for ambiguous cases) is the correct engineering posture: it limits LLM cost, stores every classification for rulebook iteration, and tunes toward recall over precision — which is the right tradeoff for a user who will churn if they miss a fight.

Coverage-tier labeling (Tier-1/2/3) with honest badges is the most mature trust-design decision in the document. The explicit 'Federal EIS and rulemaking only — we do not systematically monitor local permits' framing, forced into the onboarding flow, is the right antidote to the local-long-tail mismatch risk.

The honest procedural receipts model is technically correct and legally defensible. Storing filed-on-time (boolean), Regulations.gov submission ID, comment_count_at_filing, and draft-to-final diff — while explicitly never attributing causation — gives grant-reporting value without creating a liability claim the product cannot back.

The DocketClock degraded-mode fallback (REST polling with agency/CFR-part pre-filter until enrichment hook registry ships) is pragmatic and documented. Shipping a working-if-inelegant v0 and upgrading in place is the correct posture for not blocking a fundable pilot on substrate readiness.

The standalone timing risk mitigation (build thin standalone with OCD-Event-shaped records, swap DocketClock feed in later without touching action loop or EIS pipeline) is architecturally sound and the right contingency for the scenario where a Chesapeake partner commits before DocketClock has B2B customers.

PostGIS for HUC polygon storage and ST_Intersects for basin intersection is the right technical choice — it is the one genuine divergence from DocketClock's pure-relational Postgres and it is the correct tool for the job.

The Chesapeake Bay pilot selection is defensible: multi-state (exercising cross-jurisdiction logic), dense federal EIS and NPDES activity, organized funded coalition (CBF / Waterkeeper affiliate), and the user has direct access — these are the four conditions a design-partner pilot needs.

weaknesses.issue: Basin relevance classification false-negative risk is structurally underestimated for the EIS adapter
weaknesses.why: The BLM false-positive trap documented in the research (a notice titled 'Extension, Public Meetings and Correction for Segments of the Colorado' where 'extension' means a public-land withdrawal extension, not a comment-period extension) is cited in the proposal, but the symmetric false-negative trap is not adequately addressed for EIS records. EPA EIS records often describe upstream watershed impacts using county names, project names, and drainage basin terminology that do not match HUC-coded named_waters[] in the WBD basin profile. A pipeline project crossing the Choptank River headwaters may appear in the EPA EIS system under the project's county and state descriptor, not a named tributary that the named_waters[] match will catch. The keyword pre-filter will miss these — they will never reach the Haiku disambiguation pass. The labeled-dataset mitigation is correct but requires the Waterkeeper pilot partner to actually sit down and label historical EIS records, which is a design-partner obligation that must be negotiated explicitly, not assumed.

weaknesses.issue: The EPA EIS database is an HTML form interface with no confirmed machine-readable API
weaknesses.why: The proposal's own gotcha section acknowledges 'scraping may be required for some fields — validate the machine-readable endpoint before committing.' This is a fatal assumption left unverified. The cdxnodengn.epa.gov EPA EIS database (also accessible at cdxapps.epa.gov/cdx-enepa-public) is a legacy JSF/Java EE application that returns HTML table results. There is no official REST API documented in EPA's API catalog or the GSA developer portal. If this requires scraping, the EIS adapter is not a 4-hour-poll TypeScript service — it is a fragile scraper that will break when EPA migrates the application, changes table structure, or adds bot-detection. The entire EIS clock engine's reliability depends on this not being confirmed. This should have been validated before architecture was written; it is a go/no-go engineering question that should be answered in a one-day spike before any other EIS adapter work begins.

weaknesses.issue: The EIS statutory clock confidence model is simpler on paper than in practice, and the extension-watch mechanism is underspecified
weaknesses.why: The proposal says the EIS adapter watches for 'extension/correction notices in the EPA EIS database (look for records with the same project number and a later effective date, or explicit extension of comment period in the record title).' This is the same keyword-matching problem DocketClock has for FR extension notices — and the research documents that keyword matching on 'extension' alone produces false positives (the BLM land-withdrawal-extension case). More critically, EIS comment period extensions are frequently published in the Federal Register, not updated in the EPA EIS database. A coalition partner watching the EPA EIS system would see a 45-day clock ticking while the actual close date has been extended by FR notice. The proposal's reconciliation answer — 'use Federal Register publication date when available as the authoritative clock start' — is the right instinct but the reconciliation between EPA EIS records and FR extension notices is not architecturally specified. This is DocketClock's core reconciliation problem reproduced at the EIS layer with less infrastructure.

weaknesses.issue: The DocketClock enrichment hook registry and tag-scoped webhook are load-bearing deferred features with no committed timeline
weaknesses.why: The proposal documents this risk and the degraded-mode fallback correctly, but understates the operational impact of the fallback. In degraded mode, the watershed watch enrichment worker cannot write huc_8: tags into DocketClock, so the basin-scoped subscription cannot exist. The fallback is client-side REST polling with agency/CFR-part pre-filter and basin scoping done locally. This means: (1) Watershed Watch must poll DocketClock's GET /windows on a schedule, (2) pull all EPA/USACE/NPS/BLM dockets, (3) run basin scoping client-side, and (4) manage its own change-detection to avoid reprocessing. At the Chesapeake pilot scale this is manageable, but it also means the Watershed Watch enrichment worker has no write path into DocketClock — it is a pure consumer, not a bidirectional participant. When DocketClock's enrichment hook registry eventually ships, the cutover requires the enrichment worker to be retrofitted with a write path that was not built during the degraded phase. This is not impossible but it is a second-build-of-the-same-component risk that should be flagged honestly.

weaknesses.issue: The action loop's Regulations.gov filing path silently fails for the highest-stakes EIS windows
weaknesses.why: For EIS windows, the proposal says 'the comment is directed to the lead agency's stated submission address (from the EIS record — often a NEPA project manager email or agency web portal, not Regulations.gov).' This is accurate but the action loop's structured-evidence pre-fill, submission tracking, and receipt model are all designed around the Regulations.gov commenting API. For EIS comments — which are some of the highest-stakes windows a watershed group will encounter — the user is handed a link-out to an agency email or web portal. The structured-evidence composer, the Regulations.gov submission ID, the comment_count_at_filing receipt field, and the filed-on-time boolean all depend on a Regulations.gov submission. For EIS comments the receipt is degraded to 'we linked you out; we cannot verify you actually submitted or that the period was still open.' The proposal should be explicit that EIS windows produce a second-class action/receipt experience compared to Regs.gov-mediated federal rulemaking windows — and honest about whether this undermines the value proposition for the exact window type the vertical was built to surface.

weaknesses.issue: Open States v3 is labeled Tier-2 but the confidence model is not adequate for the worst-case scenario
weaknesses.why: The proposal correctly labels Open States state bill hearing dates as confidence LOW with conflict_reason state_bill_timeline_volatile, suppresses automated lead-time push reminders, and requires explicit opt-in. But there is a gap in the failure mode: what happens when a state committee hearing date shifts and Watershed Watch does not detect the change before a member travels to testify? The proposal says users 'must verify directly with the committee before acting' but this responsibility-offloading is adequate for a regulatory-intelligence professional and inadequate for a volunteer conservationist who took a day off work to testify. A stronger design would either (a) not surface Open States hearing dates as actionable at all — display only as informational context — or (b) build an explicit 'hearings require direct verification' gating step in the UI before any reminder fires, not just a badge label. The current design surfaces Tier-2 windows in the feed with a badge but still makes them visible and actionable enough that a motivated volunteer will act without verifying.

weaknesses.issue: The 'not a DocketClock skin' defense collapses if the EIS API turns out to be a scraper and the Chesapeake pilot partner cannot label a training set
weaknesses.why: The proposal's anti-skin claim rests on three pillars: (1) EPA EIS ingestion, (2) HUC/WBD geo-scoping with LLM classifier, and (3) the action/receipt loop. If pillar 1 is a fragile scraper that breaks on EPA's next UI update, and if pillar 2's classifier has poor recall because the labeled dataset could not be assembled (the Waterkeeper partner's institutional knowledge takes months to extract), then pillar 3 (comment composer + receipts) is the only remaining differentiator. A comment composer that pre-fills water body names and HUC codes over a Regulations.gov docket feed is a thin feature, not a defensible vertical. The anti-skin test proposed in the key risks section ('can you show a pilot-partner coalition a list of in-basin EIS windows that DocketClock's bare API cannot produce?') is the right empirical check, but it must be applied before the Chesapeake pilot commitment, not after. Building the EIS adapter and classifier as the first two shipped components (as the mitigation states) is correct — but the engineering spike on the EPA EIS API machine-readability must precede that commitment.

weaknesses.issue: Causal impact pressure from grant funders is understated as an organizational risk
weaknesses.why: The proposal addresses this under key risks and the receipt data model is correctly designed. But the mitigation underestimates the organizational dynamics. Foundation/EJ grant funders increasingly require 'theory of change' narratives and quantified impact metrics. A product that honestly says 'you participated, on time, in a real federal process' will be competing for renewal dollars against organizations that claim (however dubiously) 'our comments influenced 3 rulemakings this year.' The draft-to-final diff feature — when it ships — will be the closest the product comes to impact evidence, but it is deferred. In the interim, the grant reporting export (total windows monitored, total comments filed, procedural receipts) is the impact story, and that story is defensible but thin compared to what foundation program officers will have been sold by competing tools. This is not a product design failure but it is a go-to-market risk that requires proactive framing with pilot partners before they sign grant applications that set expectations the product cannot meet.

weaknesses.issue: The standalone timing contingency does not specify what minimum reconciliation to reimplement for federal dockets
weaknesses.why: If a Chesapeake chapter commits before DocketClock has B2B customers, the proposal says 'build a thin standalone: EIS Adapter + Regulations.gov + Federal Register fetched directly (re-implementing only the minimum reconciliation needed for environmental dockets, not the full cross-source confidence engine).' What is that minimum? The research documents that FR comments_close_on and Regs.gov commentEndDate can differ by a day on the same docket, that extension notices may update the FR record but not the Regs.gov document, and that the openForComment boolean can be stale. The minimum viable reconciliation for the standalone is: (a) fetch both FR and Regs.gov for each docket, (b) compare comments_close_on vs commentEndDate, (c) flag disagreement as CONFLICTING, (d) poll for extension/correction notices by docket ID in FR. This is not the full DocketClock engine but it is not a one-line GET either. The standalone path should be scoped to a specific engineering effort estimate so it does not grow into a parallel DocketClock.

critique.verdict:
The architecture is substantially better than a DocketClock skin. The EPA EIS adapter with its own statutory clock, the HUC/WBD geo-scoping, and the honest procedural receipt loop are all genuine net-new value. The coverage-tier honesty and the degraded-mode fallback discipline are the strongest trust-design decisions in the document.

The fatal unverified assumption is the EPA EIS database machine-readability. Everything else is architecturally sound or mitigatable. Before any Chesapeake pilot commitment, a one-day engineering spike must answer: does cdxnodengn.epa.gov expose a machine-readable endpoint, or is this a scraper? If it is a scraper, the EIS clock engine confidence model collapses and the first pillar of the anti-skin argument becomes fragile infrastructure. The 'validate the machine-readable endpoint before committing' note in the gotchas section is the most important sentence in the entire proposal and should be the first line item on the engineering backlog.

The second structural risk is that the EIS extension-watch mechanism reproduces DocketClock's core reconciliation problem at the EIS layer with less infrastructure. EIS comment period extensions are published in the Federal Register, not necessarily updated in the EPA EIS system. Without a reconciliation path between EPA EIS records and FR extension notices, the EIS clock engine will produce incorrect deadlines for exactly the high-stakes extended EIS windows where a wrong deadline has legal consequences — precisely the problem DocketClock was designed to solve for rulemaking windows.

From the perspective of a watershed group director: the Tier-1/2/3 coverage labeling and the NoticeNail deferral are the right trust-design choices, but they guarantee that the first pilot conversation will surface the local-long-tail gap as a disappointment. The federal-first architecture is correct for data quality reasons, but the emotional salience mismatch — volunteers most care about the subdivision going in behind their house, the product most reliably covers EPA NPDES general permits — must be addressed in the onboarding framing before the group director signs on, not after the first disappointed member asks why the product missed the local planning board variance.

Score: 6.5 out of 10. The architecture earns its existence as a vertical, not a skin. It loses points on the unverified EIS API assumption (a potentially fatal implementation risk treated as a one-line gotcha), the underspecified EIS extension reconciliation (reproducing DocketClock's hard problem at the vertical layer), and the action/receipt degradation for EIS windows (the most emotionally salient window type produces the weakest receipt). It gains points for coverage-tier honesty, the classifier recall posture, the degraded-mode fallback, and the standalone timing contingency.
missing: Engineering spike result on EPA EIS machine-readable API vs. scraper before any pilot commitment — this is go/no-go information, not a detail

Specified reconciliation contract between EPA EIS records and Federal Register extension notices — the EIS clock engine needs to watch for FR extension documents by EIS project number/docket linkage, not just EPA system records

Explicit degradation of the action/receipt loop for EIS windows vs. Regs.gov windows — volunteers need to understand before they click 'file comment' that EIS submissions produce a link-out receipt, not a Regulations.gov submission ID

Negotiated design-partner obligations from the Chesapeake/CBF pilot partner — the labeled EIS dataset for classifier recall validation requires the partner to contribute institutional knowledge; this is a contract term, not an assumption

Onboarding script for the local-long-tail mismatch conversation — the watershed group director will ask about local floodplain rezoning in the first demo; the product team needs a practiced, honest answer that sets expectations without killing the pilot

Engineering scope estimate for the standalone path minimum reconciliation — 'fetch FR and Regs.gov, compare dates, flag disagreement' should be a story-pointed backlog item before the standalone contingency is relied on
